records.content:
Estas cinco preguntas se ajustan al estilo de los exámenes del curso: cada una te pide explicar,
evaluar o interpretar en oraciones completas, no solo calcular un número. Escribe tu propia respuesta
antes de abrir la respuesta modelo. Un número correcto sin razonamiento ganaría poco crédito en el
examen real, y una explicación clara con un pequeño desliz aritmético ganaría la mayor parte.

PE 5.1 (explica por qué). El capítulo llama a la prueba t clásica para la pendiente del punting
“sobre hielo delgado” con n = 13, y sin embargo corre la prueba de permutación sobre esos mismos
trece pateadores sin disculparse. Explica, en oraciones completas, exactamente cuál supuesto necesita
la prueba t que la de permutación no, y por qué tener solo trece pateadores hace que esa diferencia
importe aquí en lugar de ser un tecnicismo.

Respuesta modelo

La prueba t clásica trata la razón t^{\ast} = b_1 / s\{b_1\} como exactamente t-distribuida con
n - 2 grados de libertad, y esa distribución es exacta solo cuando los errores son normales. Cuando
los errores no son normales, el resultado t vale solo como aproximación, y solo cuando la muestra es
lo bastante grande para que un efecto de límite central suavice la estimación de la pendiente hacia la
normalidad. Con trece pateadores no hay rescate de muestra grande, y trece residuos no pueden distinguir
una distribución normal de una no normal (

Figure 2), así que la única premisa sobre
la que se apoya la prueba t es precisamente la que no se puede verificar. Eso es lo que significa
“hielo delgado”: no que la prueba se sepa incorrecta, sino que su red de seguridad es no verificable.

La prueba de permutación no necesita ningún supuesto distribucional en absoluto. Bajo la nula de no
asociación, las trece distancias se adjuntan a las trece fuerzas sin tenerlas en cuenta, así que la
pendiente observada es literalmente uno de los 13! emparejamientos igualmente probables, y remezclar
genera esa distribución de referencia exactamente a partir de los datos en mano. La normalidad nunca
entra al argumento, así que la muestra pequeña que socava la prueba t no socava la de permutación. El
único precio que la prueba de permutación paga por un n pequeño es un conjunto de referencia más
grueso (menos emparejamientos genuinamente distintos), no un supuesto roto.

Una respuesta débil solo dice “las pruebas de permutación no suponen normalidad” sin explicar que el
requisito de normalidad de la prueba t es exactamente el no verificable con n = 13, y sin notar que
la distribución de referencia de permutación proviene de los emparejamientos igualmente probables bajo
la nula.

PE 5.2 (interpreta una salida y una figura en contexto). Una prueba física distinta, el tiempo de
suspensión (los segundos que el balón permanece en el aire), se usa para predecir la distancia del punt
con el modelo Distance ~ Hang. El ajuste clásico y una prueba de permutación con B = 5000 (semilla
4210) dan:Distance ~ Hang
             Estimate Std. Error  t value Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept) -22.32579   36.30608 -0.61493  0.55111
Hang         43.50138    9.19420  4.73139  0.00062

permutation test: 2 of 5000 reshuffles as extreme,  p_perm = (2+1)/(5000+1) = 0.0006



Figure 12:La distribución nula de permutación de la pendiente del tiempo de suspensión. Las pendientes remezcladas se agrupan alrededor de cero; la pendiente observada 43.5 queda fuera del borde derecho, superada por solo dos de 5000 remezclas, así que el valor p de permutación de dos colas es de alrededor de 0.0006.

Interpreta esta salida y figura en contexto. Enuncia qué significa la pendiente 43.5 en las unidades
del problema, qué dicen los dos valores p y si concuerdan, y da una oración de precaución sobre lo que
este análisis no establece.

Respuesta modelo

La pendiente dice que entre estos trece pateadores, cada segundo adicional de tiempo de suspensión va
con alrededor de 43.5 pies más de distancia promedio del punt. El intercepto -22.3 pies no es
interpretable por sí solo, porque un tiempo de suspensión de cero está muy fuera del rango de los datos
y significaría que el balón nunca salió del pie. Ambos valores p son diminutos y esencialmente
idénticos: la prueba t clásica da 0.00062 y la prueba de permutación da
(2 + 1)/(5000 + 1) \approx 0.0006. La figura muestra por qué concuerdan, porque la pendiente observada
43.5 queda fuera del borde derecho lejano de la nula remezclada, superada por solo dos de cinco mil
mezclas. Así que una pendiente tan pronunciada casi nunca surge cuando el tiempo de suspensión y la
distancia no están relacionados, y tenemos evidencia fuerte de que los dos están asociados, una
conclusión que no se apoya en errores normales porque la prueba de permutación libre de supuestos
aterriza en el mismo lugar que la prueba t.

La precaución: esta es una asociación observacional medida dentro de trece pateadores, no un efecto
causal. No muestra que un pateador podría alargar una patada manteniendo deliberadamente el balón más
tiempo en el aire, porque el tiempo de suspensión y la distancia son ambos consecuencia de la misma
fuerza de pierna y técnica, y con solo trece puntos la estimación 43.5 es ella misma imprecisa.

Una respuesta débil lee el valor p como “significativo” pero no interpreta la pendiente en las unidades
del problema, no nota que los dos métodos concuerdan y por qué esa concordancia importa, o se desliza
hacia una afirmación causal.

PE 5.3 (un estudiante afirma algo, evalúalo). Un estudiante corre la prueba de permutación del
punting (Distance ~ RStr, B = 5000) dos veces, una con set.seed(4210) y otra con
set.seed(2026), y obtiene:seed 4210:  M = 4 extreme reshuffles,  p_perm = 0.00100
seed 2026:  M = 8 extreme reshuffles,  p_perm = 0.00180

El estudiante concluye: “La prueba de permutación no es confiable, porque da una respuesta distinta cada
vez que la corro. Debería simplemente reportar el valor p de la prueba t clásica de 0.00127, que es
exacto y reproducible.” Evalúa el razonamiento del estudiante. Di qué está bien y qué está mal en cada
mitad de la afirmación.

Respuesta modelo

El estudiante tiene mitad de razón y mitad no. Es cierto que un valor p de permutación de Monte Carlo
tambalea de corrida a corrida, porque estima el verdadero valor p de permutación a partir de una
muestra aleatoria finita de B remezclas. Los valores 0.00100 y 0.00180 son dos de esas
estimaciones, y la brecha entre ellas es ruido de Monte Carlo ordinario, que encoge como 1/\sqrt{B} y
casi desaparecería si el estudiante corriera muchas más remezclas o enumerara los 13! emparejamientos.
Pero “distinto cada vez” no es lo mismo que “no confiable”. Ambas corridas ubican el valor p cerca de
0.001, ambas están muy por debajo de cualquier corte usual, y ambas llevan exactamente a la misma
decisión, así que la conclusión no está en duda. La preocupación por la reproducibilidad también ya está
resuelta al fijar la semilla, cosa que el estudiante hizo.

La segunda mitad de la afirmación es donde falla el razonamiento. El clásico 0.00127 es “exacto” solo
si su supuesto de normalidad se cumple, y la razón entera por la que el capítulo usa los datos del
punting es que trece residuos no pueden confirmar la normalidad. Así que ese número no es más confiable
que el valor p de permutación, solo se ve más preciso. El reporte honesto es el valor p de permutación
(alrededor de 0.001) junto con la nota de que concuerda con la prueba t, o una corrida de permutación
con un B mayor si se quiere una cifra más precisa.

Una respuesta débil solo dice “es aleatorio, así que está bien” sin nombrar el error de Monte Carlo, la
tasa 1/\sqrt{B}, o el hecho de que ambas corridas dan la misma decisión, y no cuestiona la afirmación
falsa de que el valor p de la prueba t es exacto aquí.

PE 5.4 (un estudiante afirma algo, evalúalo). El ejemplo de RStr del capítulo encontró el error
estándar del bootstrap de casos (0.249) mayor que el del bootstrap de residuos (0.198), y explicó
que el remuestreo de casos “añade una fuente más de variabilidad”. Un estudiante lo convierte en regla:
“El remuestreo de casos siempre da un error estándar mayor que el remuestreo de residuos, porque también
deja variar los valores del predictor.” Para probar la regla reajustan la distancia del punt sobre la
fuerza general de piernas (OStr) con B = 5000 y obtienen:Distance ~ OStr
classical formula standard error   = 0.0991
case-resampling bootstrap SE       = 0.0889
residual-resampling bootstrap SE   = 0.0910

Evalúa la regla del estudiante a la luz de esta salida.

Respuesta modelo

La regla es falsa tal como se enuncia, y esta salida es un contraejemplo limpio. Aquí el error estándar
del bootstrap de casos (0.089) es en realidad menor que el del bootstrap de residuos (0.091), lo
opuesto al patrón de RStr. La explicación del capítulo describía una tendencia, no una garantía. El
remuestreo de casos y el de residuos estiman la misma variabilidad muestral subyacente de la pendiente
por dos mecanismos distintos, y cuál sale mayor en un conjunto de datos dado depende de los residuos
particulares y de los puntos de alto apalancamiento que casualmente se remuestrean, más el ruido de
Monte Carlo ordinario. Con solo trece puntos, estos accidentes fácilmente ahogan el argumento de la
“fuente extra de variación”.

La lectura sólida es que los tres errores estándar (0.089, 0.091 y 0.099) concuerdan todos dentro
de alrededor de un diez por ciento y cuentan la misma historia sobre una pendiente de 0.43, así que la
elección entre los dos bootstraps debe hacerse por la historia de recolección de datos (remuestreo de
casos cuando los pateadores son una muestra al azar de una población, remuestreo de residuos cuando las
fuerzas se tratan como fijas por diseño), no por cuál produce el número más grande.

Una respuesta débil simplemente declara la regla correcta o incorrecta sin usar los números impresos
para mostrar que el de casos es menor que el de residuos aquí, o sin explicar que el efecto de
“variación extra” es solo una tendencia que el ruido de muestra pequeña puede invertir.

PE 5.5 (qué cambiaría si). Para la pendiente del punting Distance ~ RStr (observada
b_1 = 0.902), responde cada parte en oraciones completas.

(a) El capítulo usa B = 5000 remezclas y reporta p_{\text{perm}} \approx 0.001. ¿Qué cambiaría
sobre el número que podrías reportar honestamente si volvieras a correr la prueba de permutación con
solo B = 500 remezclas y de nuevo no encontraras ninguna remezcla que superara la pendiente
observada?

(b) El capítulo remuestrea por casos a los pateadores porque se tratan como una muestra de una
población mayor de pateadores. ¿Qué cambiaría sobre cuál bootstrap es la elección honesta si en cambio
estos trece fueran el grupo entero que te importa, digamos la plantilla completa de un equipo, y solo
quisieras describir la recta dentro de ese grupo fijo?

Respuesta modelo

(a) Con B = 500 y M = 0, el valor p de permutación sería (0 + 1)/(500 + 1) = 1/501 \approx 0.002,
así que el número más pequeño que podrías reportar honestamente aproximadamente se duplica respecto al
piso de B = 5000 de 1/5001 \approx 0.0002. Menos remezclas suben el piso de resolución 1/(B + 1),
así que pierdes la capacidad de resolver valores p muy pequeños y tu estimación es más ruidosa de corrida
a corrida, aunque la conclusión cualitativa (evidencia fuerte, p muy por debajo de 0.05) no cambia.
Para reportar algo más pequeño tendrías que correr más remezclas.

(b) Si los trece pateadores son el grupo entero y solo quieres la recta dentro de ese conjunto fijo, el
argumento a favor del remuestreo de casos se debilita. El remuestreo de casos imita la aleatoriedad de
extraer sujetos de una población, y no queda tal aleatoriedad una vez que ya tienes a todos. La
variabilidad que vale la pena modelar es entonces la dispersión de las distancias alrededor de la recta,
que es exactamente lo que captura el remuestreo de residuos al mantener fijas las fuerzas y reasignar
residuos remuestreados, así que el remuestreo de residuos (o la visión clásica de diseño fijo) se vuelve
la elección más honesta. En el extremo donde el ajuste se trata como una descripción pura de un grupo
finito fijo, la inferencia sobre una población más amplia posiblemente ni siquiera sea la pregunta
correcta que plantear.

Una respuesta débil a (a) omite que es el piso 1/(B + 1) lo que cambia (alrededor de 0.002 frente a
0.0002) en lugar de la decisión; una respuesta débil a (b) solo repite “usa el remuestreo de casos”
sin reconocer que quitar la aleatoriedad del muestreo de sujetos quita la razón misma para preferirlo.

records.content:
Juega el juego del Capítulo 5

Juega el juego del Capítulo 5 en tu teléfono o computadora portátil: 10 rondas rápidas, sin preparación.


Abrir el juego. Ejercita las jugadas centrales del capítulo: ordenar el ciclo de
permutación, calcular el valor p (M+1)/(B+1), ajustar la recta real del punting, emparejar cada método
de remuestreo con su función, y reflejar un intervalo de percentiles en uno básico, con un párrafo de
razón después de cada ronda.

Chapter summary (in English)

This chapter presents resampling inference for a regression slope, useful when the sample is small
and the normal-error assumption cannot be trusted. The motivating example is the punting data:
thirteen punters, with punt distance against right-leg strength. The Chapter 3 t test rests on
assumptions that cannot be checked, because thirteen residuals do not distinguish normal from
non-normal.

The permutation test answers whether an association exists. The response is shuffled against the
fixed predictor many times, breaking any link, and the slope is computed on each shuffle. The
permutation p-value is p = (M+1)/(B+1), where M counts shuffles with a slope as extreme as the
observed one. For the punters, with B = 5000, the result was p \approx 0.001, close to the t
test’s 0.00127. For Toluca, no shuffle reached the real slope 3.5702, so p < 1/5001. The
plus-one correction prevents ever reporting a p-value of exactly zero.

The bootstrap measures the precision of the estimate. Case resampling draws whole rows with
replacement; residual resampling holds the predictors and the fitted line fixed and reassigns
residuals with replacement. The standard deviation of the resampled slopes is the bootstrap standard
error. From it two intervals are read: the percentile interval and the basic interval, which
reflects the percentiles through 2 b_1. For the punters, both gave near (0.42, 1.39), a bit wider
than the classical t interval (0.440, 1.364).

The closing lesson: when the classical assumptions hold, the computational and classical methods agree,
which justifies trusting the t test. When they differ sharply, it is a sign that some assumption is
failing, and the bootstrap, which does not need it, is the safer report.

hierarchy.lvl1: 5. Randomization and bootstrap inference for regression
5. Randomization and bootstrap inference for regression

records.content:
Leer en espanol

A football coach wants to know what makes a punter kick far. He measures thirteen college
punters on a set of physical tests: right and left leg strength (in pounds, on a leg-press
machine), hamstring flexibility, an overall leg-strength score, and hang time. For each punter
he also records the average distance of ten punts, in feet. The question is simple to ask and
hard to answer with so few kickers: does a stronger leg really buy more distance, or could the
pattern he sees be a fluke of thirteen athletes?

Figure 1 plots punt distance against right-leg strength. The trend slopes up,
and the fitted line climbs about nine tenths of a foot for every extra pound of strength. That
looks like a real effect. But thirteen points is not many. The inference tools from Chapter 3,
the t test and its confidence interval for the slope, all rest on a chain of assumptions.
Either the errors are normal, or the sample is large enough for the normal approximation to
save us. With thirteen punters, neither is guaranteed, and there is no way to check normality
from thirteen residuals with any confidence.



Figure 1:The thirteen punters, with the least-squares line. Distance climbs with right-leg strength at about 0.9 feet per pound, but thirteen points make it fair to ask whether the slope is real or an accident of a small sample.

This chapter answers the question a different way. Instead of assuming a shape for the errors,
it uses the computer to shuffle and resample the data thousands of times and watches what
happens. Two ideas do the work. A permutation test (Definition 5.1) breaks the link between
predictor and response by shuffling, then asks how often pure chance produces a slope as steep as
the one you saw. The bootstrap (Definition 5.4) resamples the data with replacement to see how
much the slope would bounce around if you could collect the study again. Both replace a fragile assumption with a
computation you can always run. By the end you will be able to build each one from scratch, read
its output, and say when it agrees with the classical t and F tests and when it does not.

This lesson at a glance

What we are doing: Testing a regression slope and putting a confidence interval on it by shuffling and resampling the small punting dataset on the computer, instead of assuming a shape for the errors.

Why we are doing it: With only thirteen punters the normal-theory t and F machinery of Chapter 3 may not hold, and normality cannot be checked from thirteen residuals.

Main objective: Build a permutation test and a bootstrap confidence interval for the slope from scratch, and say when they agree with the classical t test and when they do not.

What changed from the last chapters: Chapter 3’s inference leaned on the normal-error assumption (

2.6 Maximum likelihood under normal errors); this chapter drops it and replaces it with computation, comparing its permutation p-value against Chapter 3’s t-test p-value on the same slope.

Learning objectives

By the end of this chapter you will be able to:

Explain why normal-theory t and F inference for a regression slope can fail, and why small samples make the risk worse.

State the no-association null hypothesis and describe the exact logic of a permutation test for the slope.

Implement the permutation test for the slope from scratch in R and Python, with no special packages, and compute a permutation p-value.

Justify the permutation p-value and the plus-one correction, and compare it against the classical t-test p-value on the same data.

Distinguish case resampling from residual resampling for the bootstrap, and code each one.

Construct and interpret percentile and basic bootstrap confidence intervals for the slope.

Decide when computational and classical inference agree, and what to report when they disagree.

records.content:
Every test from Chapter 3 came with fine print. Its formulas give exact answers only when the
errors follow a normal bell curve, or when the sample is large enough to stand in for that. With
thirteen punters we have neither, and this section shows that we cannot even check the fine print.
Here is that fine print written out in symbols.

Chapter 3 built a confidence interval for the slope as b_1 \pm t^{\ast} \, s\{b_1\} and tested
H_0: \beta_1 = 0 with the ratio t^{\ast} = b_1 / s\{b_1\}, comparing it to a t distribution
with n - 2 degrees of freedom. That machinery is exact only when the errors
\varepsilon_i are normal. When they are not normal, the t ratio is still approximately
t-distributed if the sample is large, because a version of the central limit theorem smooths
the estimate toward normality. The catch is the word large. With thirteen punters there is no
large-sample rescue, and there is no way to confirm the errors are normal.

You might think you could just check: fit the line, plot the thirteen residuals on a normal
quantile plot, and see whether they fall on a straight line. The problem is that thirteen points
are too few to tell normal from non-normal. 

Figure 2 makes this concrete. The
top-left panel is the real punting residuals; the other five are genuinely normal samples of the
same size, drawn by the computer. Every one of them bends and wiggles away from the straight
line. If known-normal data looks this crooked at n = 13, then a crooked plot from real data
tells you almost nothing. The normality assumption is simply uncheckable here.



Figure 2:At n = 13 even data that is genuinely normal (the five blue panels) produces a bent, wandering quantile plot. So the crooked plot of the real residuals (top left) is no evidence against normality: thirteen points cannot tell the two apart. This is why we want inference that does not lean on a normality check.

The way out is to stop assuming a distribution and start generating one. If we can build the
distribution of the slope under some clearly stated chance mechanism, using only the data in
hand, then we can measure how surprising the observed slope is without ever writing down a normal
density. That is what the rest of the chapter does.

Try it 5.1

A classmate fits a regression on n = 15 observations, looks at the normal quantile plot of the
residuals, sees a slight bend, and declares “the normality assumption is violated, so the
t-test p-value is worthless.” What is wrong with both halves of that statement?

Solution

First, a slight bend in a quantile plot of only 15 residuals is expected even when the errors are
perfectly normal, as 

Figure 2 shows, so a mild bend is not evidence of a
violation. The classmate is reading noise as signal. Second, even if the errors were genuinely
non-normal, the t-test p-value is not automatically “worthless”; it may still be a decent
approximation, and the honest response is to check it against a method that does not need
normality, such as a permutation test, rather than to throw it out by decree. The point of this
chapter is to replace the argument about normality with a computation.

records.content:
Here is the question a hypothesis test asks, in plain words: if leg strength had nothing to do
with punt distance, how often would random chance hand us a slope as steep as 0.902? To answer
it we need to see slopes produced by “nothing going on.” We can manufacture them. Keep the
thirteen strength values exactly where they are, take the thirteen distances, and shuffle them so
that each distance is reassigned to a random punter. Shuffling destroys any real link between
strength and distance while keeping both columns of numbers intact. Fit a line to the shuffled
data and record its slope. That slope is a sample of “what a slope looks like when there is no
association.”

Figure 3 shows one shuffle. On the left, the real data slopes up. On the right, the
same distances reattached to random strengths give a nearly flat line. Do the shuffle thousands
of times and you get thousands of no-association slopes: their histogram is the null distribution
of the slope, built entirely from your own data with no formula and no normality assumption.



Figure 3:Shuffling the distances against the fixed strengths breaks the link between them, so a reshuffled fit has a slope near zero. Repeating the shuffle thousands of times shows what slopes chance alone can produce.

hierarchy.lvl1: 5. Randomization and bootstrap inference for regression
hierarchy.lvl2: 5.2 The permutation test for the slope

hierarchy.lvl1: 5. Randomization and bootstrap inference for regression
hierarchy.lvl2: 5.2 The permutation test for the slope

records.content:
Definition 5.1: Permutation test

A permutation test for a regression slope builds the null distribution of the slope by
repeatedly reshuffling the response values Y_i against the fixed predictor values X_i (a
sample without replacement), which breaks any real association, and then compares the observed
slope to the reshuffled ones. It tests H_0: X and Y are unrelated (\beta_1 = 0) against
the alternative that they are related.

The permutation test for the slope works with these pieces. Let the data be pairs
(X_i, Y_i) for i = 1, \dots, n, and let the observed least-squares slope beb_1 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^n (X_i - \bar X)(Y_i - \bar Y)}{\sum_{i=1}^n (X_i - \bar X)^2}
= \frac{S_{xy}}{S_{xx}} .

In words: the slope is the cross-product of X and Y deviations divided by the spread of X,
exactly as in 

2.2 Least squares from first principles. The null hypothesis isH_0 : X \text{ and } Y \text{ are unrelated} \quad(\text{in particular } \beta_1 = 0),

and the alternative is that they are related (\beta_1 \neq 0). A permutation is a
reshuffling \pi of the response values that pairs Y_{\pi(i)} with X_i. For each permutation
compute the reshuffled slope b_1^{\pi} = \sum (X_i - \bar X)(Y_{\pi(i)} - \bar Y) / S_{xx}. The
two-sided permutation p-value is the fraction of permutations whose slope is at least as far
from zero as the observed one:p_{\text{perm}} = \frac{\#\{\pi : |b_1^{\pi}| \ge |b_1|\} + 1}{(\text{number of permutations}) + 1} .

Definition 5.2: Permutation p-value

The two-sided permutation p-value is the fraction of permutations whose reshuffled slope is at
least as far from zero as the observed slope, with a plus one added to both the count and the
total for the observed data itself:p_{\text{perm}} = \frac{M + 1}{B + 1}, \qquad M = \#\{\pi : |b_1^{\pi}| \ge |b_1|\},

where B is the number of permutations drawn. The plus one keeps p_{\text{perm}} \ge 1/(B+1),
so it can never be exactly zero.

In words: the p-value is how often a reshuffle beats or ties the real slope in size, plus one for
the real data itself, divided by the number of tries plus one. There are n! possible
permutations, far too many to list (13! is over six billion), so we draw B of them at random
and use those. The extra +1 on top and bottom counts the observed arrangement as one of the
possibilities, which we explain and justify next.

hierarchy.lvl2: 5.2 The permutation test for the slope

records.content:
Theorem 5.3: Validity of the permutation p-value

Under the no-association null H_0, in which the response values are attached to the predictor
values without regard to them, the observed slope is one draw from the permutation distribution
that places probability 1/n! on each of the n! equally likely pairings. The permutation
p-value p_{\text{perm}} = (M+1)/(B+1) is then a valid p-value: it never rejects a true null more
often than its stated level, and its smallest attainable value is the floor 1/(B+1).

Proof. Suppose H_0 holds in the strong
sense that the response values were attached to the predictor values without any regard to them.
Then the particular pairing we observed is just one of the n! equally likely ways to match the
fixed collection of Y values to the fixed X values. Any statistic computed on a random pairing,
here the slope b_1^{\pi}, therefore has a completely known distribution under H_0: it places
probability 1/n! on each of the n! arrangements. This is the permutation distribution, and
it needs no assumption about the shape of the errors. Normality never appears.

Our observed slope b_1 is the value from one particular arrangement, so under H_0 it is one
draw from that permutation distribution. The exact two-sided p-value is the null probability of a
slope at least as extreme in size,p_{\text{exact}} = \frac{\#\{\pi : |b_1^{\pi}| \ge |b_1|\}}{n!} ,

which counts arrangements, including the observed one (it satisfies |b_1^{\pi}| \ge |b_1| with
equality). When n! is too large to enumerate, we estimate p_{\text{exact}} by drawing B
random permutations. Writing M for the number of those B draws with |b_1^{\pi}| \ge |b_1|,
the estimatorp_{\text{perm}} = \frac{M + 1}{B + 1}

adds the observed arrangement to both the count and the total. This +1 is not a fudge. It keeps
the test honest: the observed data is itself a valid arrangement under H_0, so it belongs in the
reference set, and including it guarantees p_{\text{perm}} can never be exactly zero. A test that
could report p = 0 would reject H_0 with certainty on finite evidence, which is never
justified. With the +1, the smallest possible p-value is 1/(B+1), and one can show the
resulting test never rejects a true null more often than its stated level. \blacksquare

One tidy consequence: because S_{xx} and the X_i do not change when we shuffle Y, the
reshuffled slope b_1^{\pi} is just S_{xy}^{\pi}/S_{xx}, a fixed positive constant times the
reshuffled cross-product S_{xy}^{\pi}. So permuting the slope is the same test as permuting the
sample correlation, or permuting S_{xy} itself. Whatever monotone version of “association” you
prefer, the permutation test gives the same p-value.

records.content:
The permutation test is a short loop. Read the data, write a one-line slope function, compute the
observed slope, then shuffle and refit B times. No package beyond base R is needed.punting <- read.csv("data/punting.csv")
dim(punting)
fit <- lm(Distance ~ RStr, data = punting)
round(summary(fit)$coefficients, 5)[1] 13  7
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept) 15.00896   31.35922 0.47861  0.64159
RStr         0.90204    0.21003 4.29474  0.00127

The classical fit gives a slope of 0.902 feet per pound with a t-test p-value of 0.00127.
Hold on to that number; the permutation test will produce its own p-value to compare against it.
Now the from-scratch pieces.slope <- function(x, y) {
  sum((x - mean(x)) * (y - mean(y))) / sum((x - mean(x))^2)
}
x <- punting$RStr
y <- punting$Distance
b1_obs <- slope(x, y)
b1_obs[1] 0.9020383set.seed(4210)
B <- 5000
perm_slopes <- numeric(B)
for (i in 1:B) {
  y_shuffled <- sample(y)              # shuffle distances, keep strengths fixed
  perm_slopes[i] <- slope(x, y_shuffled)
}
count_extreme <- sum(abs(perm_slopes) >= abs(b1_obs))
p_perm <- (count_extreme + 1) / (B + 1)
c(count_extreme = count_extreme, p_permutation = p_perm)count_extreme p_permutation 
    4.0000000     0.0009998 

Out of 5000 reshuffles, only 4 produced a slope as steep as the real one, so the permutation
p-value is (4+1)/(5000+1) \approx 0.001.

records.content:
The same loop in Python with NumPy. rng.permutation does the shuffling; everything else is a
handful of array operations.import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf

punting = pd.read_csv("data/punting.csv")
print(punting.shape)
fit = smf.ols("Distance ~ RStr", data=punting).fit()
print(fit.summary().tables[1])(13, 7)
==============================================================================
                 coef    std err          t      P>|t|      [0.025      0.975]
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Intercept     15.0090     31.359      0.479      0.642     -54.012      84.030
RStr           0.9020      0.210      4.295      0.001       0.440       1.364
==============================================================================def slope(x, y):
    x = np.asarray(x, float)
    y = np.asarray(y, float)
    return np.sum((x - x.mean()) * (y - y.mean())) / np.sum((x - x.mean()) ** 2)

x = punting["RStr"].to_numpy()
y = punting["Distance"].to_numpy()
b1_obs = slope(x, y)
print(b1_obs)0.9020382716049381rng = np.random.default_rng(4210)
B = 5000
perm_slopes = np.empty(B)
for i in range(B):
    y_shuffled = rng.permutation(y)       # shuffle distances, keep strengths fixed
    perm_slopes[i] = slope(x, y_shuffled)
count_extreme = int(np.sum(np.abs(perm_slopes) >= abs(b1_obs)))
p_perm = (count_extreme + 1) / (B + 1)
print("count_extreme =", count_extreme, " p_permutation =", p_perm)count_extreme = 5  p_permutation = 0.0011997600479904018

R and Python use different random number generators, so they draw different shuffles: R found 4
extreme slopes and Python found 5, giving permutation p-values of about 0.0010 and 0.0012.
The two disagree only in the third decimal place, which is Monte Carlo noise, and both point to
the same clear conclusion.

Example 5.1: The punting slope by permutation

Question. Is the upward slope of punt distance on right-leg strength stronger than chance
would produce, without assuming normal errors?

Intuition. Shuffle the distances against the fixed strengths 5000 times, collect the
no-association slopes, and see where the real slope 0.902 falls among them.

Formula. p_{\text{perm}} = (M + 1)/(B + 1), where M counts reshuffles with
|b_1^{\pi}| \ge |b_1|.

Computation. The R and Python loops above give M = 4 (R) and M = 5 (Python) out of
B = 5000, so p_{\text{perm}} \approx 0.001. 

Figure 4 shows the null
distribution with the observed slope marked far out in the tail.



Figure 4:The permutation null distribution of the punting slope. Reshuffled slopes cluster around zero; the observed slope 0.90 (green) sits in the far right tail, with only four of 5000 reshuffles as extreme, so the two-sided permutation p-value is about 0.001.

Interpretation. A slope as steep as 0.902 almost never happens when strength and distance
are unrelated: about one reshuffle in a thousand. So the data give strong evidence that leg
strength really does predict punt distance. The permutation p-value 0.001 agrees closely with
the classical t-test p-value 0.00127, which is reassuring: here the small sample did not
break the t test, and now we know that without having to trust normality.

Durable skill: Replace an untestable assumption with a computation

The permutation test trades an assumption you cannot check (normal errors from thirteen points)
for a mechanism you can state exactly (shuffle the labels) and run on a computer. This move,
turning “I hope the assumptions hold” into “I will simulate the null and look,” is one of the most
portable ideas in modern statistics. Whenever a formula-based test rests on a premise you cannot
verify, ask whether you can instead generate the null distribution directly by resampling or
shuffling. To practice, take any two-group comparison you meet and build its permutation test in
ten lines before reaching for a named procedure.

Try it 5.2

In the permutation loop, why do we shuffle only the response y and leave x untouched? Would
shuffling x instead, or shuffling both, change the test?

Solution

The null hypothesis is that the pairing between X and Y carries no information, so we destroy
the pairing by randomly rematching the two columns. It does not matter which column you shuffle:
shuffling Y against fixed X, shuffling X against fixed Y, or shuffling both, all produce
the same set of random pairings and the same permutation distribution of the slope, because the
slope depends only on how the X and Y values are matched up. We shuffle Y by convention.
What you must not do is shuffle the two columns together (a paired shuffle), because that keeps
every original pair intact and tests nothing.

records.content:
The punting example ended in a near-tie between the permutation and t-test p-values. To see the
two methods on a case where the signal is overwhelming, return to the Toluca Company data from
Chapters 2 and 3: the labor hours to produce a part against the lot size, with the least-squares
slope b_1 = 3.5702 hours per unit (

2.2 Least squares from first principles). Chapter 3 tested that slope with a t
test and found a p-value of about 4.45 \times 10^{-10} (

3.7 The F test and its equivalence to the t test, where the t and F
tests coincide for a single slope). We now run the permutation test on the same slope and compare.toluca <- read.csv("data/toluca.csv")
xt <- toluca$lotsize
yt <- toluca$hours
b1_toluca <- slope(xt, yt)
p_t <- summary(lm(hours ~ lotsize, data = toluca))$coefficients["lotsize", "Pr(>|t|)"]

set.seed(4210)
perm_toluca <- numeric(B)
for (i in 1:B) perm_toluca[i] <- slope(xt, sample(yt))
c(b1_toluca      = b1_toluca,
  perm_count     = sum(abs(perm_toluca) >= abs(b1_toluca)),
  max_perm_slope = max(abs(perm_toluca)),
  t_test_p_value = p_t)     b1_toluca     perm_count max_perm_slope t_test_p_value 
  3.570202e+00   0.000000e+00   2.767172e+00   4.448828e-10 toluca = pd.read_csv("data/toluca.csv")
xt = toluca["lotsize"].to_numpy()
yt = toluca["hours"].to_numpy()
b1_toluca = slope(xt, yt)
p_t = smf.ols("hours ~ lotsize", data=toluca).fit().pvalues["lotsize"]

rng = np.random.default_rng(4210)
perm_toluca = np.array([slope(xt, rng.permutation(yt)) for _ in range(B)])
print("b1_toluca      =", b1_toluca)
print("perm_count     =", int(np.sum(np.abs(perm_toluca) >= abs(b1_toluca))))
print("max_perm_slope =", np.max(np.abs(perm_toluca)))
print("t_test_p_value =", p_t)b1_toluca      = 3.57020202020202
perm_count     = 0
max_perm_slope = 2.7454545454545456
t_test_p_value = 4.4488275871889375e-10

Example 5.2: The Toluca slope by permutation

Question. How does the permutation test judge the Toluca slope, and can it match the t
test’s tiny p-value?

Intuition. Shuffle the hours against the fixed lot sizes and see whether any reshuffle comes
near the real slope 3.5702.

Formula. p_{\text{perm}} = (M + 1)/(B + 1), as before.

Computation. In 5000 reshuffles the largest slope that chance produced was about 2.77 in
size (R) or 2.75 (Python), nowhere near the observed 3.5702. So M = 0 andp_{\text{perm}} = \frac{0 + 1}{5000 + 1} = \frac{1}{5001} \approx 0.0002 .

Figure 5 shows the observed slope sitting off the right edge of the whole
permutation histogram.



Figure 5:The Toluca permutation null distribution. The real slope 3.57 is larger than every one of 5000 reshuffled slopes, so the permutation p-value is smaller than 1 in 5001, roughly 0.0002.

Interpretation. Both methods say the same thing: the Toluca slope is real beyond any
reasonable doubt. But notice a limit of the permutation test. With 5000 reshuffles, the smallest
p-value it can report is 1/5001 \approx 0.0002. It cannot produce the t test’s
4.45 \times 10^{-10}, not because the two disagree, but because a Monte Carlo permutation test
has a floor set by how many reshuffles you run. When the evidence is this strong, the permutation
test says “smaller than I can measure,” and the t test, if you trust its assumptions, puts a
number on just how small.

The floor 1/(B+1) raises a fair question: how many reshuffles is enough? Because
p_{\text{perm}} is a Monte Carlo estimate, it wobbles from run to run when B is small and
settles as B grows. 

Figure 6 tracks the running punting p-value against the
number of reshuffles used, for three different random seeds. Past a few thousand reshuffles the
three lines have nearly stopped moving, which is why this chapter uses B = 5000. If you needed
to resolve a p-value near a sharp cutoff, you would run more.



Figure 6:The running punting permutation p-value for three random seeds. Each estimate is noisy for the first few hundred reshuffles and settles near 0.001 by a few thousand, the reason B = 5000 is a reasonable default.

Try it 5.3

You run a permutation test with B = 999 reshuffles and none of them beats your observed
statistic. What p-value do you report, and what would you have to do to report something smaller?

Solution

With M = 0 out of B = 999, the permutation p-value is (0 + 1)/(999 + 1) = 1/1000 = 0.001.
You cannot honestly report anything smaller from this run, because 1/(B+1) is the floor. To
resolve a smaller p-value you must run more reshuffles (raise B), which lowers the floor, or
switch to a method like the t test that puts an analytic number on the far tail, accepting its
assumptions in exchange.

hierarchy.lvl1: 5. Randomization and bootstrap inference for regression
5. Randomization and bootstrap inference for regression
5. Randomization and bootstrap inference for regression
5. Randomization and bootstrap inference for regression

records.content:
A permutation test answers “is there an effect?” It does not tell you how big the effect is or how
precise your estimate of it is. For that we want the sampling distribution of b_1: the spread of
slopes you would see if you could repeat the whole study many times. We cannot repeat the study.
The bootstrap fakes the repetition using the one sample we have, by resampling it with
replacement. The idea, due to Bradley Efron, is that the sample is our best picture of the
population, so drawing new samples from the sample imitates drawing new samples from the
population.

Definition 5.4: Bootstrap

The bootstrap estimates the sampling variability of a statistic by resampling the observed
data with replacement many times, recomputing the statistic on each resample, and treating the
spread of those values as an estimate of how much the statistic would vary across repeated
studies. It rests on the principle that the sample is the best available stand-in for the
population.

Key idea

A permutation test and a bootstrap answer different questions. Permutation shuffling breaks the
link between predictor and response to ask whether any effect exists at all, and it returns a
p-value. Bootstrap resampling imitates repeated studies to ask how precise the effect estimate is,
and it returns a standard error or confidence interval. Reach for the first to detect an effect,
the second to measure one.

There are two natural ways to resample a regression, and they answer slightly different questions.


Figure 7 shows both. Case resampling draws whole rows (X_i, Y_i) with
replacement, so a given punter may appear twice or not at all, and the set of strength values
changes from resample to resample. This treats the punters as a random sample from a population of
punters. Residual resampling keeps the strengths and the fitted line fixed, then builds a new
response by adding reshuffled-with-replacement residuals back onto the fitted values. This treats
the strength values as fixed by design and puts all the randomness in the errors, matching the
regression model of Chapter 2 more literally.

Definition 5.5: Case resampling and residual resampling

Case resampling draws whole rows (X_i, Y_i) with replacement, so the set of predictor values
varies from resample to resample; it fits data in which the subjects were randomly sampled.
Residual resampling holds the predictors X_i and the fitted line \hat Y_i = b_0 + b_1 X_i
fixed and rebuilds the response as Y_i^{\ast} = \hat Y_i + e_{s(i)}^{\ast}, where each
e_{s(i)}^{\ast} is a residual drawn with replacement from e_1, \dots, e_n; it fits data in
which the predictor values are fixed by design.



Figure 7:The two bootstraps for regression. Case resampling (left) redraws whole rows, so which x-values appear changes each time. Residual resampling (right) keeps the x-values and the fitted line fixed and reattaches reshuffled residuals, putting all the randomness in the errors.

hierarchy.lvl1: 5. Randomization and bootstrap inference for regression
5. Randomization and bootstrap inference for regression

records.content:
Both bootstraps repeat a resample-and-refit loop B times and collect the slopes. The
case-resampling algorithm is:\text{for } b = 1, \dots, B: \quad
\text{draw } (X_i^{\ast}, Y_i^{\ast}) \text{ by sampling } n \text{ rows with replacement, then } b_1^{\ast b} = \frac{S_{xy}^{\ast}}{S_{xx}^{\ast}} .

In words: rebuild a dataset by drawing n rows at random with repeats allowed, and refit. The
residual-resampling algorithm fixes X_i and the observed fit \hat Y_i = b_0 + b_1 X_i with
residuals e_i = Y_i - \hat Y_i:\text{for } b = 1, \dots, B: \quad
Y_i^{\ast} = \hat Y_i + e_{s(i)}^{\ast}, \quad
b_1^{\ast b} = \frac{\sum (X_i - \bar X)(Y_i^{\ast} - \bar Y^{\ast})}{S_{xx}} ,

where e_{s(i)}^{\ast} is a residual drawn at random with replacement from e_1, \dots, e_n. In
words: keep the line and the strengths, then jiggle each fitted point up or down by a residual
borrowed from somewhere in the data. The bootstrap standard error of the slope is the standard
deviation of the collected slopes,s_{\text{boot}}\{b_1\} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{B-1}\sum_{b=1}^{B}\left(b_1^{\ast b} - \bar b_1^{\ast}\right)^2} ,

the ordinary spread of the B resampled slopes. It estimates how much b_1 would vary across
repeated studies.

Definition 5.6: Bootstrap standard error

The bootstrap standard error of a statistic is the standard deviation of its values across the
B bootstrap resamples, s_{\text{boot}}\{b_1\} = \operatorname{sd}(b_1^{\ast 1}, \dots, b_1^{\ast B}).
It estimates the true sampling standard deviation of the statistic without any distributional
formula.

records.content:
Case resampling is a loop that resamples row indices. We reuse the slope function from before.set.seed(4210)
n <- nrow(punting)
boot_case <- numeric(B)
for (i in 1:B) {
  idx <- sample(n, n, replace = TRUE)  # resample whole rows
  boot_case[i] <- slope(x[idx], y[idx])
}
c(boot_mean = mean(boot_case), boot_SE = sd(boot_case))boot_mean   boot_SE 
0.9045003 0.2485204 

Residual resampling keeps x fixed and rebuilds y from the fitted values plus resampled
residuals.b0_obs <- mean(y) - b1_obs * mean(x)
fitted_vals <- b0_obs + b1_obs * x
resid_obs <- y - fitted_vals

set.seed(4210)
boot_resid <- numeric(B)
for (i in 1:B) {
  y_star <- fitted_vals + sample(resid_obs, n, replace = TRUE)
  boot_resid[i] <- slope(x, y_star)
}
c(boot_mean = mean(boot_resid), boot_SE = sd(boot_resid))boot_mean   boot_SE 
0.9020770 0.1976297

    boot_case[i] = slope(x[idx], y[idx])
print("boot_mean =", boot_case.mean(), " boot_SE =", boot_case.std(ddof=1))boot_mean = 0.9099216280489136  boot_SE = 0.24681912512413196b0_obs = y.mean() - b1_obs * x.mean()

Example 5.3: Two bootstrap standard errors for the punting slope

Question. How precise is the punting slope 0.902, and does the answer depend on which
bootstrap we use?

Intuition. Resample 5000 times each way, collect the slopes, and take their standard
deviation.

Formula. s_{\text{boot}}\{b_1\} = \operatorname{sd}(b_1^{\ast 1}, \dots, b_1^{\ast B}).

Computation. Case resampling gives a bootstrap standard error of about 0.249 (R) or 0.247
(Python); residual resampling gives about 0.198 (R) or 0.195 (Python). The classical formula
standard error from the fit is 0.210. 

Figure 8 draws both bootstrap
distributions with the normal-theory curve overlaid.



Figure 8:Bootstrap slope distributions for the punting data. Case resampling (left) is wider than the normal-theory curve because it also lets the set of strength values vary; residual resampling (right) holds the design fixed and tracks the normal-theory spread closely.

Interpretation. The three standard errors, 0.249, 0.210, and 0.198, tell a consistent
story about a slope of 0.902: the effect is several standard errors from zero no matter how you
measure the spread. The case bootstrap is the widest because it adds one more source of
variability, the changing set of strength values, that the other two hold fixed. When the punters
are a sample from some larger population and you care about that population, case resampling is the
honest choice, and its slightly larger standard error is a feature, not a flaw. When you regard
the strength values as fixed by the study design, residual resampling matches the classical model
and gives a standard error close to the formula’s.

Durable skill: Get a standard error by simulating the study

A standard error is just the spread of an estimate across repeated studies you did not get to run.
The bootstrap runs them for you by resampling the one dataset you have. Once you can write a
resample-and-refit loop, you can attach a standard error to almost any statistic, even ones with
no textbook formula: a median, a ratio, a difference of correlations, the output of a whole
model-fitting pipeline. The skill is recognizing that “how much would this bounce if I did it
again?” is a question you can answer by simulation whenever you cannot answer it by algebra.

Try it 5.4

In the residual bootstrap, we add resampled residuals back onto the fitted values from the
original fit, and we never refit the intercept and slope before resampling. Explain why the
residual bootstrap holds the strength values x fixed across all B resamples, while the case
bootstrap does not.

Solution

The residual bootstrap builds each new response as Y_i^{\ast} = \hat Y_i + e^{\ast}_{s(i)} using
the same X_i every time, so the predictor column never changes; only the errors are redrawn.
That mirrors the Chapter 2 model, where the X_i are fixed constants and all randomness is in the
errors. The case bootstrap instead draws whole rows (X_i, Y_i) with replacement, so the set of
strength values that appears is different in every resample. That mirrors a study where the
punters themselves were sampled at random. The two match different data-collection stories, which
is why their standard errors differ.

records.content:
A standard error is a single number for the spread. A confidence interval is a range that should
contain the true slope \beta_1 with, say, 95\% confidence. The bootstrap distribution of the
slope already contains the information; we just have to read an interval off it. Two simple recipes
do this without assuming the distribution is normal (both are collected in Definition 5.7). The
percentile interval takes the middle 95\% of the bootstrap slopes directly. The basic
interval (also called the reflection interval) corrects for a subtle bias by flipping the
percentiles around the observed slope. When
the bootstrap distribution is symmetric they nearly coincide; when it is skewed they part ways, and
the basic interval is usually the better behaved of the two.

hierarchy.lvl1: 5. Randomization and bootstrap inference for regression
5. Randomization and bootstrap inference for regression

records.content:
Let q_{\alpha/2} and q_{1-\alpha/2} be the lower and upper percentiles of the B bootstrap
slopes, for a 100(1-\alpha)\% interval (so \alpha = 0.05 for 95\%). The two intervals are\text{percentile:} \quad \big(q_{\alpha/2}, \; q_{1-\alpha/2}\big), \qquad\qquad
\text{basic:} \quad \big(2 b_1 - q_{1-\alpha/2}, \; 2 b_1 - q_{\alpha/2}\big) .

Definition 5.7: Percentile and basic bootstrap intervals

Let q_{\alpha/2} and q_{1-\alpha/2} be the lower and upper percentiles of the B bootstrap
slopes. The 100(1-\alpha)\% percentile interval is (q_{\alpha/2},\, q_{1-\alpha/2}), read
straight off the bootstrap distribution. The basic (reflection) interval is
(2 b_1 - q_{1-\alpha/2},\, 2 b_1 - q_{\alpha/2}), the percentiles reflected through twice the
observed slope. The two coincide when the bootstrap distribution is symmetric about b_1.

In words: the percentile interval is just the 2.5th and 97.5th percentiles of the resampled
slopes; the basic interval reflects those two percentiles through twice the observed slope. Notice
the basic interval swaps the roles of the two percentiles, because reflecting turns the upper tail
into the lower endpoint.

That swap is easier to see than to say. 

Figure 9 draws it. Think of the observed slope
b_1 as a mirror standing in the middle. The percentile interval sits above; to get the basic
interval you reflect each endpoint through the mirror, so the point that was far to the right lands
far to the left and vice versa. The arithmetic 2b_1 - q is exactly “reflect q across b_1”: it
measures how far q sits from b_1 and steps the same distance to the other side.



Figure 9:The basic interval is the percentile interval mirrored through the observed slope b1. Reflecting swaps the tails: the far-right percentile becomes the left endpoint and the far-left percentile becomes the right endpoint. On a skewed distribution like this one the two intervals sit in different places; on a symmetric one they land on top of each other.

hierarchy.lvl1: 5. Randomization and bootstrap inference for regression
5. Randomization and bootstrap inference for regression

records.content:
Theorem 5.8: Basic bootstrap interval

Suppose the bootstrap principle holds, so that b_1^{\ast} - b_1 has approximately the same
distribution as b_1 - \beta_1. If q_{\alpha/2} and q_{1-\alpha/2} are the percentiles of the
bootstrap slopes b_1^{\ast}, thenP\big(2 b_1 - q_{1-\alpha/2} \le \beta_1 \le 2 b_1 - q_{\alpha/2}\big) \approx 1 - \alpha,

so the basic interval (2 b_1 - q_{1-\alpha/2},\, 2 b_1 - q_{\alpha/2}) has approximate coverage
1 - \alpha. It equals the percentile interval exactly when the bootstrap distribution is
symmetric about b_1.

Proof. The bootstrap principle says the variation of the
resampled slope around the observed slope imitates the variation of the observed slope around the
true slope. Write this as: the distribution of b_1^{\ast} - b_1 approximates the distribution of
b_1 - \beta_1. Let q_{\alpha/2} and q_{1-\alpha/2} be the percentiles of b_1^{\ast}, so by
definitionP\big(q_{\alpha/2} \le b_1^{\ast} \le q_{1-\alpha/2}\big) = 1 - \alpha .

Subtract b_1 throughout to center on the observed slope:P\big(q_{\alpha/2} - b_1 \le b_1^{\ast} - b_1 \le q_{1-\alpha/2} - b_1\big) = 1 - \alpha .

Now use the bootstrap principle to replace b_1^{\ast} - b_1 with b_1 - \beta_1, which has
approximately the same distribution:P\big(q_{\alpha/2} - b_1 \le b_1 - \beta_1 \le q_{1-\alpha/2} - b_1\big) \approx 1 - \alpha .

Solve the inequalities for \beta_1. Subtracting b_1 and negating (which flips the inequality
directions and swaps the endpoints) givesP\big(2 b_1 - q_{1-\alpha/2} \le \beta_1 \le 2 b_1 - q_{\alpha/2}\big) \approx 1 - \alpha .

That bracket is the basic interval. The percentile interval (q_{\alpha/2}, q_{1-\alpha/2}) is
what you get if you skip the pivoting step and read the bootstrap percentiles as if they were
percentiles of \beta_1 directly. The two agree exactly when the bootstrap distribution is
symmetric about b_1, because then reflecting the percentiles through 2 b_1 lands them back on
themselves. \blacksquare

Example 5.4: A 95% interval for the punting slope, three ways

Question. What is a 95\% confidence interval for the punting slope, and do the bootstrap and
classical intervals agree?

Intuition. Read the percentile and basic intervals off the case-bootstrap slopes and put them
next to the classical t interval.

Formula. Percentile (q_{0.025}, q_{0.975}); basic (2b_1 - q_{0.975}, 2b_1 - q_{0.025});
classical b_1 \pm t^{\ast}_{0.975, n-2}\, s\{b_1\}.

Computation. From the case bootstrap, R gives percentile (0.416, 1.388) and basic
(0.416, 1.388); Python gives percentile (0.413, 1.382) and basic (0.422, 1.391). The
classical t interval is (0.440, 1.364). 

Figure 10 draws all three on the bootstrap
histogram.



Figure 10:Three 95% intervals for the punting slope on the case-bootstrap distribution. The percentile and basic intervals nearly coincide because the bootstrap distribution is close to symmetric, and both are a little wider than the classical t interval, which trusts the normal model.

Interpretation. All three intervals say the slope is somewhere around 0.4 to 1.4 feet per
pound, comfortably above zero, so the effect is real and its size is estimated to within a factor
of about three. The percentile and basic intervals nearly coincide here because the case-bootstrap
distribution is close to symmetric about 0.902 (recall 

Figure 8). The
bootstrap intervals are slightly wider than the classical (0.440, 1.364) because the classical
interval trusts a normal model that thirteen points cannot confirm, while the case bootstrap pays
for that honesty with a little extra width.

Try it 5.5

The observed slope is b_1 = 0.902 and the Python percentile interval is (0.413, 1.382). Use the
reflection formula to compute the basic interval by hand, and check it against the printed
(0.4216, 1.3911).

Solution

The basic interval is (2 b_1 - q_{0.975}, \; 2 b_1 - q_{0.025}) with 2 b_1 = 1.8041. The lower
endpoint is 1.8041 - 1.382 = 0.4221 and the upper endpoint is 1.8041 - 0.413 = 1.3911, giving
(0.422, 1.391), matching the printed values to rounding. Notice the upper percentile 1.382
became the lower endpoint: reflection swaps the tails.

hierarchy.lvl1: 5. Randomization and bootstrap inference for regression
hierarchy.lvl2: 5.6 When computational and classical inference agree

hierarchy.lvl1: 5. Randomization and bootstrap inference for regression
hierarchy.lvl2: 5.6 When computational and classical inference agree

records.content:
You have now met every tool in the chapter. Before comparing their answers, it helps to see them
on one page. 

Figure 11 is a map: it starts from your fitted slope and asks what you want
to know, then walks you to the right method. The first fork is the big one. If you want to know
whether an effect exists at all, you run a permutation test and report a p-value. If you want to
know how precise your estimate is, you run a bootstrap and report a standard error or confidence
interval. The bootstrap then forks again by how the data were collected: case resampling for
randomly sampled subjects, residual resampling for a design with fixed predictor values.



Figure 11:The chapter on one page. The first question, detect an effect or measure its precision, chooses between the permutation test and the bootstrap; the bootstrap then splits by how the data arose.

You have now seen three verdicts on the punting slope (permutation, bootstrap, classical) that all
pointed the same way, and a Toluca slope where they agreed up to the permutation test’s resolution
floor. That is the common case: when the classical assumptions roughly hold, the computational and
classical answers match, and the agreement is itself evidence that the classical p-value can be
trusted. The methods earn their keep when the assumptions are shaky, because then the resampling
answer does not depend on those assumptions.

Key idea

When computational and classical inference agree, the agreement is itself evidence that the
classical assumptions roughly hold and its p-value can be trusted. When they disagree by a lot,
that gap is a diagnostic: it warns that an assumption is failing, and the assumption-free
resampling answer is the safer one to report while you investigate.

To see the agreement directly, compare the bootstrap and classical standard errors on the Toluca
slope, where n = 25 and the data are clean.set.seed(4210)
boot_toluca <- numeric(B)
nt <- length(xt)
for (i in 1:B) {
  idx <- sample(nt, nt, replace = TRUE)
  boot_toluca[i] <- slope(xt[idx], yt[idx])
}
classical_se <- summary(lm(hours ~ lotsize, data = toluca))$coefficients["lotsize", "Std. Error"]
c(bootstrap_SE = sd(boot_toluca), classical_SE = classical_se)bootstrap_SE classical_SE 
   0.3765516    0.3469722 rng = np.random.default_rng(4210)
nt = len(xt)
boot_toluca = np.empty(B)
for i in range(B):
    idx = rng.integers(0, nt, nt)
    boot_toluca[i] = slope(xt[idx], yt[idx])
classical_se = smf.ols("hours ~ lotsize", data=toluca).fit().bse["lotsize"]
print("bootstrap_SE =", boot_toluca.std(ddof=1), " classical_SE =", classical_se)bootstrap_SE = 0.36568568108182364  classical_SE = 0.3469721568487608

Example 5.5: Bootstrap and classical standard errors on Toluca

Question. On the clean n = 25 Toluca data, does the case bootstrap reproduce the classical
standard error of the slope?

Intuition. Case-resample the Toluca rows 5000 times, take the spread of slopes, and compare
to the formula standard error 0.347 from 

2.5 Sampling behavior and the Gauss-Markov theorem.

Formula. Compare s_{\text{boot}}\{b_1\} with s\{b_1\} = \sqrt{\mathrm{MSE}/S_{xx}}, where
\mathrm{MSE} is the mean squared error (the residual variance estimate) from the fit.

Computation. The case bootstrap gives a standard error of about 0.377 (R) or 0.366
(Python), against the classical 0.347.

Interpretation. The two standard errors are close, within about ten percent, which confirms
that with a healthy sample the bootstrap and the classical formula measure the same variability.
The bootstrap is a touch larger because case resampling adds the variation from the changing lot
sizes and because n = 25 is still modest. The lesson is that the bootstrap is not a rival that
overturns classical inference when the assumptions hold; it agrees with it, and that agreement is
what lets you trust the classical result. The bootstrap matters most when you cannot check the
assumptions, exactly the punting situation.

A wrong reading to avoid: a student sees the bootstrap standard error 0.377 next to the classical
0.347 and concludes “the bootstrap is wrong, because it does not match the formula.” That gets it
backward. Neither number is the truth; both estimate the same unknown standard deviation of b_1,
and they differ only by sampling noise and by the small extra variability case resampling includes
on purpose. The right reading is that they agree well enough to reinforce each other. You would
only worry if they disagreed by a lot, which would be a signal that the classical assumptions are
failing and the bootstrap is telling you something the formula cannot.

Try it 5.6

Suppose on some dataset the bootstrap standard error of a slope came out three times larger than
the classical formula standard error. What would you suspect, and which number would you be more
inclined to trust?

Solution

A large gap means the classical formula is missing a source of variability. Likely culprits are
strong non-constant error variance, a few high-influence points whose repeated appearance in case
resamples swings the slope, or a badly non-normal error distribution in a small sample. In that
situation the classical standard error, which assumes constant variance and leans on normality, is
probably too small, and the case-bootstrap standard error, which makes neither assumption, is the
more trustworthy of the two. The disagreement is a diagnostic: it tells you to investigate the
assumptions before reporting the classical interval. Chapters 9 and 10 build the tools to find and
fix the underlying problem.

hierarchy.lvl1: 5. Randomization and bootstrap inference for regression
5. Randomization and bootstrap inference for regression

records.content:
You can now do inference for a regression slope without leaning on the normal error assumption.
When a small sample puts the t and F tests on thin ice and thirteen residuals cannot confirm
normality, you replace the untestable assumption with a computation. A permutation test shuffles
the response against the fixed predictor to build the no-association null of the slope and reports
p_{\text{perm}} = (M+1)/(B+1); on the punting data it gave \approx 0.001, matching the
classical 0.00127. A bootstrap resamples the data with replacement to measure precision: case
resampling and residual resampling gave slope standard errors of about 0.249 and 0.198, and
percentile and basic intervals near (0.42, 1.39), all consistent with the classical
(0.440, 1.364). The recurring lesson is that when computational and classical answers agree the
agreement builds trust, and when they diverge sharply that divergence is a warning about
assumptions.

Key results at a glance.

Result

Statement or formula

Valid when

Permutation test (Definition 5.1)

Shuffle Y against fixed X, refit, collect slopes

No-association null; any error distribution

Permutation p-value (Definition 5.2)

p_{\text{perm}} = (M+1)/(B+1)

M of B reshuffles at least as extreme

Validity of the permutation p-value (Theorem 5.3)

P(p_{\text{perm}} \le \alpha) \le \alpha; floor 1/(B+1)

Under the no-association null

Bootstrap (Definition 5.4)

Resample with replacement, recompute, take the spread

Sample stands in for the population

Case vs residual resampling (Definition 5.5)

Rows with replacement, or \hat Y_i + e^{\ast}_{s(i)}

Random subjects, or fixed-design predictor

Bootstrap standard error (Definition 5.6)

s_{\text{boot}}\{b_1\} = \operatorname{sd}(b_1^{\ast 1}, \dots, b_1^{\ast B})

B resamples of the fitted model

Percentile / basic intervals (Definition 5.7)

(q_{\alpha/2}, q_{1-\alpha/2}) / (2b_1 - q_{1-\alpha/2}, 2b_1 - q_{\alpha/2})

Read off the bootstrap distribution

Basic interval by pivoting (Theorem 5.8)

Reflect percentiles through 2b_1; equals percentile if symmetric

Bootstrap principle holds

Key terms. Permutation test, permutation distribution, permutation p-value,
plus-one correction, bootstrap, case resampling, residual resampling, bootstrap
standard error, percentile interval, basic bootstrap interval, exchangeability.

You should now be able to.

Explain why normal-theory t and F inference for a slope can fail, and why a small sample makes the risk worse and normality uncheckable.

State the no-association null and describe the exact logic of a permutation test for the slope.

Implement the permutation test from scratch in R and Python and compute a permutation p-value.

Justify the plus-one correction and the resolution floor 1/(B+1), and compare against the classical t-test p-value.

Distinguish case resampling from residual resampling and code each one.

Construct and interpret percentile and basic bootstrap confidence intervals for the slope.

Decide when computational and classical inference agree, and what to report when they disagree.

Where this fits. On the workflow spine of 

The modeling workflow, this
chapter lives in USE: it is inference, turning a fitted slope into a defensible statement about the
world. It is also a quiet CHECK, because comparing computational and classical answers audits
the assumptions behind the classical ones. Chapter 3 gave the classical t and F inference that
assumes normal errors; this chapter gave the resampling inference that does not, and showed the two
agree when the assumptions hold. The bootstrap returns later as a working tool: Chapter 9 reaches
for it when diagnostics say normality has failed, and Chapter 12 uses the same resample-and-refit
mindset to validate models on data they were not fit to (

12.4 Validation: train, test, and cross-validate). The habit you
built here, generate the distribution instead of assuming it, is one you will use for the rest of
the book.

hierarchy.lvl1: 5. Randomization and bootstrap inference for regression
5. Randomization and bootstrap inference for regression

records.content:
Q1. Is a permutation test the same as a bootstrap? No, though both resample. A permutation test
shuffles without replacement to break the link between X and Y, building the distribution of a
statistic under the null hypothesis of no association; it answers “is there an effect?” The
bootstrap resamples with replacement to imitate repeated sampling from the population, building the
distribution of the estimate itself; it answers “how precise is the effect?” Use a permutation test
for a p-value, a bootstrap for a standard error or confidence interval.

Q2. Why shuffle without replacement for the permutation test but with replacement for the
bootstrap? The permutation test needs every reshuffle to be a genuine rearrangement of the same
values, so that it represents one of the equally likely pairings under the null; sampling without
replacement (a shuffle) does exactly that. The bootstrap needs each resample to look like a fresh
draw from the population, and real repeated samples can contain repeats and omissions, so it samples
with replacement.

Q3. How many reshuffles or resamples do I need? Enough that the answer stops moving. For a
p-value you are comparing to 0.05, a few thousand reshuffles is usually plenty; near a sharp
cutoff, use more, because the Monte Carlo floor is 1/(B+1) and the noise shrinks like
1/\sqrt{B}. For a bootstrap standard error, 1000 to 2000 resamples often suffice; for the tail
percentiles of a confidence interval, use 5000 or more, since the extreme quantiles are noisier
than the center.

Q4. My permutation p-value is 0. Do I report p = 0? Never report exactly zero. If no
reshuffle out of B beat your statistic, the plus-one correction gives p_{\text{perm}} =
1/(B+1), and you report “less than” that: for B = 5000, report p < 0.0002. Reporting p = 0
would claim infinite certainty from finite data.

Q5. Which bootstrap should I use, case or residual? Match the resampling to how the data arose.
If the predictor values were fixed by design (a planned experiment with set doses, or the Toluca
lot sizes chosen by schedulers), residual resampling fits the model literally. If the rows are a
random sample from a population and the predictor is just another measured variable (the punters),
case resampling is more honest. It is also the safer default when you are unsure, because it
captures variability in the predictor and does not assume constant error variance.

Q6. The bootstrap and the t test gave slightly different intervals. Which is right? Neither is
“right” in an absolute sense; both estimate the same true uncertainty. When they are close, as in
the punting and Toluca examples, trust either and note the agreement. When they differ a lot, the
disagreement is information: it usually means a classical assumption (normality, constant variance,
no influential points) is failing, and the assumption-free bootstrap is the safer report while you
investigate.

Q7. Do these methods work for multiple regression? Yes. The bootstrap generalizes directly:
case-resample whole rows, or residual-resample around the fitted surface, and collect whichever
coefficient you care about. Permutation is subtler with several predictors, because shuffling one
predictor while holding others fixed requires care about what null you are testing, but the core
idea carries over. This chapter keeps to simple regression so the logic stays visible; the
resampling code changes little when you add predictors.

hierarchy.lvl1: 5. Randomization and bootstrap inference for regression
5. Randomization and bootstrap inference for regression

records.content:
Note

Unless a problem says otherwise, use punting.csv with the model Distance ~ RStr (slope
b_1 = 0.902, classical s\{b_1\} = 0.210, n = 13), or toluca.csv with hours ~ lotsize
(slope b_1 = 3.5702, n = 25). Set seeds (set.seed(4210) in R, np.random.default_rng(4210)
in Python) so your resampling answers are reproducible. Problems are marked (A) concepts,
(B) theory, or (C) data analysis. Odd-numbered answers appear in Appendix H; full solutions are in
the instructor materials.

(A) In one sentence each, state what question a permutation test answers and what question a bootstrap answers, and say which resamples with replacement.

(A) Explain why thirteen residuals cannot confirm or rule out normal errors, referring to 

Figure 2.

(A) State the null and alternative hypotheses of the permutation test for a slope, in words and in symbols.

(A) Why does the permutation p-value use the plus-one correction (M+1)/(B+1) instead of M/B? What goes wrong if a test can report exactly zero?

(A) A permutation test with B = 4999 reshuffles finds M = 0. What p-value do you report, and what is the smallest p-value this run could ever give?

(A) Describe in words the difference between case resampling and residual resampling, and give one data-collection story that fits each.

(A) The percentile interval for a slope is (1.1, 2.9) and the observed slope is b_1 = 2.2. Without a computer, find the basic interval.

(A) A classmate reports a permutation p-value of 0 for a slope. Rewrite their conclusion so it is defensible, assuming they used B = 9999 reshuffles.

(A) Explain why shuffling Y against fixed X, shuffling X against fixed Y, and shuffling both give the same permutation test.

(B) Show that when you permute the response, the reshuffled slope b_1^{\pi} = S_{xy}^{\pi}/S_{xx} is a fixed positive constant times the reshuffled cross-product S_{xy}^{\pi}, and conclude that the permutation test on the slope is identical to the permutation test on the sample correlation.

(B) Derive the basic bootstrap interval (2b_1 - q_{1-\alpha/2}, \, 2b_1 - q_{\alpha/2}) (Theorem 5.8) from the bootstrap principle that b_1^{\ast} - b_1 has approximately the same distribution as b_1 - \beta_1. Show every step of the algebra that flips the endpoints.

(B) Prove that the percentile and basic intervals coincide exactly when the bootstrap distribution of b_1^{\ast} is symmetric about b_1.

(B) The exact two-sided permutation p-value counts arrangements with |b_1^{\pi}| \ge |b_1| out of n!. Explain why the observed arrangement is always counted, and use this to argue that the exact p-value is at least 1/n!.

(B) A statistic T has permutation distribution placing probability 1/n! on each arrangement. Explain why, under the null, the random variable p_{\text{perm}} (computed from the full enumeration) satisfies P(p_{\text{perm}} \le \alpha) \le \alpha for any cutoff \alpha that is a multiple of 1/n!, so the test controls its error rate (Theorem 5.3).

(B) The Monte Carlo standard error of a bootstrap standard-error estimate shrinks like 1/\sqrt{B}. Explain qualitatively why doubling the accuracy of a tail confidence-interval endpoint costs four times the resamples.

(B) Explain why the case-bootstrap standard error can exceed the classical formula standard error even when the model is correct, by naming the extra source of variation case resampling includes that the residual bootstrap and the classical formula hold fixed.

(C) Reproduce the punting permutation test in R or Python with set.seed(4210) / default_rng(4210) and B = 5000. Report the count of extreme reshuffles and the permutation p-value, and compare to the classical t-test p-value 0.00127.

(C) Repeat the punting permutation test with the predictor LStr (left-leg strength) instead of RStr. Report the observed slope, the permutation p-value, and whether your conclusion changes.

(C) Run the Toluca permutation test with B = 5000. Report the largest reshuffled slope in size and explain why the permutation p-value cannot go below 1/5001 here.

(C) Compute the case-bootstrap and residual-bootstrap standard errors of the punting slope with B = 5000. Report both and explain which is larger and why.

(C) Compute percentile and basic 95\% intervals for the punting slope from the case bootstrap, and put them next to the classical t interval (0.440, 1.364). Comment on the agreement.

(C) Using the punting data and predictor OStr (overall leg strength), build a 90\% percentile bootstrap interval for the slope (use the 5th and 95th percentiles). Report the interval and interpret it in the units of the problem.

(C) Bootstrap the Toluca slope 5000 times by case resampling and compare the bootstrap standard error to the classical 0.347. State the percentage difference and whether the two agree.

(C) Write a permutation test for the punting slope but use the sample correlation as the statistic instead of the slope. Confirm that the p-value matches the slope-based test (problem 17), illustrating the identity from problem 10.

(C) Increase the punting permutation test to B = 50000 reshuffles. Does the p-value estimate change materially from the B = 5000 value? Report both and comment on Monte Carlo noise.

(C) Make a normal quantile plot of the punting residuals in R or Python, then generate three normal samples of size 13 and plot their quantile plots. Describe how hard it is to tell the real residuals from the normal samples, connecting to 

Figure 2.

(C) For the punting slope, compute a bootstrap standard error and form the interval b_1 \pm 1.96 \, s_{\text{boot}}\{b_1\} (a normal-approximation bootstrap interval). Compare it to the percentile interval and explain when the two would disagree.

(B) An analyst uses a paired shuffle: they reorder the rows of the data but keep each (X_i, Y_i) pair together, then refit. Show that every such “permutation” gives exactly the observed slope, and explain why this tests nothing.
