records.content:
In Chapter 2 a single predictor gave one slope and one intercept. Add a second
predictor and you have two slopes plus an intercept; the mean response is now a
tilted plane instead of a line, as 

Figure 2 shows for Dwaine. With more
predictors it is a flat sheet in a space you cannot picture. Writing separate
symbols for every coefficient and separate sums for every normal equation would
bury the idea under bookkeeping. The fix is to stack the observations into vectors
and matrices and let one line of matrix algebra carry all the bookkeeping for us.



Figure 2:With one predictor the fit is a line; with two it is a tilted plane. Each city’s sales is a green dot, the plane is the model’s guess, and each dashed stem is a residual. Multiple regression finds the plane whose stems are smallest in total squared length.

The systematic part of each observation is a weighted sum of that city’s predictor
values, the same weights (\beta_0, \beta_1, \dots) reused for every city. Stacking
the cities vertically turns “the same weights applied to every row” into a single
matrix-times-vector product. 

Figure 3 shows the four objects and
their shapes.



Figure 3:The general linear model as stacked arrays. The design matrix X carries a leading column of ones so that the intercept beta-0 is handled exactly like any other coefficient.

hierarchy.lvl2: 7.1 The model and least squares in matrix form
7.1 The model and least squares in matrix form

records.content:
The general linear model (Definition 7.1) collects all n observations into one
matrix equation.

Definition 7.1: General linear model

The general linear model for n observations and p parameters is\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon},

where \mathbf{Y} is the n \times 1 vector of responses, \mathbf{X} the
n \times p design matrix, \boldsymbol{\beta} the p \times 1 vector of
coefficients, and \boldsymbol{\varepsilon} the n \times 1 vector of random errors.

In words: each observation’s response equals a weighted sum of that observation’s
predictor values plus a random error, and the same weights are reused for every row.
The four objects are:

\mathbf{Y} is the n \times 1 vector of responses, one entry per observation.

\mathbf{X} is the n \times p design matrix (Definition 7.2). Its first column is all ones (for the intercept); each remaining column holds one predictor’s values. Row i is city i’s data.

\boldsymbol{\beta} is the p \times 1 vector of unknown parameters (\beta_0, \beta_1, \dots, \beta_{p-1})'.

\boldsymbol{\varepsilon} is the n \times 1 vector of random errors.

p is the number of parameters, counting the intercept; with k predictors, p = k + 1. Here n = 21 and p = 3.

Definition 7.2: Design matrix

The design matrix \mathbf{X} is the n \times p matrix whose first column is
all ones (carrying the intercept) and whose remaining columns each hold one
predictor’s values, so row i is observation i’s predictor data.

The error assumptions of Chapter 2 become two compact statements about the whole
vector at once:E\{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\} = \mathbf{0}, \qquad
\operatorname{Cov}\{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\} = \sigma^2 \mathbf{I} .

In words: every error averages to zero, and the covariance matrix is \sigma^2
times the identity. That one symbol packs both assumptions at once: each error has
the same variance \sigma^2 (down the diagonal), and distinct errors are
uncorrelated (off the diagonal). Because \mathbf{X} and \boldsymbol{\beta} are
fixed, E\{\mathbf{Y}\} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} and
\operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{Y}\} = \sigma^2 \mathbf{I} as well. We assume
throughout that \mathbf{X} has full column rank p: no predictor is a copy of
another, or an exact linear combination of the others. That is exactly what makes
\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X} invertible (

6.4 The inverse and the normal equations).

To fit the model we reuse the least-squares idea from 

2.2 Least squares from first principles: pick the
coefficient vector \mathbf{b} that makes the fitted values closest to the data in
total squared error. Writing a candidate vector as \mathbf{b}, the sum of squared
errors isQ(\mathbf{b}) = \sum_{i=1}^n (Y_i - \mathbf{x}_i'\mathbf{b})^2
= (\mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{b})'(\mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{b}) ,

where \mathbf{x}_i' is row i of \mathbf{X}. In words: Q is the squared length
of the residual vector, the same quantity Chapter 2 minimized, now written as one
dot product. The minimizer is the least-squares estimator (Theorem 7.3)\mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y} .

In words: gather the cross-products of the predictors (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}) and
of the predictors with the response (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}), and multiply by the
inverse of the first. We derive this next, then check it on Dwaine.

hierarchy.lvl2: 7.1 The model and least squares in matrix form
7.1 The model and least squares in matrix form

records.content:
Theorem 7.3: The least-squares estimator

Under the general linear model with \mathbf{X} of full column rank, the sum of
squared errors Q(\mathbf{b}) has a unique minimizer, the solution of the normal
equations \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}\mathbf{b} = \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}:\mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y} .

Proof. Expand Q using the transpose
rules of 

6.2 Transpose and matrix multiplication. Since \mathbf{b}'\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y} is a scalar, it
equals its own transpose \mathbf{Y}'\mathbf{X}\mathbf{b}, so the two middle terms
combine:Q(\mathbf{b}) = \mathbf{Y}'\mathbf{Y} - 2\,\mathbf{b}'\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}
+ \mathbf{b}'\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}\mathbf{b} .

We minimize by taking the gradient with respect to \mathbf{b} and setting it to
zero. The gradient is just the vector of partial derivatives, one per entry of
\mathbf{b}, and setting it to zero finds the bottom of the bowl. Two
vector-derivative rules do the work: for a constant vector \mathbf{a},
\nabla_{\mathbf{b}}(\mathbf{a}'\mathbf{b}) = \mathbf{a}; and for a symmetric matrix
\mathbf{M}, \nabla_{\mathbf{b}}(\mathbf{b}'\mathbf{M}\mathbf{b}) = 2\mathbf{M}\mathbf{b}.
These are the vector twins of the scalar rules \frac{d}{db}(ab) = a and
\frac{d}{db}(mb^2) = 2mb you already know. The matrix \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X} is
symmetric, so\nabla_{\mathbf{b}} Q = -2\,\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y} + 2\,\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}\mathbf{b} .

Setting the gradient to zero and dividing by 2 gives the normal equations
(Definition 7.4)\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}\mathbf{b} = \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y} .

Because \mathbf{X} has full column rank, \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X} is invertible
(

6.4 The inverse and the normal equations), so we may multiply on the left by
(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}:\mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y} .

This stationary point is the global minimum, not a saddle or maximum, because the
matrix of second derivatives of Q is 2\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}, which is positive
definite; a quadratic with a positive-definite Hessian is strictly convex and has a
single lowest point. \blacksquare

Definition 7.4: Normal equations

The normal equations are the linear system\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}\mathbf{b} = \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y},

whose solution is the least-squares estimator. Equivalently, they state that the
residual \mathbf{e} = \mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{b} is orthogonal to every
column of \mathbf{X}.

These are the same normal equations as Chapter 2, now stated once for any number of
predictors. In 

2.2 Least squares from first principles the two scalar normal equations were
\sum e_i = 0 and \sum X_i e_i = 0; the single matrix equation
\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}\mathbf{b} = \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y} contains both of those and
one more for each extra predictor, as we will see in 

7.7 Simple regression as a special case.

hierarchy.lvl2: 7.1 The model and least squares in matrix form
7.1 The model and least squares in matrix form

records.content:
Chapter 6 built \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X} and \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y} for Dwaine by
hand. Here we let the computer form them and solve.dwaine <- read.csv("data/dwaine.csv")
head(dwaine, 3)
X <- cbind(1, dwaine$targtpop, dwaine$dispoinc)
Y <- dwaine$sales
dim(X)  targtpop dispoinc sales
1     68.5     16.7 174.4
2     45.2     16.8 164.4
3     91.3     18.2 244.2
[1] 21  3

Example 7.1: Fitting Dwaine Studios by matrix algebra

Question. What are the least-squares coefficients for predicting studio sales
from target population and disposable income?

Intuition. Form the cross-product matrix \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X} and the
cross-product vector \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}, then solve the normal equations by
multiplying \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y} by (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}.

Formula. \mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}.

Computation.XtX <- t(X) %*% X
XtY <- t(X) %*% Y
XtX
XtY       [,1]     [,2]     [,3]
[1,]   21.0  1302.40   360.00
[2,] 1302.4 87707.94 22609.19
[3,]  360.0 22609.19  6190.26
          [,1]
[1,]   3820.00
[2,] 249643.35
[3,]  66072.75XtX_inv <- solve(XtX)
b <- XtX_inv %*% XtY
b          [,1]
[1,] -68.85707
[2,]   1.45456
[3,]   9.36550

The built-in lm fits the same model with a formula, and its coefficients agree to
every printed digit.coef(lm(sales ~ targtpop + dispoinc, data = dwaine))(Intercept)    targtpop    dispoinc
  -68.85707     1.45456     9.36550

Now the same computation in Python, reading the same CSV.import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf

dwaine = pd.read_csv("data/dwaine.csv")
X = np.column_stack([np.ones(len(dwaine)), dwaine["targtpop"], dwaine["dispoinc"]])
Y = dwaine["sales"].to_numpy()
XtX = X.T @ X
XtY = X.T @ Y
b = np.linalg.inv(XtX) @ XtY
print(b)
print(smf.ols("sales ~ targtpop + dispoinc", data=dwaine).fit().params.values)[-68.85707315   1.45455958   9.36550038]
[-68.85707315   1.45455958   9.36550038]

Interpretation. The fitted model is
\hat{Y} = -68.86 + 1.4546\,X_1 + 9.3655\,X_2, with X_1 the young-population count
(thousands) and X_2 disposable income (thousand dollars). Holding income fixed,
each extra thousand young residents is associated with about $1{,}455 more in annual
sales; holding population fixed, each extra thousand dollars of per-capita income is
associated with about $9{,}366 more. The intercept -68.86 is the model’s sales at
zero population and zero income, far outside the data, so it only anchors the plane.
Chapter 8 develops the “holding the other predictor fixed” reading and its limits;
here the point is that one matrix formula produced all three numbers at once.

Try it 7.1

The design matrix \mathbf{X} has n = 21 rows and p = 3 columns. Without
running code, state the dimensions of \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X},
\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}, \mathbf{b}, and the fitted vector \hat{\mathbf{Y}}, and
say why \mathbf{X}\mathbf{X}' would be the wrong matrix to invert.

Solution

\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X} is 3 \times 3 (p \times p), \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y} is
3 \times 1, \mathbf{b} is 3 \times 1, and \hat{\mathbf{Y}} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{b}
is 21 \times 1. The matrix \mathbf{X}\mathbf{X}' is 21 \times 21 but has rank
at most 3, so it is singular and cannot be inverted; the normal equations need the
small full-rank p \times p matrix \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}, not the large rank-deficient
\mathbf{X}\mathbf{X}'.

The calculus derivation finds the answer but hides why it is the answer. There is a
picture that makes least squares obvious, and it is worth carrying for the rest of
the course. Think of the response \mathbf{Y} as a single arrow (a point) in
n-dimensional space, one axis per observation. As the coefficient vector
\mathbf{b} ranges over all possible values, the fitted vector
\hat{\mathbf{Y}} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{b} traces out a flat subspace: every linear
combination of the columns of \mathbf{X}. This subspace is the column space of
\mathbf{X} (Definition 7.5), and it holds every prediction the model can possibly make.

Definition 7.5: Column space

The column space of \mathbf{X} is the set of all vectors \mathbf{X}\mathbf{b}
as \mathbf{b} ranges over every coefficient vector: a p-dimensional flat subspace
of n-dimensional space that holds every fitted vector the model can produce.

The data point \mathbf{Y} almost never sits exactly in that subspace, because real
data has noise. So the fitting problem is: which point in the column space is closest
to \mathbf{Y}? The closest point on a flat surface to an outside point is the foot
of the perpendicular dropped from the point to the surface. That foot is
\hat{\mathbf{Y}}, and the perpendicular segment is the residual vector
\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{Y} - \hat{\mathbf{Y}}. 

Figure 4 draws exactly this
for the smallest case that fits on a page, three observations and two columns.



Figure 4:Least squares as projection. The fitted vector Y-hat is the point of the column space closest to Y, reached by dropping a perpendicular; the residual e is that perpendicular, meeting the column space at a right angle.

The geometric condition is that the residual is orthogonal to every column of
\mathbf{X}:\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{0}, \qquad \text{where } \mathbf{e} = \mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{b} .

In words: each column of \mathbf{X}, dotted with the residual vector, gives zero, so
the residual points in a direction the model cannot represent. Expanding
\mathbf{X}'(\mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{b}) = \mathbf{0} gives back the normal
equations \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}\mathbf{b} = \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}, so the geometry
and the calculus lead to the identical solution.

Derivation (least squares as orthogonal projection). Let \mathbf{b} solve the
normal equations, so \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{0} with
\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{b}. Take any other candidate coefficient
vector \mathbf{b}^{\ast}, with fitted vector \mathbf{X}\mathbf{b}^{\ast}. Split the
gap from \mathbf{Y} to that candidate by routing through \hat{\mathbf{Y}} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{b}:\mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{b}^{\ast}
= \underbrace{(\mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{b})}_{\mathbf{e}}
+ \underbrace{\mathbf{X}(\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{b}^{\ast})}_{\mathbf{g}} .

The second piece \mathbf{g} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{b}^{\ast}) lives in the
column space. Its squared length adds without a cross term, because the cross term
vanishes by orthogonality:\mathbf{e}'\mathbf{g} = \mathbf{e}'\mathbf{X}(\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{b}^{\ast})
= (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{e})'(\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{b}^{\ast}) = \mathbf{0}'(\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{b}^{\ast}) = 0 .

Therefore, by the Pythagorean theorem in n dimensions,\|\mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{b}^{\ast}\|^2
= \|\mathbf{e}\|^2 + \|\mathbf{g}\|^2 \; \ge \; \|\mathbf{e}\|^2 ,

with equality only when \|\mathbf{g}\|^2 = 0, that is
\mathbf{X}\mathbf{b}^{\ast} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{b}. Since \mathbf{X} has full column
rank, this forces \mathbf{b}^{\ast} = \mathbf{b}. So the normal-equations solution
gives the unique smallest sum of squared errors, and \hat{\mathbf{Y}} is the closest
point of the column space to \mathbf{Y}. \blacksquare

This is the same algebraic identity as the Chapter 2 proof in 

2.2 Least squares from first principles,
now read as geometry: the cross term vanishes there because \sum e_i = 0 and
\sum X_i e_i = 0, which is precisely \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{0} written
out. The projection view (in 

6.5 Idempotent and projection matrices) is the one to remember, because
diagnostics in Chapter 9 are all about which observations sit in awkward corners of
the column space.

Example 7.2: The residual is orthogonal to the predictors

Question. Do the Dwaine residuals really satisfy \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{0}?

Intuition. Compute the fitted vector \hat{\mathbf{Y}} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{b} and
the residual \mathbf{e} = \mathbf{Y} - \hat{\mathbf{Y}}, then form
\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{e}. Each of its three entries should be zero (up to
floating-point dust), one per column of \mathbf{X}.

Formula. \mathbf{e} = \mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{b} and \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{0}.

Computation.n <- nrow(X)
p <- ncol(X)
yhat <- X %*% b
e <- Y - yhat
round(t(X) %*% e, 6)          # should be the zero vector     [,1]
[1,]    0
[2,]    0
[3,]    0n, p = X.shape
yhat = X @ b
e = Y - yhat
print(np.round(X.T @ e, 6))   # should be the zero vector[0. 0. 0.]

Interpretation. All three dot products are zero. The first (from the column of
ones) is \sum e_i = 0; the other two say the residuals are uncorrelated with each
predictor. The residual vector carries exactly the part of \mathbf{Y} that the
predictors cannot explain, which is why plotting it against the fitted values is the
first diagnostic we draw, in 

Figure 5.



Figure 5:The Dwaine residuals against fitted values form a flat, patternless band around zero, the picture that supports the constant-variance and linearity assumptions. Chapter 9 turns this habit into a full diagnostic toolkit.

Durable skill: See a fit as a projection

Once you picture fitting as dropping a perpendicular from the data onto the space of
possible fits, a lot of regression stops needing memorization. The residual is
orthogonal to the fit because the perpendicular is the shortest route. Adding a
predictor enlarges the column space, so the residual can only get shorter. Two
predictors that point in nearly the same direction span a thin sliver of space, which
is why their coefficients are unstable. You will meet this picture again in the
leverage values (Chapter 9), in added-variable plots (

8.5 Added-variable plots), and in any method that decomposes data into
“fit plus leftover.” To practice, next time you fit a model, ask what space the fit
lives in and what direction the residual points.

Section 7.2 pictured the fit as a projection of \mathbf{Y} onto the column space.
That projection is carried out by a single matrix, and giving it a name pays off for
the rest of the chapter. The fitted vector is a fixed linear function of the data:
plug the formula for \mathbf{b} into \hat{\mathbf{Y}} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{b} and you get
\hat{\mathbf{Y}} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}. The
matrix sitting in front of \mathbf{Y} turns the raw responses into fitted values,
so it earns the name hat matrix (Definition 7.6): it puts the hat on \mathbf{Y}. It is the single
most useful matrix in the rest of the course. Its diagonal entries are the leverages
that flag unusual predictor values (Chapter 9), and its algebra makes the variance
results below fall out in a line.

Definition 7.6: Hat matrix

The hat matrix is the n \times n matrix\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}',

which depends only on the predictors and maps the responses to the fitted values,
\hat{\mathbf{Y}} = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{Y}: it puts the hat on \mathbf{Y}.

The hat matrix and the fitted and residual vectors are\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}', \qquad
\hat{\mathbf{Y}} = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{Y}, \qquad
\mathbf{e} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})\mathbf{Y} .

\mathbf{H} is n \times n and depends only on the predictors, not on \mathbf{Y}.

\hat{\mathbf{Y}} = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{Y}: each fitted value is a fixed weighted average of all the responses, with weights read off a row of \mathbf{H}.

\mathbf{e} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})\mathbf{Y}: the residual is what is left after projecting, so \mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H} is itself a projection, onto the space perpendicular to the columns of \mathbf{X}.

Figure 6 shows the Toluca hat matrix as a grid of weights; the diagonal
entries, outlined, are the leverages h_{ii}.



Figure 6:The Toluca hat matrix H turns responses into fitted values. Reading across row i gives the weights that build the i-th fitted value; the outlined green diagonal entries are the leverage values that Chapter 9 uses to find unusual observations.

Theorem 7.7: Properties of the hat matrix

For a design matrix \mathbf{X} of full column rank, the hat matrix
\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}' is symmetric
(\mathbf{H}' = \mathbf{H}) and idempotent (\mathbf{H}\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{H}),
with \operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{H}) = p. Consequently \mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H} is
also symmetric and idempotent, with \operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}) = n - p.

Proof. Write
\mathbf{A} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}, which is symmetric because
\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X} is (the inverse of a symmetric matrix is symmetric, by


6.4 The inverse and the normal equations).

Symmetric. Using (\mathbf{BCD})' = \mathbf{D}'\mathbf{C}'\mathbf{B}' from


6.2 Transpose and matrix multiplication,\mathbf{H}' = \big(\mathbf{X}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}'\big)' = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{A}'\mathbf{X}' = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}' = \mathbf{H} .

Idempotent. Projecting twice equals projecting once. The middle
\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X} cancels its inverse:\mathbf{H}\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{A}\,\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}\,\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}'
= \mathbf{X}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}' = \mathbf{H} .

Figure 7 shows why: once \mathbf{H} has landed a vector in the column
space, projecting again cannot move it.

Trace p. The trace of a matrix is the sum of its diagonal entries. Use the cyclic property of the trace, \operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{B}\mathbf{C}) = \operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{C}\mathbf{B}) (a standard identity: both sides equal \sum_{i,j} B_{ij} C_{ji}), to slide \mathbf{X} around:\operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{H}) = \operatorname{tr}\!\big(\mathbf{X}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}'\big)
= \operatorname{tr}\!\big(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}\big)
= \operatorname{tr}\!\big((\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})\big)
= \operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{I}_p) = p .

Consequently \mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H} is also symmetric and idempotent, with
\operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}) = n - p. That trace is the degrees of
freedom for error, and it is the reason the divisor in the variance estimate is
n - p, proved in 

7.5 Estimating the error variance. \blacksquare



Figure 7:Why H is idempotent. Projecting the vector y onto the column space lands it at Hy inside that space; projecting a second time leaves it exactly where it is, so HH equals H.

Key idea

Almost every result left in this chapter rests on two facts about the hat matrix: it
is a projection (symmetric and idempotent), and its trace counts the parameters, so
\operatorname{tr}\mathbf{H} = p and \operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}) = n - p.
The first fact makes the fitted values and residuals behave; the second fixes the
divisor in the variance estimate. Hold those two and the rest is bookkeeping.

Example 7.3: Checking the hat matrix properties

Question. For the Dwaine design, is \mathbf{H} symmetric and idempotent, and
is its trace equal to p = 3?

Intuition. Build \mathbf{H} from \mathbf{X}, compare it to its transpose and
to \mathbf{H}\mathbf{H}, and sum its diagonal.

Formula. \mathbf{H} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}',
\mathbf{H}' = \mathbf{H}, \mathbf{H}\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{H}, \operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{H}) = p.

Computation.H <- X %*% XtX_inv %*% t(X)
c(symmetric = all.equal(H, t(H)),
  idempotent = all.equal(H %*% H, H))
sum(diag(H))                  # trace of H symmetric idempotent
      TRUE       TRUE
[1] 3H = X @ np.linalg.inv(XtX) @ X.T
print(np.allclose(H, H.T), np.allclose(H @ H, H))
print(round(np.trace(H), 6))  # trace of HTrue True
3.0

Interpretation. The hat matrix is symmetric and idempotent, and its trace is
exactly 3 = p. The trace being p is not a coincidence of these data: it holds for
any full-rank design, and it is the fact that makes \mathrm{MSE} unbiased. The
21 \times 21 matrix \mathbf{H} compresses to a single meaningful number, its
trace, which counts the parameters the fit spends.

Try it 7.2

The Toluca simple-regression model has n = 25 and p = 2. Using only the trace
property, state \operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{H}) and \operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}),
and say what each counts.

Solution

\operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{H}) = p = 2, the number of parameters (intercept and
slope), which is the dimension of the column space the fit lives in.
\operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}) = n - p = 25 - 2 = 23, the error degrees
of freedom, the dimension of the space the residual lives in. The two spaces are
perpendicular and their dimensions add to n = 25.

The vector \mathbf{b} came from one sample of 21 cities. A different 21 cities would
give a slightly different \mathbf{b}, so \mathbf{b} is random, with a mean and a
covariance across the samples we could have drawn. Two questions repeat from Chapter
2, now for a whole vector: is \mathbf{b} right on average, and how much does it vary?
The answers are clean because \mathbf{b} is a fixed matrix times \mathbf{Y}, and


6.7 Random vectors, expectation, and covariance matrices tells us exactly how means and covariances pass through a linear
map.

The least-squares vector is unbiased with a covariance matrix built from
(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}:E\{\mathbf{b}\} = \boldsymbol{\beta}, \qquad
\operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{b}\} = \sigma^2 (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1} .

In words: on average \mathbf{b} hits the true coefficient vector, and its
covariance matrix is \sigma^2 times the inverse cross-product matrix. The diagonal
entries are the variances of the individual coefficients; the off-diagonal entries
are the covariances between them, which is why two coefficients can be correlated
(the tilted cloud in 

Figure 8). Replacing \sigma^2 by its estimate
\mathrm{MSE} gives the estimated covariance matrix
s^2\{\mathbf{b}\} = \mathrm{MSE}\,(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}, whose diagonal square
roots are the standard errors software prints.

Theorem 7.8: Mean and covariance of the least-squares estimator

Under the general linear model with E\{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\} = \mathbf{0} and
\operatorname{Cov}\{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\} = \sigma^2\mathbf{I}, the least-squares
estimator is unbiased with covarianceE\{\mathbf{b}\} = \boldsymbol{\beta}, \qquad
\operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{b}\} = \sigma^2 (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1} .

If in addition \boldsymbol{\varepsilon} \sim N(\mathbf{0}, \sigma^2\mathbf{I}), then
\mathbf{b} \sim N\!\big(\boldsymbol{\beta},\ \sigma^2 (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\big).

Proof. Write
\mathbf{b} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{Y} with the fixed matrix
\mathbf{A} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'. For a fixed matrix times a
random vector, 

6.7 Random vectors, expectation, and covariance matrices gives E\{\mathbf{A}\mathbf{Y}\} = \mathbf{A}\,E\{\mathbf{Y}\}
and \operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{A}\mathbf{Y}\} = \mathbf{A}\operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{Y}\}\mathbf{A}'.

Mean. Since E\{\mathbf{Y}\} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta},E\{\mathbf{b}\} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\,\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}
= (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})\boldsymbol{\beta} = \boldsymbol{\beta} .

Covariance. Since \operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{Y}\} = \sigma^2\mathbf{I},\operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{b}\} = \mathbf{A}\,(\sigma^2\mathbf{I})\,\mathbf{A}'
= \sigma^2 (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\,\mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}
= \sigma^2 (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1} ,

where the middle \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X} cancels one inverse and the symmetry of
(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1} handles the transpose. \blacksquare

If we add the normality assumption \boldsymbol{\varepsilon} \sim N(\mathbf{0}, \sigma^2\mathbf{I}),
then \mathbf{Y} is multivariate normal, and a fixed linear map of a multivariate
normal is again multivariate normal (

6.8 The multivariate normal, in brief). So\mathbf{b} \sim N\!\big(\boldsymbol{\beta},\ \sigma^2 (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\big) .

In words: under normal errors each coefficient is normally distributed around its
true value, and this is what powers the t tests and confidence intervals of Chapter
8. 

Figure 8 confirms the covariance by simulation: refitting many
datasets scatters the two Dwaine slopes into a tilted ellipse that matches
\sigma^2 (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}.



Figure 8:Simulating 4000 refits scatters the two Dwaine slope estimates into a tilted ellipse. The downward tilt is a negative covariance: the two slope estimates trade off, because the predictors are correlated. The red theoretical ellipse from sigma-squared times the inverse cross-product matrix matches the simulated cloud.

Example 7.4: Standard errors from the covariance matrix

Question. How precisely are the three Dwaine coefficients estimated, and do the
matrix-formula standard errors match what lm and statsmodels print?

Intuition. Multiply (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1} by \mathrm{MSE} and take the
square roots of the diagonal. (We compute \mathrm{MSE} itself in 

7.5 Estimating the error variance; here
we use it.)

Formula. s^2\{\mathbf{b}\} = \mathrm{MSE}\,(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}, and
s\{b_j\} = \sqrt{[\,s^2\{\mathbf{b}\}\,]_{jj}}.

Computation.SSE <- sum(e^2)
MSE <- SSE / (n - p)
cov_b <- MSE * XtX_inv
se_b <- sqrt(diag(cov_b))
round(se_b, 4)[1] 60.0170  0.2118  4.0640SSE = np.sum(e ** 2)
MSE = SSE / (n - p)
cov_b = MSE * np.linalg.inv(XtX)
se_b = np.sqrt(np.diag(cov_b))
print(np.round(se_b, 4))[60.017   0.2118  4.064 ]

The built-in summaries print the same standard errors in their “Std. Error” column.summary(lm(sales ~ targtpop + dispoinc, data = dwaine))$coefficients             Estimate Std. Error   t value     Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept) -68.85707 60.0169532 -1.147294 2.662817e-01
targtpop      1.45456  0.2117817  6.868201 2.001691e-06
dispoinc      9.36550  4.0639581  2.304527 3.332136e-02

Interpretation. The standard errors are 60.02, 0.212, and 4.064 in the
coefficients’ own units. The population slope 1.4546 has a standard error of only
0.212, so it is pinned down tightly (its t value near 6.9 is far from zero); the
income slope 9.37 with standard error 4.06 is less certain; the intercept is
loose, as intercepts far from the data usually are. Every number matches the built-in
output to all printed digits, because the built-in output is computing this exact
matrix formula.

Key idea

The mean E\{\mathbf{b}\} = \boldsymbol{\beta}, the covariance
\sigma^2(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}, and the Gauss-Markov optimality all hold with no
normality assumption: mean-zero, equal-variance, uncorrelated errors are enough.
Normality is a separate, stronger claim. It buys the exact normal sampling distribution
of \mathbf{b}, and with it the t and F tests of Chapter 8.

The coefficients describe the plane; the model still has one more unknown, the error
variance \sigma^2, which sets how far the data scatter off the plane. As in Chapter
2 we estimate it from the residuals, but now we must find the right divisor for a
model with p parameters instead of 2. The answer is n - p, and the reason is
the trace we computed in 

7.3 The hat matrix: fitting p coefficients uses up p
directions of the data, leaving n - p directions for the residual to live in.


Figure 9 keeps the bookkeeping in view: the n directions of the data
split into a fit part and a residual part, and we average the squared residual over
its own n - p directions, not all n.



Figure 9:The n directions of the data split into p directions spent fitting the plane and n minus p left for the residual. Dividing SSE by n minus p, not by n, averages over exactly the directions the residual is free to fill, which is what makes MSE unbiased.

The error sum of squares and the estimator of \sigma^2 are\mathrm{SSE} = \mathbf{e}'\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{Y}'(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})\mathbf{Y}, \qquad
s^2 = \mathrm{MSE} = \frac{\mathrm{SSE}}{n - p}, \qquad s = \sqrt{\mathrm{MSE}} .

\mathrm{SSE} is the squared length of the residual vector, the variation the model did not explain.

\mathrm{MSE} divides \mathrm{SSE} by its degrees of freedom n - p, and estimates \sigma^2.

s is the estimated error standard deviation, in the units of Y.

hierarchy.lvl3: Derivation (why the divisor is n - p)
Derivation (why the divisor is n - p)

records.content:
Theorem 7.9: Unbiasedness of MSE

Under the general linear model with
\operatorname{Cov}\{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\} = \sigma^2\mathbf{I} and \mathbf{X}
of full column rank,E\{\mathrm{SSE}\} = (n - p)\sigma^2, \qquad \text{so} \qquad E\{\mathrm{MSE}\} = \sigma^2 .

The divisor n - p, and no other, makes \mathrm{MSE} = \mathrm{SSE}/(n - p) an
unbiased estimator of the error variance \sigma^2.

We need one fact about the expected value of a quadratic form. It is provable at our
level, so we prove it, then apply it.

Lemma (expectation of a quadratic form). For any random vector \mathbf{Y} with
mean \boldsymbol{\mu} and covariance \boldsymbol{\Sigma}, and any fixed symmetric
matrix \mathbf{A},E\{\mathbf{Y}'\mathbf{A}\mathbf{Y}\} = \operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}) + \boldsymbol{\mu}'\mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\mu} .

Proof. The scalar \mathbf{Y}'\mathbf{A}\mathbf{Y} equals its own trace, and the
trace is cyclic, so \mathbf{Y}'\mathbf{A}\mathbf{Y} = \operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{Y}\mathbf{Y}').
Taking expectations and using E\{\mathbf{Y}\mathbf{Y}'\} = \boldsymbol{\Sigma} + \boldsymbol{\mu}\boldsymbol{\mu}' (

6.7 Random vectors, expectation, and covariance matrices),E\{\mathbf{Y}'\mathbf{A}\mathbf{Y}\} = \operatorname{tr}\!\big(\mathbf{A}(\boldsymbol{\Sigma} + \boldsymbol{\mu}\boldsymbol{\mu}')\big)
= \operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}) + \operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\mu}\boldsymbol{\mu}')
= \operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}) + \boldsymbol{\mu}'\mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\mu} ,

since \operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\mu}\boldsymbol{\mu}') = \boldsymbol{\mu}'\mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\mu} is a scalar. \blacksquare

Proof of Theorem 7.9. Apply the lemma with
\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}, \boldsymbol{\mu} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta},
and \boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \sigma^2\mathbf{I}. The two pieces are:

Trace piece. \operatorname{tr}\!\big((\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})\sigma^2\mathbf{I}\big) = \sigma^2 \operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}) = \sigma^2 (n - p), using the trace from 

7.3 The hat matrix.

Mean piece. The mean term vanishes, because (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{X} - \mathbf{H}\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{X} - \mathbf{X} = \mathbf{0} (the hat matrix leaves the columns of \mathbf{X} unchanged, since they already lie in the column space). Therefore\boldsymbol{\mu}'(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})\boldsymbol{\mu} = \boldsymbol{\beta}'\mathbf{X}'(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} = \boldsymbol{\beta}'\mathbf{0}\,\boldsymbol{\beta} = 0 .

Combining, E\{\mathrm{SSE}\} = \sigma^2(n - p) + 0 = (n - p)\sigma^2, soE\{\mathrm{MSE}\} = \frac{E\{\mathrm{SSE}\}}{n - p} = \sigma^2 .

The divisor n - p, and no other, makes \mathrm{MSE} unbiased. \blacksquare

This is the Chapter 2 result E\{\mathrm{MSE}\} = \sigma^2 generalized: there p = 2
gave the divisor n - 2; here any model divides by n minus the number of
parameters. 

Figure 10 shows what goes wrong with the naive divisor n:
dividing by n instead of n - p biases the estimate low, and the gap is largest
when p is a big fraction of n.



Figure 10:Dividing SSE by n - p (blue) centers the variance estimate on the truth; dividing by n (orange) pulls it systematically low. The bias is the price of estimating p coefficients before measuring the residuals.

Example 7.5: The error variance for Dwaine, and Toluca as a check

Question. What is \mathrm{MSE} for the Dwaine model, and does the matrix machinery
reproduce the Toluca \mathrm{MSE} from Chapter 2 exactly?

Intuition. For Dwaine, sum the squared residuals and divide by n - p = 18. Then
run the whole matrix pipeline on Toluca (p = 2) and confirm every number matches


2.2 Least squares from first principles.

Formula. \mathrm{MSE} = \mathrm{SSE}/(n - p); for Toluca n = 25, p = 2.

Computation.c(n = n, p = p, SSE = round(SSE, 3), MSE = round(MSE, 4), s = round(sqrt(MSE), 4))        n         p       SSE       MSE         s
  21.0000    3.0000 2180.9270  121.1626   11.0074print(n, p, round(SSE, 3), round(MSE, 4), round(np.sqrt(MSE), 4))21 3 2180.927 121.1626 11.0074

Now the Toluca refit through the identical matrix formulas:toluca <- read.csv("data/toluca.csv")
Xt <- cbind(1, toluca$lotsize)
Yt <- toluca$hours
XtXt_inv <- solve(t(Xt) %*% Xt)
bt <- XtXt_inv %*% t(Xt) %*% Yt
et <- Yt - Xt %*% bt
MSEt <- sum(et^2) / (nrow(Xt) - 2)
se_t <- sqrt(diag(MSEt * XtXt_inv))
round(c(b0 = bt[1], b1 = bt[2], MSE = MSEt,
        se_b0 = se_t[1], se_b1 = se_t[2]), 4)       b0        b1       MSE     se_b0     se_b1
  62.3659    3.5702 2383.7156   26.1774    0.3470toluca = pd.read_csv("data/toluca.csv")
Xt = np.column_stack([np.ones(len(toluca)), toluca["lotsize"]])
Yt = toluca["hours"].to_numpy()
XtXt_inv = np.linalg.inv(Xt.T @ Xt)
bt = XtXt_inv @ Xt.T @ Yt
et = Yt - Xt @ bt
MSEt = np.sum(et ** 2) / (len(toluca) - 2)
se_t = np.sqrt(np.diag(MSEt * XtXt_inv))
print(round(bt[0], 4), round(bt[1], 4), round(MSEt, 4),
      round(se_t[0], 4), round(se_t[1], 4))62.3659 3.5702 2383.7156 26.1774 0.347

Interpretation. For Dwaine, \mathrm{MSE} = 121.16 and s = 11.01 thousand
dollars: even knowing a city’s population and income, the model’s sales prediction is
typically off by about $11{,}000. For Toluca the matrix pipeline returns
b_0 = 62.3659, b_1 = 3.5702, \mathrm{MSE} = 2383.72, s\{b_0\} = 26.1774, and
s\{b_1\} = 0.3470, matching 

2.2 Least squares from first principles (and 

2.5 Sampling behavior and the Gauss-Markov theorem for the
standard errors) to every digit shown. The two-parameter machinery of Chapter 2 and
the general machinery here are the same method; 

7.7 Simple regression as a special case shows the algebra
of why.

Try it 7.3

The Dwaine model has n = 21, p = 3, \mathrm{SSE} = 2180.93. A classmate reports
s^2 = 2180.93 / 21 = 103.9. Which quantity did they compute, is it too large or too
small as an estimate of \sigma^2, and what is the correct \mathrm{MSE}?

Solution

They divided by n instead of n - p, which is the maximum-likelihood variance
estimate, biased low because it ignores the three parameters spent on the fit. The
correct estimate is \mathrm{MSE} = 2180.93 / (21 - 3) = 2180.93 / 18 = 121.16. The
classmate’s 103.9 understates \sigma^2; it matches the biased mean in


Figure 10.

We can also read off how well the plane fits overall. The total variation
\mathrm{SSTO} = \sum (Y_i - \bar{Y})^2 splits into the part the model explains and
the part it does not, and R^2 = 1 - \mathrm{SSE}/\mathrm{SSTO} reports the explained
fraction, exactly as in 

3.6 The analysis of variance approach but now for many predictors.SSTO <- sum((Y - mean(Y))^2)
SSR <- SSTO - SSE
c(SSTO = round(SSTO, 2), SSR = round(SSR, 2), SSE = round(SSE, 2),
  R2 = round(1 - SSE / SSTO, 4))      SSTO        SSR        SSE         R2
26196.2100 24015.2800  2180.9300     0.9167SSTO = np.sum((Y - Y.mean()) ** 2)
SSR = SSTO - SSE
print(round(SSTO, 2), round(SSR, 2), round(SSE, 2), round(1 - SSE / SSTO, 4))26196.21 24015.28 2180.93 0.9167

The two predictors together explain about 91.7\% of the variation in Dwaine sales.
Chapter 8 unpacks this number, warns about how it can only rise when predictors are
added, and builds the general linear test on top of the \mathrm{SSE} we just
computed (

8.3 Extra sums of squares).

We chose least squares because squared error is a natural penalty, and Chapter 2
rewarded that choice: in simple regression, no other linear unbiased estimator beats
b_1. The same reward holds for the full vector, and the matrix proof is barely
longer than the scalar one. The claim is that any competitor built as a fixed linear
combination of the responses, and required to be right on average, must have a
covariance matrix at least as large as \mathbf{b}'s. No normality is needed; the
mean-zero, constant-variance, uncorrelated error assumptions are enough.


Figure 11 shows the payoff on Toluca: least squares and a competing
unbiased estimator are both centered on the truth, but least squares scatters far
less.



Figure 11:Both the least-squares slope (blue, narrow) and a two-point competitor (orange, wide) are unbiased, centered on the true slope. Least squares has the smaller spread, exactly what the Gauss-Markov theorem guarantees.

Theorem 7.10: Gauss-Markov theorem

Under the model \mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}
with E\{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\} = \mathbf{0} and
\operatorname{Cov}\{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\} = \sigma^2\mathbf{I}, and \mathbf{X}
of full column rank, the least-squares estimator
\mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y} is the best linear
unbiased estimator of \boldsymbol{\beta}: for any fixed vector \mathbf{c}, the
estimator \mathbf{c}'\mathbf{b} has the smallest variance among all estimators that
are linear in \mathbf{Y} and unbiased for \mathbf{c}'\boldsymbol{\beta}.

Proof. Let \mathbf{b}^{\ast} = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{Y} be any linear estimator
with a fixed p \times n matrix \mathbf{C}. Its mean is
E\{\mathbf{C}\mathbf{Y}\} = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}. For
\mathbf{b}^{\ast} to be unbiased for \boldsymbol{\beta} for every possible
\boldsymbol{\beta}, we need\mathbf{C}\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{I}_p .

Write \mathbf{C} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}' + \mathbf{D}, defining
\mathbf{D} as the difference from the least-squares matrix. The unbiasedness
condition \mathbf{C}\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{I} becomes\mathbf{I} = \big[(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}' + \mathbf{D}\big]\mathbf{X}
= \mathbf{I} + \mathbf{D}\mathbf{X}, \qquad \text{so} \qquad \mathbf{D}\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{0} .

Now compute the covariance. Since \operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{Y}\} = \sigma^2\mathbf{I},
\operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{b}^{\ast}\} = \sigma^2\mathbf{C}\mathbf{C}'. Expand
\mathbf{C}\mathbf{C}' with \mathbf{C} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}' + \mathbf{D}.
The two cross terms vanish because \mathbf{D}\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{0} (and its
transpose \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{D}' = \mathbf{0}):(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{D}' = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}(\mathbf{D}\mathbf{X})' = \mathbf{0} .

What survives is\mathbf{C}\mathbf{C}' = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1} + \mathbf{D}\mathbf{D}'
= (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1} + \mathbf{D}\mathbf{D}' .

Therefore\operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{b}^{\ast}\} = \sigma^2 (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1} + \sigma^2\mathbf{D}\mathbf{D}'
= \operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{b}\} + \sigma^2\mathbf{D}\mathbf{D}' .

For any fixed \mathbf{c}, the variance of the scalar \mathbf{c}'\mathbf{b}^{\ast} is\operatorname{Var}\{\mathbf{c}'\mathbf{b}^{\ast}\} = \mathbf{c}'\operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{b}\}\mathbf{c} + \sigma^2 \mathbf{c}'\mathbf{D}\mathbf{D}'\mathbf{c}
= \operatorname{Var}\{\mathbf{c}'\mathbf{b}\} + \sigma^2\|\mathbf{D}'\mathbf{c}\|^2 .

The added term \sigma^2\|\mathbf{D}'\mathbf{c}\|^2 \ge 0 is a squared length, so
every linear unbiased competitor has variance at least that of \mathbf{c}'\mathbf{b},
with equality only when \mathbf{D}'\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{0}; taking \mathbf{c} to
range over the coordinate vectors forces \mathbf{D} = \mathbf{0}, that is, the
competitor is \mathbf{b} itself. Least squares is BLUE. \blacksquare

Setting \mathbf{X} = [\mathbf{1}, \mathbf{x}] and \mathbf{c} = (0, 1)' recovers the
Chapter 2 statement that b_1 is BLUE (

2.5 Sampling behavior and the Gauss-Markov theorem); the matrix proof did
every coefficient, and every linear combination of coefficients, at once.

Durable skill: Prove the general case, use it forever

The scalar Gauss-Markov proof in Chapter 2 took a page and covered one estimator. The
matrix proof here is about the same length and covers every linear regression you will
ever fit, with any number of predictors, plus every linear combination of coefficients
(predictions included). That is the payoff of the right notation: a single argument at
the general level retires an unbounded list of special cases. When you find yourself
proving the same kind of thing twice, look for the notation that lets you prove it
once. To practice, take a result you know for two predictors and rewrite its proof so
that nothing in it mentions the number two.

records.content:
Everything in Chapters 2 and 3 should drop out of the matrix formulas when there is
one predictor, and it does. Seeing it happen once turns the general formulas from
abstractions into trusted tools: the same machine that fit Dwaine’s plane fits
Toluca’s line, and returns the exact numbers you already know. 

Figure 12
is the whole idea in one picture: one formula, two jobs.



Figure 12:One matrix formula does two jobs. Set p equals 2 and it collapses into the exact Chapter 2 scalar formulas for the Toluca line; set p equals 3 and it fits the Dwaine plane. The general model does not replace simple regression, it contains it.

7.7 Simple regression as a special case

records.content:
Derivation (the p = 2 case). With one predictor, the design matrix is
\mathbf{X} = [\mathbf{1}, \mathbf{x}], so\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X} = \begin{pmatrix} n & \sum X_i \\ \sum X_i & \sum X_i^2 \end{pmatrix}, \qquad
\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y} = \begin{pmatrix} \sum Y_i \\ \sum X_i Y_i \end{pmatrix} .

where S_{xx} = \sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2 and S_{xy} = \sum (X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})
are the Chapter 2 sums of squares and cross-products, and \bar{X}, \bar{Y} are the
sample means. The inverse of a 2 \times 2 matrix divides by its determinant
\det(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}) = n\sum X_i^2 - (\sum X_i)^2 = n\,S_{xx} (

6.4 The inverse and the normal equations),(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1} = \frac{1}{n\,S_{xx}}\begin{pmatrix} \sum X_i^2 & -\sum X_i \\ -\sum X_i & n \end{pmatrix} .

Multiplying by \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y} and simplifying (each entry collapses using
\sum X_i = n\bar{X} and the definitions of S_{xx}, S_{xy}) gives\mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y} = \begin{pmatrix} \bar{Y} - b_1\bar{X} \\ S_{xy}/S_{xx} \end{pmatrix} ,

which are exactly the Chapter 2 estimators b_0 = \bar{Y} - b_1\bar{X} and
b_1 = S_{xy}/S_{xx}. The covariance matrix reduces the same way: the bottom-right
entry of \sigma^2(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1} is \sigma^2 n / (n S_{xx}) = \sigma^2/S_{xx},
matching \operatorname{Var}\{b_1\}, and the top-left entry is
\sigma^2 \sum X_i^2/(n S_{xx}) = \sigma^2(1/n + \bar{X}^2/S_{xx}), matching
\operatorname{Var}\{b_0\} from 

2.5 Sampling behavior and the Gauss-Markov theorem. \blacksquare

Example 7.5 already confirmed this numerically: the matrix pipeline returned Toluca’s
b_0 = 62.3659, b_1 = 3.5702, and standard errors 26.1774 and 0.3470, the same
figures Chapter 2 produced from the scalar formulas. The general linear model does not
replace simple regression; it contains it.

This chapter turned Chapter 6’s matrix arithmetic into a full multiple regression. One
equation, \mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon},
carries any number of predictors. Least squares projects \mathbf{Y} onto the column
space of \mathbf{X}, giving \mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}
by calculus and by geometry alike. The hat matrix \mathbf{H} packages that projection,
and its two properties, being a projection (symmetric and idempotent) with trace p,
drive the sampling distribution of \mathbf{b}, the n - p divisor in \mathrm{MSE},
and the Gauss-Markov optimality. Every Chapter 2 formula returns as the p = 2 special
case.

Key results at a glance.

Result

Statement or formula

Valid when

Least-squares estimator (Theorem 7.3)

\mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y} minimizes Q(\mathbf{b})

\mathbf{X} full column rank

Normal equations (Definition 7.4)

\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}\mathbf{b} = \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}, equivalently \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{0}

any least-squares fit

Hat matrix properties (Theorem 7.7)

\mathbf{H} symmetric, idempotent, \operatorname{tr}\mathbf{H} = p, \operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{H}) = n-p

\mathbf{X} full column rank

Mean and covariance of \mathbf{b} (Theorem 7.8)

E\{\mathbf{b}\} = \boldsymbol{\beta}, \operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{b}\} = \sigma^2(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}

E\{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\}=\mathbf{0}, \operatorname{Cov}\{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\}=\sigma^2\mathbf{I}

Sampling distribution (Theorem 7.8)

\mathbf{b} \sim N(\boldsymbol{\beta}, \sigma^2(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1})

errors also normal

Unbiasedness of MSE (Theorem 7.9)

E\{\mathrm{SSE}\} = (n-p)\sigma^2, so E\{\mathrm{MSE}\} = \sigma^2

\operatorname{Cov}\{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\}=\sigma^2\mathbf{I}

Gauss-Markov (Theorem 7.10)

\mathbf{b} is the best linear unbiased estimator of \boldsymbol{\beta}

E\{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\}=\mathbf{0}, \operatorname{Cov}\{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\}=\sigma^2\mathbf{I}; no normality needed

Coefficient of determination

R^2 = 1 - \mathrm{SSE}/\mathrm{SSTO} (Dwaine 0.9167)

descriptive, any fit

Key terms. general linear model, design matrix, normal equations,
least-squares estimator, column space, hat matrix, idempotent matrix,
full column rank, error degrees of freedom, best linear unbiased estimator (BLUE).

You should now be able to.

Write any linear regression as \mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon} and identify the design matrix, coefficient vector, and error vector.

Derive \mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y} two ways: by calculus and by orthogonal projection.

Interpret least squares geometrically as projecting \mathbf{Y} onto the column space of \mathbf{X}, with the residual perpendicular to it.

Prove that the hat matrix \mathbf{H} is symmetric and idempotent with trace p, and use it to write fitted values and residuals.

Derive the mean and covariance of \mathbf{b} and state its sampling distribution under normal errors.

Prove that s^2 = \mathrm{MSE} = \mathrm{SSE}/(n-p) is unbiased for \sigma^2 using the trace of \mathbf{I}-\mathbf{H}.

State and prove the Gauss-Markov theorem in matrix form: \mathbf{b} is the best linear unbiased estimator.

Recover every simple-regression formula from Chapter 2 as the special case p = 2.

Where this fits. This chapter is the general engine for the FIT stage of the
course workflow (

The modeling workflow): it estimates
the coefficients and the error variance for a model of any size, replacing the
predictor-by-predictor algebra of Chapters 2 and 3 with one formula that a computer
can run for ten predictors as easily as one. It also arms the CHECK stage, because the
hat matrix \mathbf{H} is the object Chapter 9 (

9. Model diagnostics) turns into the leverage values
and influence measures, and the USE stage, because
\operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{b}\} is what Chapter 8 turns into tests and intervals.
Chapter 8 takes the Dwaine fit built here, interprets each coefficient with care, and
develops the extra-sums-of-squares and general-linear-test machinery (

8.3 Extra sums of squares)
that decides which predictors a model needs.

Q1. Why does the design matrix need a column of ones? The column of ones lets the
intercept \beta_0 be handled like any other coefficient: its “predictor value” is
1 for every observation, so \beta_0 \cdot 1 is the same baseline for every row.
Without it the fitted plane would be forced through the origin. The first normal
equation, coming from that column, is exactly \sum e_i = 0.

Q2. What does full column rank mean, and what breaks without it? Full column rank
means no predictor column is a linear combination of the others (including the ones
column). If one is, say a predictor recorded in both meters and feet, then
\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X} is singular and (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1} does not exist,
so there is no unique \mathbf{b}: infinitely many coefficient vectors fit equally
well. Software then drops a column or returns an error. Near-violations (highly
correlated predictors) are multicollinearity, the subject of Chapter 12.

Q3. Is the hat matrix worth computing for real data? As a full n \times n matrix,
usually not; software gets fitted values and residuals more cheaply. But its diagonal
entries h_{ii}, the leverage values, are computed and used constantly in
diagnostics, and its algebra (symmetric, idempotent, trace p) is what makes the
theory work. Think of \mathbf{H} as a proof tool and a source of the leverages, not
a thing you print.

Q4. Why is the divisor n - p and not n - 1 or n? Each parameter you estimate
uses one degree of freedom before the residuals are measured. A sample variance
estimates one quantity (the mean) and divides by n - 1; a regression with p
parameters divides by n - p. The trace derivation in 

7.5 Estimating the error variance shows
E\{\mathrm{SSE}\} = (n - p)\sigma^2 exactly, so n - p is the unique divisor making
\mathrm{MSE} unbiased. Dividing by n (the maximum-likelihood choice) is biased low.

Q5. Do I need normal errors for any of this? No, for most of it. The estimator
\mathbf{b}, its unbiasedness, its covariance \sigma^2(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1},
the unbiasedness of \mathrm{MSE}, and the Gauss-Markov optimality all hold with only
mean-zero, constant-variance, uncorrelated errors. Normality is an extra assumption,
used to get the exact multivariate normal sampling distribution of \mathbf{b} and the
t and F tests of Chapter 8.

Q6. The Dwaine coefficients from the matrix formula and from lm matched exactly.
Is that always guaranteed? Yes, up to tiny floating-point differences. Both compute
the same (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}; lm and statsmodels
use a more numerically stable routine (a QR or SVD decomposition) instead of forming
the inverse explicitly, which matters only for badly conditioned designs. For
well-behaved data like these, every printed digit agrees.

Q7. Why are the two Dwaine slope estimates negatively correlated in


Figure 8? Because the two predictors are themselves positively
correlated across cities. When a sample happens to make the population slope come out
high, the fit compensates by making the income slope come out low to avoid
double-counting the shared signal. That trade-off is exactly the off-diagonal entry of
\sigma^2(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}, and it is why you cannot read one coefficient’s
uncertainty without the whole covariance matrix.

Note

Unless a problem says otherwise, use dwaine.csv (with n = 21, p = 3, fitted
coefficients (-68.86, 1.4546, 9.3655), \mathrm{MSE} = 121.16, R^2 = 0.9167) or
toluca.csv (with n = 25, p = 2). Problems are marked (A) concepts, (B) theory,
or (C) data analysis. Odd-numbered answers appear in Appendix H; full solutions are in
the instructor materials.

(A) Write the Dwaine model in the form \mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}, giving the dimensions of each of the four objects and describing the first column of \mathbf{X}.

(A) Explain in one sentence each what \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X} and \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y} represent, and why the normal equations use them.

(A) State the two error assumptions E\{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\} = \mathbf{0} and \operatorname{Cov}\{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\} = \sigma^2\mathbf{I} in plain words, saying what the diagonal and the off-diagonal of \sigma^2\mathbf{I} each encode.

(A) In the projection picture, what is the column space of \mathbf{X}, where does \hat{\mathbf{Y}} sit, and in what direction does the residual point? Answer without formulas.

(A) Give the three properties of the hat matrix proved in this chapter and say what each one means geometrically.

(A) Why is the divisor in \mathrm{MSE} equal to n - p? Answer by naming what the number p counts and what n - p counts.

(A) A colleague says “with normality the least-squares estimator is BLUE; without it, it is not.” Correct the statement, naming exactly which assumptions the Gauss-Markov theorem uses.

(A) Explain why \mathbf{X}\mathbf{X}' (an n \times n matrix) cannot replace \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X} in the normal equations.

(B) Starting from Q(\mathbf{b}) = (\mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{b})'(\mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{b}), expand it, take the gradient, and derive the normal equations and \mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y} (Theorem 7.3). Justify why the stationary point is a minimum.

(B) Prove the projection identity \|\mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{b}^{\ast}\|^2 = \|\mathbf{e}\|^2 + \|\mathbf{X}(\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{b}^{\ast})\|^2 for any \mathbf{b}^{\ast}, showing the cross term vanishes, and conclude that \mathbf{b} uniquely minimizes SSE.

(B) Prove that \mathbf{H} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}' is symmetric and idempotent (Theorem 7.7). Then prove \mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H} is symmetric and idempotent as well.

(B) Prove \operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{H}) = p using the cyclic property of the trace, and deduce \operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}) = n - p.

(B) Show \mathbf{H}\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{X} and use it to prove (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{0}. Explain in words why the hat matrix leaves the columns of \mathbf{X} unchanged.

(B) Derive E\{\mathbf{b}\} = \boldsymbol{\beta} and \operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{b}\} = \sigma^2(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1} (Theorem 7.8), naming the random-vector facts you use at each step. Then, adding the assumption \boldsymbol{\varepsilon} \sim N(\mathbf{0}, \sigma^2\mathbf{I}), state the full sampling distribution of \mathbf{b} and explain why the mean and covariance did not need normality but this distributional statement does.

(B) Prove the quadratic-form lemma E\{\mathbf{Y}'\mathbf{A}\mathbf{Y}\} = \operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}) + \boldsymbol{\mu}'\mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\mu}, then use it to show E\{\mathrm{SSE}\} = (n - p)\sigma^2 (Theorem 7.9).

(B) State and prove the Gauss-Markov theorem in matrix form (Theorem 7.10). Where in the proof does the assumption \operatorname{Cov}\{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\} = \sigma^2\mathbf{I} enter, and where does full column rank enter?

(B) For the simple-regression design \mathbf{X} = [\mathbf{1}, \mathbf{x}], compute \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}, its determinant, and (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}, and derive b_1 = S_{xy}/S_{xx} and b_0 = \bar{Y} - b_1\bar{X} from the matrix formula.

(B) Using the reduction in problem 17, derive \operatorname{Var}\{b_1\} = \sigma^2/S_{xx} and \operatorname{Var}\{b_0\} = \sigma^2(1/n + \bar{X}^2/S_{xx}) from \sigma^2(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}.

(B) Show that \operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{e}, \hat{\mathbf{Y}}\} = \mathbf{0} under the model, using \hat{\mathbf{Y}} = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{Y}, \mathbf{e} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})\mathbf{Y}, and \mathbf{H}(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}) = \mathbf{0}. Why does this matter for diagnostics?

(B) Prove that adding a predictor (a new column to \mathbf{X}) cannot increase \mathrm{SSE}, using the projection argument that enlarging the column space can only move \hat{\mathbf{Y}} closer to \mathbf{Y}.

(C) Read dwaine.csv, build \mathbf{X} and \mathbf{Y}, and compute \mathbf{b} from (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y} in R or Python. Confirm your answer matches lm / smf.ols.

(C) For the Dwaine fit, compute the residual vector and verify numerically that \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{0} (all three entries) and that \sum \hat{Y}_i = \sum Y_i.

(C) Build the Dwaine hat matrix and verify it is symmetric and idempotent and has trace 3. Then report the five largest diagonal entries h_{ii} and say what a large h_{ii} indicates about that city’s predictors.

(C) Compute \mathrm{SSE}, \mathrm{MSE}, and s for Dwaine two ways: from the residual vector, and from summary / .summary2(). Then compute \mathrm{SSE}/n and report the percentage by which it understates \mathrm{MSE}.

(C) Compute the estimated covariance matrix \mathrm{MSE}\,(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1} for Dwaine, report the three standard errors, and confirm they match the built-in summary. Report the correlation between the two slope estimates from the off-diagonal entry.

(C) Refit Toluca through the matrix formulas and confirm b_0 = 62.3659, b_1 = 3.5702, \mathrm{MSE} = 2383.72, and the two standard errors, matching Chapter 2 to every digit.

(C) Write a simulation (seed 4210) that draws 2000 datasets from the fitted Dwaine model, refits each, and reports the mean of the 2000 estimates of each coefficient (checking unbiasedness) and the standard deviation of the target-population slope (checking \operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{b}\}).

(C) Regress Dwaine sales on targtpop alone, then on targtpop and dispoinc together, and confirm numerically that adding dispoinc lowers \mathrm{SSE}. Relate the drop to the projection argument in problem 20.

Note

These five problems are written in the style of the course exams: each one asks you
to explain in full sentences, not just to produce a number. Work each answer
before opening its model answer. The model answers show the depth that earns full
marks, and each ends with a note on what a weak answer leaves out. Datasets are
dwaine.csv (n = 21, p = 3) and toluca.csv (n = 25, p = 2), the same fits
used throughout the chapter.

EP 7.1 (interpret output in context). A colleague fits the Dwaine model in R and
reads off the coefficient table.            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept) -68.8571    60.0170 -1.1473   0.2663
targtpop      1.4546     0.2118  6.8682   0.0000
dispoinc      9.3655     4.0640  2.3045   0.0333

Interpret the targtpop coefficient and its t value in the context of studio sales,
being explicit about what is held fixed. Then evaluate the colleague’s conclusion:
“the intercept has p = 0.27, so it is not significantly different from zero, which
means the model is misspecified and we should drop the intercept.” Say whether the
conclusion is right and why.

Model answer

Holding disposable income fixed, each additional thousand young residents in a city is
associated with about 1.4546 thousand dollars, roughly $1{,}455, of additional
predicted annual sales. The t value of 6.87 is the estimate divided by its standard
error (1.4546 / 0.2118); it is far from zero and its p-value is essentially zero, so
the target-population slope is clearly distinguishable from zero at any usual level, and
target population carries real predictive signal once income is already in the model.

The colleague’s conclusion is wrong on both counts. The intercept’s insignificance means
only that the data cannot pin down the fitted plane’s height at the point where both
predictors equal zero, which is far outside the range of any real city (populations near
62 thousand and incomes near 17 thousand dollars in these data), so a loose intercept
there is expected and harmless. An insignificant intercept is not evidence of
misspecification, and dropping the intercept is a strong, usually wrong, modeling choice:
it forces the fitted plane through the origin, distorts every slope, and breaks the
identity \sum e_i = 0 that the column of ones guarantees. Keep the intercept.

A weak answer reports the slope as a raw number without the “holding income fixed”
comparison or the units, and treats the intercept’s large p-value as a defect to fix
rather than a statement about an extrapolated region no city occupies.

EP 7.2 (explain why, and what would change if). Explain in full sentences why the
divisor in \mathrm{MSE} = \mathrm{SSE}/(n - p) is n - p and not n. Then answer a
“what would change if”: suppose an analyst kept adding predictors to the Dwaine data
until the model had p = 21 parameters, one for every observation. Describe what happens
to \mathrm{SSE}, to the divisor, and to \mathrm{MSE}, and explain why a fit with
\mathrm{SSE} = 0 is a warning rather than a success.

Model answer

The divisor is n - p because fitting p coefficients uses up p of the n
independent directions in the data before the residuals are measured. Formally,
\mathrm{SSE} = \mathbf{Y}'(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})\mathbf{Y} and
\operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}) = n - p, so E\{\mathrm{SSE}\} = (n - p)\sigma^2;
dividing by n - p, and no other number, makes \mathrm{MSE} an unbiased estimator of
\sigma^2. Dividing by n would systematically underestimate the error variance.

If p = 21 = n, the column space of \mathbf{X} fills the whole 21-dimensional
response space, so the projection \hat{\mathbf{Y}} = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{Y} equals
\mathbf{Y} exactly and every residual is zero, giving \mathrm{SSE} = 0. The divisor
n - p = 21 - 21 = 0, so \mathrm{MSE} = 0/0 is undefined: there are no error degrees
of freedom left. A zero \mathrm{SSE} does not mean the model is perfect; it means the
model has enough parameters to interpolate the data exactly, so it has learned the noise
along with the signal and can say nothing about \sigma^2 or about a new city. Perfect
in-sample fit with zero degrees of freedom is the signature of overfitting.

A weak answer says “n - p adjusts for the parameters” without connecting it to
unbiasedness or the trace, and treats \mathrm{SSE} = 0 as a good outcome instead of
recognizing that zero error degrees of freedom leave nothing to estimate the variance
with.

EP 7.3 (a student claims X, evaluate). A student writes: “Adding a predictor can
never increase \mathrm{SSE}, and it always raises R^2. So the best strategy is to
keep adding predictors until R^2 is as close to 1 as possible.” Using the projection
picture of least squares, evaluate the two factual claims and the strategy they support.

Model answer

The two factual claims are true. Adding a column to \mathbf{X} enlarges its column
space, and projecting \mathbf{Y} onto a larger subspace can only bring the foot of the
perpendicular \hat{\mathbf{Y}} closer to \mathbf{Y}, never farther, so
\mathrm{SSE} = \|\mathbf{Y} - \hat{\mathbf{Y}}\|^2 cannot rise; the old fit is still
available as a special case where the new coefficient is zero. Because
R^2 = 1 - \mathrm{SSE}/\mathrm{SSTO} and \mathrm{SSTO} is fixed, a non-increasing
\mathrm{SSE} means a non-decreasing R^2.

The strategy, however, is wrong. Since R^2 rises even for predictors that are pure
noise, a high R^2 bought by piling on predictors is not evidence of a good model; in
the limit p = n the fit interpolates the data with R^2 = 1 and \mathrm{SSE} = 0
while explaining nothing out of sample. Each added predictor also spends a degree of
freedom, shrinking n - p and inflating the variances \sigma^2(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}
of the coefficients, especially when the new predictor is correlated with those already
present. The right question is not whether \mathrm{SSE} dropped but whether it dropped
by more than noise would predict, which is exactly what the general linear test of
Chapter 8 measures.

A weak answer either wrongly disputes the two true facts or accepts the strategy, missing
that a guaranteed rise in R^2 carries no information about whether a predictor belongs
in the model.

EP 7.4 (interpret computed output in context). The leverage values h_{ii} are the
diagonal entries of the hat matrix \mathbf{H} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'
for the Dwaine fit. The six largest, and a check on their sum, are below.row 20  targtpop=  82.7  dispoinc= 19.1  h_ii=0.2788
row 13  targtpop=  88.4  dispoinc= 17.4  h_ii=0.2390
row 15  targtpop=  52.5  dispoinc= 17.8  h_ii=0.2095
row  3  targtpop=  91.3  dispoinc= 18.2  h_ii=0.1737
row  5  targtpop=  46.9  dispoinc= 17.3  h_ii=0.1620
row 19  targtpop=  89.6  dispoinc= 18.1  h_ii=0.1611
sum(h_ii) = 3.0000     mean(targtpop)=62.02  mean(dispoinc)=17.14

Explain what a large h_{ii} says about a city, and interpret why row 20 has the largest
leverage value even though its target population (82.7) is not the largest in the data.
Then explain why the leverage values sum to exactly 3, and use the rule-of-thumb cutoff
2p/n = 0.286 to say whether any Dwaine city should be called a high-leverage point.

Model answer

A large h_{ii} means the city sits far from the center of the predictor cloud, so its
predictor values are unusual and its own response pulls its fitted value strongly toward
itself. Row 20 has the largest leverage value not because of any single coordinate but
because of the combination: its disposable income 19.1 is the highest in the data,
well above the mean of 17.14, and its target population 82.7 is also well above the
mean of 62.02, so it sits in a far corner of the two-dimensional predictor space.
The leverage value measures distance from the joint center, so a city can have a higher
leverage value than one with a larger population alone if it is more extreme on both predictors
together.

The leverage values sum to 3 because \sum_i h_{ii} = \operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{H}) = p = 3,
proved in 

7.3 The hat matrix: the trace of the projection matrix counts the parameters, so
the total of the leverage values the fit distributes across cities is fixed at p regardless of the
data. Against the cutoff 2p/n = 0.286, the largest leverage value (0.2788) falls just
below it, so by this rule of thumb no Dwaine city is flagged as a high-leverage point;
the design is fairly balanced, with no city dominating the predictor space.

A weak answer reads the leverage value off target population alone, misses that it measures joint
distance from the center of both predictors, and cannot explain the sum-to-p property
as the trace of \mathbf{H}.

EP 7.5 (what would change if). An analyst re-expresses target population in hundreds
of people instead of thousands, multiplying every targtpop value by 10, and refits.
The two coefficient tables are below.original                              rescaled
             Estimate Std. Error       Estimate Std. Error
(Intercept)  -68.8571    60.0170       -68.8571    60.0170
targtpop       1.4546     0.2118        0.1455     0.0212
dispoinc       9.3655     4.0640        9.3655     4.0640
original  MSE=121.16  R2=0.9167     rescaled  MSE=121.16  R2=0.9167

Explain, in full sentences and with reference to the model algebra, why the target-
population coefficient and its standard error both divide by 10 while its t value,
the fitted values, \mathrm{MSE}, and R^2 are all unchanged. What does this tell you
about reading the size of a coefficient as a measure of a predictor’s importance?

Model answer

Rescaling a predictor by a factor of 10 replaces its column \mathbf{x} by
10\mathbf{x}. For the fitted plane to make the same predictions, the coefficient on
that column must divide by 10, since (10\mathbf{x})(\beta/10) = \mathbf{x}\beta: the
product that enters \hat{\mathbf{Y}} is unchanged, so the fitted values, the residuals,
\mathrm{SSE}, \mathrm{MSE}, and R^2 are all identical to the original fit. The
standard error divides by 10 for the same reason the estimate does, because it is
measured in the coefficient’s own units (dollars per hundred people instead of dollars
per thousand people), so the ratio t = b / s\{b\} is dimensionless and does not move;
the evidence that the slope differs from zero is exactly as strong as before.

The lesson is that the raw magnitude of a coefficient is not a measure of a predictor’s
importance, because you can make a coefficient ten times larger or smaller just by
changing the units of its predictor, with no change to the fit or to the strength of the
evidence. To compare importance you must look at scale-free quantities such as the t
value, or standardize the predictors first.

A weak answer states that the numbers change without tying the factor of 10 to the
\mathbf{x}\beta product staying fixed, and fails to draw the conclusion that
coefficient size, being unit-dependent, cannot rank predictors by importance.

Play the Chapter 7 game

Play the Chapter 7 game on your phone or laptop: 10 quick rounds, no setup.


Open the game

It drills the general linear model on the real Dwaine and Toluca data: reading a
coefficient with the other predictor held fixed, ordering the (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}
pipeline, fitting least squares as a projection, spotting the high-leverage city,
matching the hat-matrix objects, and using the n - p divisor. You get the reason
after every answer.

Resumen del capítulo (en español)

Este capítulo presenta el modelo lineal general (general linear model)
\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}, que escribe
cualquier regresión, con uno o con muchos predictores, en una sola forma matricial.
La matriz de diseño (design matrix) \mathbf{X} tiene una primera columna de unos
para el intercepto y una columna por cada predictor; \boldsymbol{\beta} reúne los
parámetros y \boldsymbol{\varepsilon} los errores, con
E\{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\} = \mathbf{0} y
\operatorname{Cov}\{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\} = \sigma^2\mathbf{I}.

El estimador de mínimos cuadrados (least squares) es
\mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}, obtenido de dos
maneras: por cálculo, derivando las ecuaciones normales (normal equations)
\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}\mathbf{b} = \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}; y por proyección
ortogonal (orthogonal projection), donde \hat{\mathbf{Y}} es el punto del espacio
de columnas de \mathbf{X} más cercano a \mathbf{Y} y el residuo
\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{Y} - \hat{\mathbf{Y}} es perpendicular a ese espacio. Para
Dwaine Studios, \mathbf{b} = (-68.86,\ 1.4546,\ 9.3655).

La matriz sombrero (hat matrix) \mathbf{H} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'
cumple \hat{\mathbf{Y}} = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{Y} y es simétrica e idempotente con traza
p. De ahí sale que \operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}) = n - p, el divisor
correcto para estimar la varianza: \mathrm{MSE} = \mathrm{SSE}/(n - p) es
insesgado (unbiased), lo cual se prueba con un argumento de traza. Para Dwaine,
\mathrm{MSE} = 121.16 y R^2 = 0.9167.

El vector \mathbf{b} tiene media \boldsymbol{\beta} y covarianza
\sigma^2(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}; bajo errores normales sigue una distribución
normal multivariante. El teorema de Gauss-Markov (Gauss-Markov theorem) demuestra
que \mathbf{b} es el mejor estimador lineal insesgado, sin necesitar normalidad.
Finalmente, la regresión simple del Capítulo 2 aparece como el caso p = 2: las
fórmulas matriciales reproducen b_0 = 62.3659, b_1 = 3.5702 y
\mathrm{MSE} = 2383.72 de la Toluca Company, dígito por dígito.

records.content:
Read in English

Volvemos a las ciudades de Dwaine Studios de los Capítulos 6 y 7 (

7.1 El modelo y los mínimos cuadrados en forma matricial), donde una
cadena de estudios de retratos en pueblos de tamaño medio quiere decidir, para una ciudad en la que
nunca ha entrado, cuánto venderá un nuevo estudio. Dos números registrados sobre cada una de sus 21
ciudades explican de forma plausible esa respuesta: la población objetivo targtpop (miles de
personas menores de 16 años, el conjunto de posibles clientes de retratos familiares) y el ingreso
disponible dispoinc (miles de dólares por persona, cuánto tienen esas familias para gastar), que
juntos predicen las ventas anuales sales (miles de dólares).

En el Capítulo 6 organizamos estos datos en matrices, y en el Capítulo 7 resolvimos las ecuaciones
normales \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}\mathbf{b} = \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y} para obtener los coeficientes de
mínimos cuadrados \mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y} = (-68.86,\ 1.45,\ 9.37);
véase 

7.1 El modelo y los mínimos cuadrados en forma matricial. Esa fue la etapa de AJUSTE. Este capítulo trata de lo que viene después:
leer esos tres números con honestidad, decidir cuáles predictores se ganan su lugar, y conocer las
trampas que esperan a cualquiera que reporte un coeficiente sin pensar. La 

Figure 1
muestra la materia prima, las ventas contra cada predictor uno a la vez.



Figure 1:Las ventas de Dwaine contra cada predictor por separado. Ambas pendientes son positivas: una mayor población objetivo y un mayor ingreso van cada uno con mayores ventas. El trabajo del capítulo es desenredar estas dos historias que se solapan en un solo modelo que sostiene ambas a la vez.

Ambos cuadros suben, así que ambos predictores parecen útiles. Pero los dos predictores también se
mueven juntos (las ciudades más ricas tienden a tener perfiles de edad distintos), y ese solapamiento
es toda la dificultad de la regresión múltiple. Un coeficiente en una regresión múltiple no es la
pendiente que verías en un diagrama de dispersión de un solo predictor. Responde a una pregunta más
cuidadosa, y este capítulo trata de plantear esa pregunta correctamente, probar si un predictor
pertenece al modelo, y ver la respuesta en una imagen.

Esta lección de un vistazo

Qué estamos haciendo: Interpretar la regresión múltiple ajustada de Dwaine: leer cada coeficiente como una pendiente parcial, probar si un predictor pertenece al modelo con las sumas de cuadrados adicionales y la prueba lineal general, y ver el efecto parcial en un gráfico de variable agregada.

Por qué lo hacemos: Un coeficiente de regresión múltiple no es la pendiente de un diagrama de dispersión de un solo predictor; los predictores correlacionados hacen sutil el “mantener fijos los demás”, y necesitas una forma con principios de decidir cuáles predictores se ganan su lugar.

Objetivo principal: Interpretar correctamente una pendiente parcial, calcular y leer las sumas de cuadrados adicionales, y aplicar la prueba lineal general a un coeficiente o a un subconjunto.

Qué cambió respecto a los capítulos anteriores: El Capítulo 7 produjo el ajuste \mathbf{b} = (-68.86,\ 1.45,\ 9.37) (

7.1 El modelo y los mínimos cuadrados en forma matricial); este capítulo es la etapa de USO que convierte esos tres números en afirmaciones defendibles, y construye la maquinaria de sumas de cuadrados adicionales y de la prueba lineal general (

8.4 La prueba lineal general) que reutilizan los Capítulos 11 y 12.

Objetivos de aprendizaje

Al terminar este capítulo podrás:

Interpretar un coeficiente de regresión múltiple como una pendiente parcial, indicando las unidades y la frase exacta “manteniendo fijos los demás predictores”.

Explicar los límites de la lectura “manteniendo fijos los demás” cuando los predictores están correlacionados, y diagnosticar cuándo la frase describe una región sin datos.

Definir y calcular las sumas de cuadrados adicionales \mathrm{SSR}(X_k \mid \text{others}) y leer una tabla ANOVA secuencial.

Derivar y aplicar la prueba lineal general (F del modelo completo contra el reducido) para un coeficiente o un subconjunto de coeficientes, y mostrar que se reduce a t^2 para un solo coeficiente.

Construir y leer un gráfico de variable agregada, y demostrar que su pendiente es igual al coeficiente de regresión múltiple.

Calcular y contrastar R^2, R^2 ajustado y coeficientes estandarizados, y explicar por qué R^2 nunca baja cuando se agrega un predictor.

Reconocer la extrapolación oculta en varias dimensiones y la forma en que una variable oculta puede invertir el signo de un coeficiente.
