12.1 Multicollinearity and the variance inflation factor

records.content:
R’s car package computes every VIF at once. The formula above lets you check any one of
them by hand from a single auxiliary regression.

Example 12.2: Variance inflation factors for the body-fat model

Question. Which predictors are the worst offenders, and can we reproduce one VIF from its
auxiliary regression?

Intuition. A VIF near 1 is clean; a VIF above 10 means that predictor is more than 90
percent explained by the others. Compute all thirteen, then rebuild the weight VIF by hand.

Formula. \mathrm{VIF}_k = 1/(1 - R_k^2), with R_k^2 from regressing X_k on the rest.

Computation.library(car)
round(vif(full), 2)    age  weight  height    neck   chest   abdom     hip   thigh    knee   ankle
   2.25   33.51    1.67    4.32    9.46   11.77   14.80    7.78    4.61    1.91
 biceps forearm   wrist
   3.62    2.19    3.38aux <- lm(weight ~ age + height + neck + chest + abdom + hip + thigh +
            knee + ankle + biceps + forearm + wrist, data = fat)
R2_weight <- summary(aux)$r.squared
c(R2_weight = R2_weight, VIF_weight = 1 / (1 - R2_weight)) R2_weight VIF_weight
 0.9701576 33.5093198def vif_by_hand(data, predictors):
    out = {}
    for v in predictors:
        others = [p for p in predictors if p != v]
        r2 = smf.ols(v + " ~ " + " + ".join(others), data=data).fit().rsquared
        out[v] = 1 / (1 - r2)
    return pd.Series(out)

print(vif_by_hand(fat, preds).round(2))age         2.25
weight     33.51
height      1.67
neck        4.32
chest       9.46
abdom      11.77
hip        14.80
thigh       7.78
knee        4.61
ankle       1.91
biceps      3.62
forearm     2.19
wrist       3.38
dtype: float64others = [p for p in preds if p != "weight"]
r2_weight = smf.ols("weight ~ " + " + ".join(others), data=fat).fit().rsquared
print(round(r2_weight, 4), round(1 / (1 - r2_weight), 3))0.9702 33.509

Interpretation. Weight has \mathrm{VIF} = 33.5: the other twelve measurements explain
R_k^2 = 0.970 of it. So its coefficient’s variance is inflated 33-fold, and its standard
error is \sqrt{33.5} \approx 5.8 times what it would be if weight stood alone. Hip (14.8)
and abdomen (11.8) are also badly inflated. Age (2.25), height (1.67), and ankle
(1.91) are almost clean. 

Figure 4 shows the ranking. This is why the weight
coefficient came out negative and insignificant in Example 12.1: it is measured against a
backdrop of near-duplicates.



Figure 4:Variance inflation factors, sorted. Weight, hip, and abdomen exceed the usual VIF = 10 warning line, so their individual coefficients are the least trustworthy in the model.

hierarchy.lvl2: 12.1 Multicollinearity and the variance inflation factor
12.1 Multicollinearity and the variance inflation factor

records.content:
There are four honest responses to high VIFs. First, do nothing, if you only care about
prediction inside the range of the data: collinearity does not bias \hat{Y} or widen its
interval much, so a predictive model can carry redundant predictors safely. Second, drop
predictors: if weight, hip, and abdomen say nearly the same thing, keep the one you can
measure best and discard the rest. Third, combine them into a single index (an average, or a
principal component) that captures the shared size signal. Fourth, shrink the coefficients
with ridge regression (Section 12.5), which tolerates collinearity by design. What you must
not do is read a collinear coefficient as a real causal effect and act on its sign.

Durable skill: Distrust a coefficient before you interpret it

A coefficient is a claim about what happens when one predictor changes and the others hold
still. If the data almost never held the others still (because the predictors move together),
that claim rests on almost nothing, no matter how confidently the software prints it. Before
you tell anyone “each extra kilogram lowers body fat,” check whether the data could even see
that comparison. The habit of asking “does my data contain the contrast this coefficient
describes?” will save you from confident nonsense in every regression you ever run, and in
every regression an AI assistant hands you.

Try it 12.1

A colleague reports a VIF of 1.0 for one predictor and 25 for another, and concludes the
second predictor is “more important.” Correct the reasoning, and say what a VIF of 25
implies about the auxiliary R_k^2.

Solution

The VIF says nothing about importance; it measures redundancy. A VIF of 1.0 means the
predictor is uncorrelated with the others, so its coefficient is estimated cleanly. A VIF of
25 means the other predictors explain R_k^2 = 1 - 1/25 = 0.96 of that predictor, so its
coefficient is poorly estimated (variance inflated 25-fold), the opposite of a reason to
trust it. A high-VIF predictor may be very important or completely useless; the VIF cannot
tell you which. It only warns that this particular coefficient is unstable.

The full model has redundant predictors; a smaller model might predict just as well and be
easier to trust. But which smaller model? With thirteen predictors there are 2^{13} = 8192
possible subsets. We need a score that ranks them. The obvious score, R^2, is useless for
this, because R^2 never decreases when you add a predictor: the biggest model always wins,
even if the added predictors are noise. Recall from 

4.2 Correlation and the regression slope that R^2 is squared
correlation, a measure of fit, not of prediction. A good selection criterion must instead
reward fit while charging a penalty for each extra parameter, so that a predictor earns its
place only if it pays for itself.

Four criteria do this, each in its own currency. Adjusted R^2 discounts R^2 by the
degrees of freedom spent. Mallows C_p estimates the total prediction error and compares
it to the number of parameters. AIC and BIC trade goodness of fit against a size
penalty from information theory. PRESS predicts each case from a model fit without it.
They usually agree on the broad message (drop the deadweight) while disagreeing on the exact
size, which is itself a lesson: there is no single correct model.

Let a candidate model have p parameters (predictors plus intercept), error sum of squares
\mathrm{SSE}_p = \sum_i (Y_i - \hat{Y}_i)^2, and \mathrm{SSTO} = \sum_i (Y_i - \bar{Y})^2.
Let \hat{\sigma}^2 be the mean square error of the largest (full) model, our best estimate
of the noise variance \sigma^2. The four criteria areR^2_{a,p} = 1 - \frac{\mathrm{SSE}_p/(n - p)}{\mathrm{SSTO}/(n-1)}, \qquad
C_p = \frac{\mathrm{SSE}_p}{\hat{\sigma}^2} - (n - 2p),\mathrm{AIC}_p = n \ln\!\left(\frac{\mathrm{SSE}_p}{n}\right) + 2p, \qquad
\mathrm{BIC}_p = n \ln\!\left(\frac{\mathrm{SSE}_p}{n}\right) + p \ln n .

R^2_{a,p} (adjusted R^2): like R^2 but with \mathrm{SSE} and \mathrm{SSTO} divided
by their degrees of freedom. Adding a useless predictor lowers it. Bigger is better.

C_p (Mallows): small is better; a model with no bias has C_p \approx p, so you look for
models with low C_p that also sit near the line C_p = p.

\mathrm{AIC}_p, \mathrm{BIC}_p: fit term plus penalty; smaller is better. BIC’s penalty
p \ln n is heavier than AIC’s 2p whenever n > 7, so BIC prefers smaller models.

That last claim is easy to see in a picture. 

Figure 5 draws the two size
penalties as straight lines. With n = 252 men, BIC charges about \ln 252 \approx 5.5 for
each extra parameter while AIC charges only 2, so the BIC line climbs almost three times as
steeply. A predictor has to improve the fit more to pay off its heavier BIC toll, which is
exactly why BIC lands on smaller models.



Figure 5:Both criteria charge for size, but with n = 252 the BIC charges about 5.5 per extra parameter against AIC’s flat 2. The steeper line means each predictor must improve the fit more to earn its place, so BIC settles on leaner models.

The fifth criterion, PRESS, gets its own subsection because it measures something
different: honest out-of-sample error.\mathrm{PRESS}_p = \sum_{i=1}^n \bigl(Y_i - \hat{Y}_{(i)}\bigr)^2,

where \hat{Y}_{(i)} is the prediction for case i from the model refit with case i
removed. In words: hide each observation, predict it from the rest, and add up the squared
misses. Smaller is better, and unlike \mathrm{SSE}, PRESS cannot be driven down just by
adding predictors, because a junk predictor helps the fit but hurts the left-out prediction.

A note on AIC and BIC constants

Different software adds different constants to AIC and BIC. R’s AIC() counts the variance
as an extra estimated parameter and includes the 2\pi term from the normal likelihood;
statsmodels uses a slightly different constant. The absolute numbers therefore differ between
R and Python, but the differences between models within one program, which are all that
matter for selection, agree. Compare AIC values only within a single language.

Theorem 12.5: Mallows C_p

Let a candidate model with p parameters have error sum of squares \mathrm{SSE}_p, and let
\hat{\sigma}^2 estimate \sigma^2 from the full model. ThenC_p = \frac{\mathrm{SSE}_p}{\hat{\sigma}^2} - (n - 2p)

estimates the standardized total mean squared error \Gamma_p = \sigma^{-2}\sum_i
E\{(\hat{Y}_i - \mu_i)^2\} of the fitted values. A model with no bias has C_p \approx p; a
model that omits an important predictor has C_p well above p.

Proof. We want a criterion
that is small when a model’s fitted values \hat{Y}_i are close to the true means
\mu_i = E\{Y_i\}, across all n cases. Define the target\Gamma_p = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^n E\bigl\{(\hat{Y}_i - \mu_i)^2\bigr\},

the total mean squared error of the fitted values, standardized by \sigma^2. Split each
term into variance and squared bias with the identity E\{W^2\} = \operatorname{Var}\{W\} +
(E\{W\})^2 applied to W = \hat{Y}_i - \mu_i:\sum_i E\{(\hat{Y}_i - \mu_i)^2\} = \underbrace{\sum_i \operatorname{Var}\{\hat{Y}_i\}}_{\text{variance}} + \underbrace{\sum_i (E\{\hat{Y}_i\} - \mu_i)^2}_{\text{squared bias, call it } B}.

For the variance term, the fitted values are \hat{\mathbf{Y}} = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{Y} with the
candidate model’s hat matrix \mathbf{H}, and \sum_i \operatorname{Var}\{\hat{Y}_i\} =
\sigma^2\,\mathrm{tr}(\mathbf{H}) = p\sigma^2, using \mathrm{tr}(\mathbf{H}) = p from


7.3 The hat matrix. So\sum_i E\{(\hat{Y}_i - \mu_i)^2\} = p\sigma^2 + B .

Now connect B to something we can compute, the expected error sum of squares. Write
\mathbf{Y} = \boldsymbol{\mu} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon} and
\mathrm{SSE}_p = \|(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})\mathbf{Y}\|^2. ThenE\{\mathrm{SSE}_p\} = \|(\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{H})\boldsymbol{\mu}\|^2 + \sigma^2\,\mathrm{tr}(\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{H}) = B + (n - p)\sigma^2,

because (\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{H})\boldsymbol{\mu} has i-th entry \mu_i - E\{\hat{Y}_i\} so
its squared length is B, and \mathbf{I}-\mathbf{H} is idempotent with trace n - p (again


7.3 The hat matrix). Solve for the bias: B = E\{\mathrm{SSE}_p\} - (n - p)\sigma^2. Substitute
into \Gamma_p:\Gamma_p = \frac{p\sigma^2 + B}{\sigma^2} = \frac{p\sigma^2 + E\{\mathrm{SSE}_p\} - (n-p)\sigma^2}{\sigma^2} = \frac{E\{\mathrm{SSE}_p\}}{\sigma^2} - (n - 2p) .

Replace the unknown E\{\mathrm{SSE}_p\} by the observed \mathrm{SSE}_p and the unknown
\sigma^2 by the full model’s \hat{\sigma}^2, and you have Mallows’ statistic:C_p = \frac{\mathrm{SSE}_p}{\hat{\sigma}^2} - (n - 2p) .

Two consequences fall out. If the candidate model has no bias (B = 0, meaning it contains
all the predictors that matter), then E\{\mathrm{SSE}_p\} = (n - p)\sigma^2 and
\Gamma_p = (n - p) - (n - 2p) = p. So unbiased models have C_p \approx p: you look for
models where C_p is both small and close to p. A model that omits an important predictor
has B > 0, which pushes C_p well above p. \blacksquare

R’s leaps package searches all 

213 subsets and reports the best model of each size.

Example 12.3: Best subsets and what the criteria choose

Question. Across all subset sizes, which models are best, and how many predictors do the
criteria want?

Intuition. For each size, find the subset with the smallest \mathrm{SSE}, then read off
C_p, adjusted R^2, and BIC. The size where each criterion turns around is its choice.

Formula. The criteria above, evaluated at the best subset of each size.

Computation.library(leaps)
rs <- regsubsets(brozek ~ age + weight + height + neck + chest + abdom + hip +
                   thigh + knee + ankle + biceps + forearm + wrist,
                 data = fat, nvmax = 13)
rss <- summary(rs)
rbind(Cp = round(rss$cp, 2), adjR2 = round(rss$adjr2, 4), BIC = round(rss$bic, 1))           [,1]      [,2]      [,3]      [,4]      [,5]      [,6]      [,7]
Cp      72.3600   20.6800   14.3100    9.1500    8.4100    7.5700    6.2800
adjR2    0.6608    0.7165    0.7243    0.7308    0.7327    0.7347    0.7371
BIC   -262.4000 -303.1000 -305.6000 -307.1000 -304.4000 -301.7000 -299.6000
           [,8]      [,9]    [,10]     [,11]     [,12]     [,13]
Cp       6.1900    7.2500    8.550   10.0700   12.0000   14.0000
adjR2    0.7383    0.7383    0.738    0.7374    0.7364    0.7353
BIC   -296.2000 -291.7000 -286.900 -281.9000 -276.4000 -270.9000c(min_Cp_size = which.min(rss$cp),
  max_adjR2_size = which.max(rss$adjr2),
  min_BIC_size = which.min(rss$bic))
names(which(rss$which[8, ]))   min_Cp_size max_adjR2_size   min_BIC_size
             8              8              4
[1] "(Intercept)" "age"         "weight"      "neck"        "abdom"
[6] "hip"         "thigh"       "forearm"     "wrist"

Python has no leaps, so we run the same exhaustive search directly with itertools,
finding the smallest-\mathrm{SSE} subset of each size and its C_p.from itertools import combinations

X = fat[preds].to_numpy()
y = fat["brozek"].to_numpy()
n = len(fat)
Xf = np.column_stack([np.ones(n), X])
beta_f, *_ = np.linalg.lstsq(Xf, y, rcond=None)
sigma2 = np.sum((y - Xf @ beta_f) ** 2) / (n - Xf.shape[1])

best_cp = {}
for k in range(1, 14):
    best_rss, best_set = np.inf, None
    for combo in combinations(range(13), k):
        Xs = np.column_stack([np.ones(n), X[:, combo]])
        b, *_ = np.linalg.lstsq(Xs, y, rcond=None)
        rss = np.sum((y - Xs @ b) ** 2)
        if rss < best_rss:
            best_rss, best_set = rss, combo
    p = k + 1
    best_cp[k] = (round(best_rss / sigma2 - (n - 2 * p), 2),
                  [preds[i] for i in best_set])

for k, (cp, vars_) in best_cp.items():
    print(k, cp, vars_)1 72.36 ['abdom']
2 20.68 ['weight', 'abdom']
3 14.31 ['weight', 'abdom', 'wrist']
4 9.15 ['weight', 'abdom', 'forearm', 'wrist']
5 8.41 ['weight', 'neck', 'abdom', 'forearm', 'wrist']
6 7.57 ['age', 'weight', 'abdom', 'thigh', 'forearm', 'wrist']
7 6.28 ['age', 'weight', 'neck', 'abdom', 'thigh', 'forearm', 'wrist']
8 6.19 ['age', 'weight', 'neck', 'abdom', 'hip', 'thigh', 'forearm', 'wrist']
9 7.25 ['age', 'weight', 'neck', 'abdom', 'hip', 'thigh', 'biceps', 'forearm', 'wrist']
10 8.55 ['age', 'weight', 'neck', 'abdom', 'hip', 'thigh', 'ankle', 'biceps', 'forearm', 'wrist']
11 10.07 ['age', 'weight', 'height', 'neck', 'abdom', 'hip', 'thigh', 'ankle', 'biceps', 'forearm', 'wrist']
12 12.0 ['age', 'weight', 'height', 'neck', 'chest', 'abdom', 'hip', 'thigh', 'ankle', 'biceps', 'forearm', 'wrist']
13 14.0 ['age', 'weight', 'height', 'neck', 'chest', 'abdom', 'hip', 'thigh', 'knee', 'ankle', 'biceps', 'forearm', 'wrist']

Interpretation. R and Python agree exactly on the best subset of each size and its C_p.
Mallows C_p and adjusted R^2 both bottom out (respectively, peak) at eight predictors:
age, weight, neck, abdomen, hip, thigh, forearm, and wrist, with C_p = 6.19. BIC, with its
heavier penalty, prefers a leaner four-predictor model (weight, abdomen, forearm, wrist). The
criteria agree that abdomen alone gets you most of the way (C_p drops from 72 to 21 once
it is in) and that the last five predictors are deadweight, but they disagree on where exactly
to stop. 

Figure 6 and 

Figure 7 show the two verdicts.



Figure 6:Mallows C_p for the best subset of each size. It drops toward the dashed C_p = p line and reaches its minimum at eight predictors, the size the criterion prefers. Beyond that, adding predictors raises C_p.



Figure 7:Two criteria, two answers. Adjusted R^2 peaks at eight predictors while BIC, penalizing size more heavily, prefers four. Neither is wrong; the honest report is a range of defensible models, not one winner.

PRESS looks expensive: refitting the model n times, once per deleted case, is 252 fits per
model here. A beautiful identity makes it free. You can get every deleted prediction from a
single fit of the full data, using the ordinary residual and the leverage value.

Theorem 12.6: PRESS deleted-residual shortcut

For a least-squares fit with ordinary residual e_i = Y_i - \hat{Y}_i and leverage value
h_{ii}, the leave-one-out deleted residual isY_i - \hat{Y}_{(i)} = \frac{e_i}{1 - h_{ii}},
\qquad\text{so}\qquad
\mathrm{PRESS} = \sum_{i=1}^n \left(\frac{e_i}{1 - h_{ii}}\right)^2 .

Every leave-one-out prediction error then follows from a single fit of the full data.

Proof. Write the design matrix \mathbf{X}
with i-th row \mathbf{x}_i', the full-data estimate \mathbf{b} =
(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}, the fitted value \hat{Y}_i =
\mathbf{x}_i'\mathbf{b}, the residual e_i = Y_i - \hat{Y}_i, and the leverage value
h_{ii} = \mathbf{x}_i'(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_i from 

7.3 The hat matrix (the
diagonal of the hat matrix). Let \mathbf{b}_{(i)} be the estimate from the data with case i removed, and
\hat{Y}_{(i)} = \mathbf{x}_i'\mathbf{b}_{(i)} the deleted prediction. We showY_i - \hat{Y}_{(i)} = \frac{e_i}{1 - h_{ii}} .

Deleting case i changes the cross-product matrix and vector to
\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X} - \mathbf{x}_i\mathbf{x}_i' and \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{x}_i Y_i.
The Sherman-Morrison inverse-update identity (derived and checked in 

9.3 Influence: which points actually change the fit)
gives(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X} - \mathbf{x}_i\mathbf{x}_i')^{-1}
= (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1} + \frac{(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_i\mathbf{x}_i'(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}}{1 - h_{ii}} .

Write \mathbf{u} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_i, so that \mathbf{x}_i'\mathbf{u} = h_{ii}
and \mathbf{x}_i'(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{x}_i'\mathbf{b} = \hat{Y}_i.
Multiply the updated inverse by \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{x}_i Y_i:\mathbf{b}_{(i)} = \mathbf{b} - \mathbf{u}Y_i + \frac{\mathbf{u}\,(\hat{Y}_i - h_{ii} Y_i)}{1 - h_{ii}}
= \mathbf{b} + \mathbf{u}\,\frac{-Y_i(1 - h_{ii}) + \hat{Y}_i - h_{ii}Y_i}{1 - h_{ii}}
= \mathbf{b} - \mathbf{u}\,\frac{e_i}{1 - h_{ii}} ,

where the numerator collapsed to \hat{Y}_i - Y_i = -e_i. Now form the deleted prediction:Y_i - \hat{Y}_{(i)} = Y_i - \mathbf{x}_i'\mathbf{b}_{(i)}
= (Y_i - \mathbf{x}_i'\mathbf{b}) + \mathbf{x}_i'\mathbf{u}\,\frac{e_i}{1 - h_{ii}}
= e_i + \frac{h_{ii}\,e_i}{1 - h_{ii}} = \frac{e_i}{1 - h_{ii}} .

So each deleted residual is just the ordinary residual divided by 1 - h_{ii}, and\mathrm{PRESS} = \sum_{i=1}^n \left(\frac{e_i}{1 - h_{ii}}\right)^2 .

High-leverage points (large h_{ii}) get their residuals magnified, which is right: those are
the cases the model props itself up on, and it predicts them worst when they are removed.
\blacksquare

Example 12.4: Selection criteria and PRESS for a candidate model

Question. For the eight-predictor model C_p chose, compute C_p by hand, compare AIC
and BIC to the full model, and confirm the PRESS shortcut against brute force.

Intuition. C_p needs only \mathrm{SSE}_p and the full-model \hat{\sigma}^2. PRESS
needs only the residuals and leverages of one fit; brute force refits 252 times and must
agree.

Formula. C_p = \mathrm{SSE}_p/\hat{\sigma}^2 - (n - 2p), and
\mathrm{PRESS} = \sum (e_i/(1 - h_{ii}))^2.

Computation.cand <- lm(brozek ~ age + weight + neck + abdom + hip + thigh + forearm + wrist,
           data = fat)
n <- nrow(fat)
sigma2 <- summary(full)$sigma^2       # full-model MSE, the estimate of sigma^2
p_cand <- length(coef(cand))          # parameters in the candidate (incl. intercept)
SSEp <- sum(resid(cand)^2)
Cp <- SSEp / sigma2 - (n - 2 * p_cand)
c(p = p_cand, SSEp = round(SSEp, 2), Cp = round(Cp, 3))       p     SSEp       Cp
   9.000 3819.990    6.191c(full_AIC = AIC(full), cand_AIC = AIC(cand),
  full_BIC = BIC(full), cand_BIC = BIC(cand))full_AIC cand_AIC full_BIC cand_BIC
1427.916 1420.225 1480.857 1455.520press <- function(model) {
  h <- hatvalues(model)
  sum((resid(model) / (1 - h))^2)
}
c(PRESS_full = press(full), PRESS_cand = press(cand))PRESS_full PRESS_cand
  4306.122   4139.682press_bruteforce <- function(form, data) {
  total <- 0
  for (i in seq_len(nrow(data))) {
    fit_i <- lm(form, data = data[-i, ])
    pred_i <- as.numeric(predict(fit_i, newdata = data[i, ]))
    total <- total + (data$brozek[i] - pred_i)^2
  }
  total
}
candform <- brozek ~ age + weight + neck + abdom + hip + thigh + forearm + wrist
c(shortcut = press(cand),
  bruteforce = press_bruteforce(candform, fat))  shortcut bruteforce
  4139.682   4139.682

Now Python. Note the different AIC and BIC constants, as promised: only within-language
differences matter.cand_vars = ["age", "weight", "neck", "abdom", "hip", "thigh", "forearm", "wrist"]
cand = smf.ols("brozek ~ " + " + ".join(cand_vars), data=fat).fit()
p_cand = len(cand.params)
SSEp = np.sum(cand.resid ** 2)
Cp = SSEp / full.scale - (n - 2 * p_cand)
print(p_cand, round(SSEp, 2), round(Cp, 3))9 3819.99 6.191def press(model):
    h = model.get_influence().hat_matrix_diag
    return np.sum((model.resid / (1 - h)) ** 2)

print(round(press(full), 2), round(press(cand), 2))4306.12 4139.68def press_bruteforce(formula, data):
    total = 0.0
    for i in range(len(data)):
        fit_i = smf.ols(formula, data=data.drop(data.index[i])).fit()
        pred_i = fit_i.predict(data.iloc[[i]]).iloc[0]
        total += (data["brozek"].iloc[i] - pred_i) ** 2
    return total

candform = "brozek ~ " + " + ".join(cand_vars)
print(round(press(cand), 2), round(press_bruteforce(candform, fat), 2))4139.68 4139.68

Interpretation. The candidate’s C_p = 6.19 against p = 9 parameters sits right on the
C_p \approx p target, the mark of an unbiased model, and it matches leaps exactly. Its AIC
is lower than the full model’s in both languages (1420 versus 1428 in R), so AIC also
prefers the smaller model. Best of all, its PRESS (4140) is lower than the full model’s
(4306): the eight-predictor model predicts left-out men better than the thirteen-predictor
model, direct evidence that the extra five predictors were hurting, not helping. And the
one-line shortcut reproduces the brute-force PRESS to the digit (4139.682), so we never have
to refit 252 times again.

Durable skill: Score models by prediction, not by fit

Any model can be made to fit the data you already have; the question is whether it will
predict the data you do not. Every criterion in this section is a different attempt to answer
that question honestly, by charging for complexity (C_p, AIC, BIC, adjusted R^2) or by
predicting held-out cases (PRESS). When someone shows you a model with a dazzling R^2, ask
what it scores on data it has not seen. This one reflex separates real predictive work from
curve-fitting, in statistics and in machine learning alike.

Try it 12.2

A four-predictor model has \mathrm{SSE}_p = 4000, and the full-model estimate is
\hat{\sigma}^2 = 15.9 with n = 252. Compute C_p (here p = 5). Is this model’s C_p
close to p, and what would it mean if C_p were much larger than p?

Solution

C_p = \mathrm{SSE}_p/\hat{\sigma}^2 - (n - 2p) = 4000/15.9 - (252 - 10) = 251.6 - 242 = 9.6.
That is somewhat above p = 5, suggesting a little bias: this small model may be leaving out
a useful predictor. A C_p much larger than p signals real bias, meaning the model omits
predictors that matter and its predictions are systematically off. A C_p near p (like the
eight-predictor model’s 6.19 against 9) signals a model with negligible bias.

It is tempting to automate the search: let the computer add and drop predictors by their
p-values until nothing improves. This is stepwise selection (Definition 12.7), and it is
available in one line in every statistics package. It is also the source of more bad science
than almost any other routine procedure, and you should understand exactly why before you ever
use it.

Definition 12.7: Stepwise selection

Stepwise selection is an automatic search (forward, backward, or both directions) that
repeatedly adds or drops predictors by their p-values until no single change improves the
model. It is prone to overfitting and produces invalid post-selection inference.

Two things go wrong. The first is overfitting (Definition 12.8): a procedure that chases
the best fit on your sample will seize on accidents of that sample, patterns that are noise and
will not repeat. The second, subtler and worse, is post-selection inference (Definition
12.9): once you have chosen a model because it fit well, the p-values, confidence intervals,
and F tests that software prints for that model are wrong. They were derived assuming the
model was fixed in advance, before you looked at the data. Choosing the model by looking at the
data breaks that assumption, and the reported significance becomes fiction.

Definition 12.8: Overfitting

Overfitting is fitting the noise in the training sample: the model chases accidents of the
data at hand, so it fits that sample well but predicts new data poorly.

Definition 12.9: Post-selection inference

Post-selection inference is inference (p-values, confidence intervals, F tests)
reported for a model chosen from the same data. It is invalid because the standard
distributions assume the model was fixed before the data were seen, which selection violates.

The cleanest way to see the disaster is to run selection on data with no signal at all. Take a
response and forty predictors that are all independent random noise, unrelated to the response
and to each other. Honest testing should almost never find anything. Selection finds plenty.set.seed(4210)
noise <- data.frame(y = rnorm(100), matrix(rnorm(100 * 40), 100, 40))
names(noise)[-1] <- paste0("x", 1:40)
pvals <- sapply(1:40, function(j)
  summary(lm(noise$y ~ noise[[paste0("x", j)]]))$coefficients[2, 4])
best5 <- order(pvals)[1:5]
selected <- lm(y ~ ., data = noise[, c("y", paste0("x", best5))])
fstat <- summary(selected)$fstatistic
c(R2 = summary(selected)$r.squared,
  overall_F_pvalue = as.numeric(pf(fstat[1], fstat[2], fstat[3], lower.tail = FALSE)))              R2 overall_F_pvalue
      0.13497399       0.01663716

We picked the five predictors with the smallest individual p-values out of forty pure-noise
columns, fit a model on just those five, and got an overall F-test p-value of 0.017. By
the usual rule, that model is “significant at the 0.05 level,” and its R^2 = 0.135 looks
respectable. Every bit of it is an illusion: there is no relationship in the data at all. The
selection step manufactured the significance by cherry-picking, and the F test, blind to
the cherry-picking, reported it as real.

This is not a one-off fluke of the seed. 

Figure 8 repeats the whole experiment
2000 times. Under honest testing the overall F p-value should be uniform on [0, 1], so
only 5 percent should fall below 0.05. After selection, 94 percent do.



Figure 8:Selecting the five best of forty pure-noise predictors, 2000 times. Honest testing would give a flat histogram with 5 percent below 0.05; instead 94 percent of the selected models look significant. The p-values printed after selection are not trustworthy.

None of this means variable selection is forbidden. It means three things. First, prefer the
criterion-based methods of Section 12.2 (compare whole models by C_p, BIC, or
cross-validated error) over p-value-driven stepwise search, and report that you searched.
Second, never quote the p-values from a selected model as if the model had been fixed in
advance; recall from 

8.4 The general linear test that the F test’s distribution assumes the
comparison was chosen before seeing the data. Third, and most reliably, judge the final model
on data it was not chosen with. That is validation, the subject of the next section, and it is
the only honest referee for a model you built by searching.

Try it 12.3

A classmate runs backward stepwise selection, arrives at a five-predictor model, and reports
that “all five predictors are significant at p < 0.01, so the model is excellent.” What is
wrong with this claim, and what evidence would actually support the model?

Solution

The p-values are contaminated by the selection: the predictors were kept because they had
small p-values, so of course the survivors look significant. Those p-values no longer have
their advertised meaning, and “all five significant” is nearly guaranteed by the procedure,
not by a real effect (the noise demonstration got a significant model from nothing at all).
Honest support would come from out-of-sample evidence: a low cross-validated error, or good
prediction on a held-out test set that played no part in choosing the model.

The residuals from a fitted model tell you how well it fits the data it was built on, which is
always flattering. To know how well a model predicts, you must test it on data it has never
seen. The idea echoes the out-of-sample thinking behind the bootstrap in 

5.4 The bootstrap for regression and
the prediction intervals of 

3.5 Prediction interval for a new observation: honest error estimates come from
treating some data as genuinely new.

The simplest version splits the data once: a training set (Definition 12.10) to fit the
model, and a test set, held back, to measure its error. The split must be made before any
modeling, and the test set must never influence a single choice. A more efficient version,
k-fold cross-validation (Definition 12.11), splits the data into k equal folds, then
rotates: each fold takes a turn as the test set while the other k - 1 folds train the model.
Averaging the k test errors uses every case for testing exactly once, so it wastes no data.


Figure 9 shows the rotation.

Definition 12.10: Training and test sets

A training set is the part of the data used to fit a model; a test set is a held-back
part used only to measure the fitted model’s prediction error. The split is made before any
modeling, and the test set influences no modeling choice.

Definition 12.11: k-fold cross-validation

k-fold cross-validation splits the data into k equal folds and rotates, letting each
fold serve once as the test set while the other k-1 folds train the model. Averaging the k
test errors tests every case exactly once, wasting no data.



Figure 9:Five-fold cross-validation. The data are split into five folds; each round holds out one fold for testing (orange) and trains on the other four (blue). Every case is tested exactly once, and the five test errors are averaged.

We measure error with the root mean squared error (Definition 12.12), the typical size of
a prediction miss, in the units of the response (percentage points of body fat). A smaller one
means the model is closer, on average, to the truth for a new man.

Definition 12.12: Root mean squared error

The root mean squared error over m held-out cases is\mathrm{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^m (Y_i - \hat{Y}_i)^2},

the typical size of a prediction miss, expressed in the units of the response.

Split the 252 men with a fixed seed so the analysis reproduces, fit on the training set, and
compare training error to test error.

Example 12.5: A seeded train/test split

Question. Fit the full and candidate models on a training set. How do their training and
test errors compare?

Intuition. Training error rewards complexity; test error is the honest measure. If the
full model overfits, its test error will be worse than the smaller model’s despite a better
training fit.

Formula. \mathrm{RMSE} on the held-out test set.

Computation.set.seed(4210)
train_idx <- sort(sample(seq_len(n), size = 189))
train <- fat[train_idx, ]
test <- fat[-train_idx, ]
c(n_train = nrow(train), n_test = nrow(test))n_train  n_test
    189      63rmse <- function(model, newdata) {
  pred <- predict(model, newdata = newdata)
  sqrt(mean((newdata$brozek - pred)^2))
}
full_tr <- lm(formula(full), data = train)
cand_tr <- lm(candform, data = train)
rbind(full = c(train = rmse(full_tr, train), test = rmse(full_tr, test)),
      cand = c(train = rmse(cand_tr, train), test = rmse(cand_tr, test)))        train     test
full 3.894608 4.099456
cand 3.948575 3.892048rng = np.random.default_rng(4210)
perm = rng.permutation(n)
test_idx = np.sort(perm[:63])
train_idx = np.sort(perm[63:])
train = fat.iloc[train_idx]
test = fat.iloc[test_idx]
print(len(train), len(test))189 63def rmse(model, data):
    pred = model.predict(data)
    return np.sqrt(np.mean((data["brozek"] - pred) ** 2))

full_tr = smf.ols("brozek ~ " + " + ".join(preds), data=train).fit()
cand_tr = smf.ols(candform, data=train).fit()
print("full", round(rmse(full_tr, train), 3), round(rmse(full_tr, test), 3))
print("cand", round(rmse(cand_tr, train), 3), round(rmse(cand_tr, test), 3))full 3.861 4.035
cand 3.903 3.929

Interpretation. The full model fits the training data slightly better than the candidate
(training RMSE 3.89 versus 3.95 in R), exactly as expected: more predictors always fit the
sample better. But on the held-out test set the order reverses. The candidate predicts better
(test RMSE 3.89 versus 4.10 in R; 3.93 versus 4.04 in Python). The five extra
predictors were fitting training-set noise, and that noise did not carry over. R and Python
use different random splits, so their exact numbers differ, but both tell the same story:
the smaller model generalizes as well or better.

A single split is noisy: a lucky or unlucky test set can mislead. Cross-validation averages
over k splits. It is worth writing the loop yourself once, so the mechanism holds no
mystery. Assign each case a fold number, then loop: train on the other folds, predict the
held-out fold, collect the squared errors.

Example 12.6: Ten-fold cross-validation by hand

Question. Compare the full and candidate models by 10-fold cross-validated RMSE on the
whole dataset.

Intuition. Rotate through ten folds, predicting each once. The averaged error is a stable
estimate of prediction accuracy, and it should again favor the smaller model.

Formula. \mathrm{CV\ RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^{K}\mathrm{MSE}_k}, where
\mathrm{MSE}_k is the mean squared error on fold k.

Computation.set.seed(4210)
K <- 10
folds <- sample(rep(1:K, length.out = n))
cv_rmse <- function(form) {
  sq_err <- numeric(K)
  for (k in 1:K) {
    fit_k <- lm(form, data = fat[folds != k, ])
    pred_k <- predict(fit_k, newdata = fat[folds == k, ])
    sq_err[k] <- mean((fat$brozek[folds == k] - pred_k)^2)
  }
  sqrt(mean(sq_err))
}
c(full_CV = cv_rmse(formula(full)), cand_CV = cv_rmse(candform)) full_CV  cand_CV
4.130328 4.042110rng = np.random.default_rng(4210)
folds = rng.integers(0, 10, size=n)
def cv_rmse(formula):
    sq_err = []
    for k in range(10):
        fit_k = smf.ols(formula, data=fat[folds != k]).fit()
        pred_k = fit_k.predict(fat[folds == k])
        sq_err.append(np.mean((fat["brozek"][folds == k] - pred_k) ** 2))
    return np.sqrt(np.mean(sq_err))

print(round(cv_rmse("brozek ~ " + " + ".join(preds)), 3),
      round(cv_rmse(candform), 3))4.157 4.046

Interpretation. The candidate’s cross-validated RMSE (4.04 in R, 4.05 in Python) beats
the full model’s (4.13 in R, 4.16 in Python) by about 0.1 percentage points of body fat.
The gap is modest but consistent across languages and across the single-split result, and it
points the same way every time: the eight-predictor model predicts new men better than the
thirteen-predictor model. 

Figure 10 traces this over every model size, and it is
the central picture of the chapter.



Figure 10:Training error (blue) always falls as predictors are added. Cross-validated error (orange), the honest measure, falls, flattens, and then rises: past a point, extra predictors buy fit on this sample but not accuracy on new men. The gap between the curves is overfitting made visible.

Durable skill: Hold out data before you fall in love with a model

The single most reliable guardrail against fooling yourself is to set aside data you will not
touch until the very end, and to judge the final model only on that. It costs you nothing but
discipline, and it catches overfitting, data leakage, and post-selection optimism all at once.
Every serious predictive project, from a course paper to a production machine-learning system,
lives or dies on whether its held-out evaluation was truly held out. Build the habit now:
decide your test set first, and do not peek.

Try it 12.4

Explain why training RMSE always decreases (or stays equal) as you add predictors, but
cross-validated RMSE can increase. Which one estimates how the model will do on a new man, and
why?

Solution

Adding a predictor gives least squares more freedom, so it can only reduce (never increase)
the sum of squared residuals on the training data; training RMSE therefore falls monotonically.
But a predictor that only fits training-set noise will make predictions on held-out data
worse, so cross-validated RMSE can rise once you pass the useful predictors. Cross-validated
RMSE estimates performance on a new man, because it always scores predictions on cases that
did not train the model. Training RMSE cannot, because the model has already seen those cases.

hierarchy.lvl2: 12.5 A look at shrinkage: ridge and lasso
12.5 A look at shrinkage: ridge and lasso
12.5 A look at shrinkage: ridge and lasso
12.5 A look at shrinkage: ridge and lasso

records.content:
Selection is a blunt instrument: a predictor is either in (full coefficient) or out (zero).
Shrinkage (Definition 12.13) offers a gentler alternative. Instead of deleting predictors,
it keeps them all but pulls their coefficients toward zero, trading a little bias for a large
drop in variance. When predictors are collinear, ordinary least squares can produce enormous
coefficients that cancel each other (the negative weight from Example 12.1). Shrinkage refuses
to let coefficients grow that large, which stabilizes them.

Definition 12.13: Shrinkage

Shrinkage pulls a model’s coefficients toward zero by adding a penalty on their size,
trading a little bias for a large drop in variance. It keeps every predictor rather than
deleting any.

Key idea

The bias-variance trade-off is the single idea under every method in this chapter. A flexible
model (many predictors, no shrinkage) has low bias but high variance; a rigid one (few
predictors, heavy shrinkage) has high bias but low variance. Prediction error is their sum plus
irreducible noise, so the best predictor lives in between, as 

Figure 11 shows.
Selection and shrinkage are both ways of aiming for that sweet spot.



Figure 11:The bias-variance trade-off. As a model grows more flexible, bias falls but variance rises, and expected prediction error is their sum: a U-shape with a sweet spot. Selection and shrinkage are both ways of aiming for that sweet spot instead of the low-bias, high-variance extreme.

hierarchy.lvl2: 12.5 A look at shrinkage: ridge and lasso
12.5 A look at shrinkage: ridge and lasso

records.content:
Ordinary least squares minimizes the residual sum of squares. Ridge (Definition 12.14) and
lasso (Definition 12.15) add a penalty on the size of the coefficients.

Definition 12.14: Ridge regression

Ridge regression minimizes least squares plus an L_2 penalty on the slopes:\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{\text{ridge}} = \arg\min_{\boldsymbol{\beta}} \sum_{i=1}^n (Y_i - \mathbf{x}_i'\boldsymbol{\beta})^2 + \lambda \sum_{k=1}^{q} \beta_k^2 .

It shrinks every coefficient toward zero but rarely to exactly zero.

Definition 12.15: Lasso

The lasso minimizes least squares plus an L_1 penalty on the slopes:\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{\text{lasso}} = \arg\min_{\boldsymbol{\beta}} \sum_{i=1}^n (Y_i - \mathbf{x}_i'\boldsymbol{\beta})^2 + \lambda \sum_{k=1}^{q} |\beta_k| .

Because its penalty has corners on the axes, it can set coefficients exactly to zero, doing
variable selection and shrinkage at once.

The first term is the usual least-squares loss; it wants a good fit.

The second term is the penalty; it wants small coefficients. The sum runs over the q
slope coefficients (not the intercept), and predictors are standardized first so the penalty
treats them fairly.

\lambda \ge 0 is the tuning parameter that sets the strength of the penalty. At
\lambda = 0 both reduce to least squares; as \lambda \to \infty all coefficients are
crushed to zero. We choose \lambda by cross-validation.

Ridge uses the squared penalty (\sum \beta_k^2, the L_2 norm); lasso uses the
absolute-value penalty (\sum |\beta_k|, the L_1 norm). The difference sounds minor and is
not. Ridge shrinks every coefficient toward zero but rarely to exactly zero. Lasso can set
coefficients exactly to zero, so it does variable selection and shrinkage at once. The reason
is geometric, shown in 

Figure 12: the lasso’s constraint region is a
diamond with corners on the axes, and the best-fitting contour tends to touch a corner, where
a coefficient is zero. Ridge’s constraint region is a smooth disk with no corners.



Figure 12:Why lasso zeros coefficients and ridge does not. The solution is where a growing loss contour first meets the constraint region. The lasso diamond has corners on the axes, so contours often meet it at a corner (a coefficient set to zero); the smooth ridge disk has no corners, so it only shrinks.

hierarchy.lvl2: 12.5 A look at shrinkage: ridge and lasso
12.5 A look at shrinkage: ridge and lasso

records.content:
The R package glmnet is the standard tool. Its cv.glmnet fits the whole path of \lambda
values and picks one by cross-validation, returning lambda.min (the error minimizer) and
lambda.1se (the largest \lambda within one standard error of the minimum, a deliberately
sparser choice). Predictors are standardized automatically.

Example 12.7: Ridge and lasso on the body-fat data

Question. Fit lasso and ridge to the training set, see which predictors the lasso keeps,
and compare test-set prediction to ordinary least squares.

Intuition. Cross-validation picks the penalty. The lasso should zero out the redundant
predictors; both should predict comparably to a well-chosen OLS model.

Formula. The ridge and lasso objectives above, with \lambda chosen by cross-validation.

Computation.library(glmnet)
Xtr <- as.matrix(train[, preds]); Ytr <- train$brozek
Xte <- as.matrix(test[, preds]);  Yte <- test$brozek
set.seed(4210)
cv_lasso <- cv.glmnet(Xtr, Ytr, alpha = 1)
c(lambda_min = cv_lasso$lambda.min, lambda_1se = cv_lasso$lambda.1se)lambda_min lambda_1se
 0.1876241  0.9123410round(as.vector(coef(cv_lasso, s = "lambda.1se")), 4) [1] -27.8141   0.0000   0.0000  -0.0415   0.0000   0.0000   0.5362   0.0000
 [9]   0.0000   0.0000   0.0000   0.0000   0.0000   0.0000set.seed(4210)
cv_ridge <- cv.glmnet(Xtr, Ytr, alpha = 0)
pred_lasso <- predict(cv_lasso, newx = Xte, s = "lambda.min")
pred_ridge <- predict(cv_ridge, newx = Xte, s = "lambda.min")
c(lasso_test_RMSE = sqrt(mean((Yte - pred_lasso)^2)),
  ridge_test_RMSE = sqrt(mean((Yte - pred_ridge)^2)),
  ols_full_test_RMSE = rmse(full_tr, test))   lasso_test_RMSE    ridge_test_RMSE ols_full_test_RMSE
          4.344938           4.539166           4.099456

Python uses scikit-learn’s LassoCV and RidgeCV. We standardize the predictors by hand
first, as glmnet does internally.from sklearn.linear_model import LassoCV, RidgeCV
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

scaler = StandardScaler().fit(train[preds])
Xtr = scaler.transform(train[preds]); ytr = train["brozek"].to_numpy()
Xte = scaler.transform(test[preds]);  yte = test["brozek"].to_numpy()
lasso = LassoCV(cv=10, random_state=4210).fit(Xtr, ytr)
ridge = RidgeCV(alphas=np.logspace(-2, 3, 100)).fit(Xtr, ytr)
lasso_rmse = np.sqrt(np.mean((yte - lasso.predict(Xte)) ** 2))
ridge_rmse = np.sqrt(np.mean((yte - ridge.predict(Xte)) ** 2))
print("lasso CV nonzero:", int(np.sum(lasso.coef_ != 0)), "of 13")
print("lasso test RMSE:", round(lasso_rmse, 3),
      " ridge test RMSE:", round(ridge_rmse, 3))lasso CV nonzero: 13 of 13
lasso test RMSE: 4.019  ridge test RMSE: 4.019from sklearn.linear_model import Lasso

for a in [0.1, 0.5, 1.0]:
    fit_a = Lasso(alpha=a).fit(Xtr, ytr)
    kept = [p for p, c in zip(preds, fit_a.coef_) if c != 0]
    print(f"alpha={a}: {len(kept)} kept -> {kept}")alpha=0.1: 11 kept -> ['age', 'weight', 'height', 'neck', 'abdom', 'hip', 'thigh', 'ankle', 'biceps', 'forearm', 'wrist']
alpha=0.5: 4 kept -> ['age', 'height', 'abdom', 'wrist']
alpha=1.0: 2 kept -> ['height', 'abdom']

Interpretation. The lasso does exactly what it promises. At the sparser lambda.1se, R’s
glmnet keeps only two predictors with nonzero coefficients, abdomen (0.54) and height
(-0.04), zeroing the other eleven; the intercept (-27.8) is not penalized. Python shows the
same behavior as the penalty grows: at a light penalty eleven predictors survive, at a heavier
one only two (height and abdomen). 

Figure 13 draws the whole path. On this
particular split the shrinkage models predict slightly worse than the eight-predictor OLS
model (test RMSE around 4.3 to 4.5 versus 4.1). That is honest and worth stating
plainly. With n = 189 men and only thirteen predictors, ordinary least squares is not badly
overfit, so shrinkage has little variance to save here. Its payoff shows up when predictors
vastly outnumber cases, or when collinearity is so severe that OLS coefficients become
unusable. What the lasso always gives you, even here, is a compact, automatically selected
model you can read.



Figure 13:Lasso coefficient paths. As the penalty \log\lambda grows (moving right), coefficients are pulled to exactly zero one after another, so the lasso performs continuous variable selection. Abdomen and wrist are the last to survive.

hierarchy.lvl2: 12.5 A look at shrinkage: ridge and lasso
12.5 A look at shrinkage: ridge and lasso

records.content:
Ridge and lasso answer the collinearity and selection problems in one move. Ridge keeps every
predictor but shrinks the coefficients so no two collinear predictors can blow up against each
other; it is the natural tool when you believe many predictors each contribute a little. Lasso
shrinks and selects at once, zeroing the coefficients it does not need, so it is the natural
tool when you believe only a few predictors matter and you want the model to find them. Both
replace the discrete, unstable question “which predictors are in?” with a smooth, tunable dial
\lambda, set by cross-validation. That is why shrinkage, not stepwise selection, is the
modern default when prediction is the goal.

Try it 12.5

As \lambda increases from 0 toward infinity in ridge regression, describe what happens to
the coefficients, to the model’s variance, and to its bias. What does \lambda = 0 give, and
what does a very large \lambda give?

Solution

At \lambda = 0 the penalty vanishes and ridge is ordinary least squares: lowest bias,
highest variance. As \lambda grows, the coefficients shrink toward zero, the model’s
variance falls (the estimates stabilize, less sensitive to the sample), and its bias rises
(the shrunk coefficients are systematically too small in magnitude). As \lambda \to \infty
all slope coefficients go to zero and the model predicts the mean response for everyone:
zero variance, maximum bias. Cross-validation chooses the \lambda that balances the two for
the smallest prediction error.

You can now handle a regression with more predictors than you can trust. You diagnosed
multicollinearity with the variance inflation factor and derived it from an auxiliary
regression; you compared candidate models with adjusted R^2, Mallows C_p, AIC, BIC, and
PRESS, deriving C_p and proving the deleted-residual shortcut that makes leave-one-out free;
you saw, in a pure-noise demonstration, why stepwise selection overfits and why post-selection
p-values lie; you wrote a k-fold cross-validation loop by hand and used a held-out test set
to judge a model honestly; and you described and ran ridge and lasso, reading shrinkage as a
bias-variance trade made by a tunable penalty. On the body-fat data the eight-predictor
candidate beat the full model on every honest measure: lower C_p, lower PRESS, and lower
cross-validated error.

Figure 14 puts the whole chapter on one page: diagnose the redundancy, let your
goal decide the remedy, choose a model by a prediction-minded score, and confirm the winner on
data it never saw.



Figure 14:The chapter in one picture. Diagnose the redundancy, let your goal (predict versus interpret) pick the remedy, choose the model by a prediction-minded score, and confirm it on data it never saw.

Key terms. Multicollinearity, variance inflation factor, auxiliary regression,
adjusted R^2, Mallows C_p, AIC, BIC, PRESS, deleted residual,
stepwise selection, overfitting, post-selection inference, training and test
sets, k-fold cross-validation, root mean squared error, bias-variance
trade-off, shrinkage, ridge regression, lasso, tuning parameter \lambda.

You should now be able to

Diagnose multicollinearity with the VIF and derive it from an auxiliary regression (Theorem 12.4).

Explain what multicollinearity does and does not harm, and list the standard remedies.

Compute and compare adjusted R^2, Mallows C_p, AIC, BIC, and PRESS.

Derive Mallows C_p (Theorem 12.5) and prove the PRESS deleted-residual shortcut (Theorem 12.6).

Explain, bluntly, why stepwise selection and post-selection inference mislead.

Implement k-fold cross-validation by hand in R and Python.

Describe ridge and lasso shrinkage and demonstrate them on the body-fat data.

Where this fits. This chapter is the FIT and CHECK stages of 

The modeling workflow done together
and repeatedly: you fit many models, check each for collinearity and prediction error, and
iterate toward one you can defend. Chapters 8 through 11 gave you a single multiple regression
and the tools to interpret and diagnose it; this chapter faced the harder situation of many
competing models and taught you to choose among them by prediction rather than fit. The
validation mindset carries straight into 

13. Logistic regression, where the same held-out thinking becomes the
classification metrics (accuracy, ROC) that judge a logistic model, and the shrinkage and
criterion ideas reappear wherever a model has more parameters than the data can comfortably
support.

Q1. Does multicollinearity bias my coefficients? No. Least squares stays unbiased under
collinearity: on average the coefficients are still correct. What collinearity does is inflate
their variance, so any single sample’s estimates can be wildly off, with flipped signs and
huge standard errors. The average is fine; the individual estimate is unreliable.

Q2. If collinearity does not hurt prediction, why care about it? It depends on your goal.
If you only want to predict body fat for men like those in the data, a collinear model predicts
fine and you can ignore the VIFs. If you want to interpret coefficients (to say which
measurement matters and by how much), collinearity makes those coefficients meaningless, and
you must fix it by dropping, combining, or shrinking predictors.

Q3. Which selection criterion should I use? For interpretation, BIC and its heavier
penalty give you a lean, defensible model. For prediction, cross-validated error is the most
direct answer, and Mallows C_p is a fast, well-motivated stand-in. Do not agonize over small
differences: report that the criteria pointed to a range of models (here, four to eight
predictors) and pick within that range for a reason you can state.

Q4. Why is the C_p of the full model always equal to the number of its parameters? By
construction. C_p = \mathrm{SSE}_p/\hat{\sigma}^2 - (n - 2p), and for the full model
\hat{\sigma}^2 = \mathrm{SSE}_{\text{full}}/(n - p_{\text{full}}), so
\mathrm{SSE}_{\text{full}}/\hat{\sigma}^2 = n - p_{\text{full}} and $C_p = (n - p_{\text{full}})

(n - 2p_{\text{full}}) = p_{\text{full}}. The full model is the reference point, so itsC_p lands exactly on the C_p = p$ line.

Q5. Is a lower PRESS always better? As a comparison of models on the same data, yes: lower
PRESS means better leave-one-out prediction. But PRESS is still an in-sample estimate in the
sense that it uses the same 252 men to build and to test (just never the same man for both at
once), so it can be optimistic if the whole dataset is unusual. A truly held-out test set, or
k-fold cross-validation repeated with different seeds, is a stronger check.

Q6. Can I just trust the model my software’s step() function returns? No. Stepwise
selection overfits and its reported p-values are invalid (Section 12.3). If you use it,
treat its output as a candidate to be validated on held-out data, never as a finished model,
and never quote its coefficients’ significance as if the model had been chosen in advance.

Q7. Lasso and stepwise both drop predictors. What is the difference? Stepwise makes hard,
unstable in-or-out decisions by p-value and reports dishonest inference. Lasso makes the
decision continuously through a single penalty \lambda chosen by cross-validation, shrinking
coefficients smoothly and turning them off only as the penalty grows. Lasso is more stable, has
a coherent bias-variance justification, and is built for prediction. When in doubt, prefer it.

Q8. Why standardize predictors before ridge or lasso? The penalty \sum \beta_k^2 or
\sum |\beta_k| adds up coefficients measured in each predictor’s own units. Without
standardizing, a predictor measured in small units (and so carrying a large coefficient) would
be penalized more than an identical predictor in large units. Standardizing puts every
predictor on the same scale so the penalty is fair. Both glmnet and the scaled scikit-learn
fit here do this.

These five questions are written in the style of the course exams: each asks you to
explain your reasoning in full sentences, not just to produce a number. Where output is
shown, it was produced on the course machine from fat.csv; read the numbers you need off the
printout and say which you used. Work each one before opening its model answer.

EP 12.1 (concepts, evaluate a claim). The full body-fat model of Example 12.1 was refit and
the weight coefficient and three variance inflation factors were printed.full <- lm(brozek ~ age + weight + height + neck + chest + abdom + hip +
             thigh + knee + ankle + biceps + forearm + wrist, data = fat)
round(coef(summary(full))["weight", ], 4)
round(car::vif(full)[c("weight", "hip", "abdom")], 2)  Estimate Std. Error    t value   Pr(>|t|)
   -0.0803     0.0496    -1.6198     0.1066
weight    hip  abdom
 33.51  14.80  11.77

A student reads the negative weight coefficient and concludes: “holding the other twelve
measurements fixed, heavier men have less body fat, so we should tell clients that gaining
weight lowers their body fat percentage.” Evaluate this claim. Use the variance inflation
factor in your answer, and state clearly what the coefficient can and cannot support.

Model answer

The coefficient is a partial slope: it is meant to describe what happens to body fat when
weight rises by one pound while all other measurements are held fixed. The claim fails
because the data barely contain that comparison. Weight has \mathrm{VIF} = 33.51, which means
the other twelve measurements explain R_k^2 = 1 - 1/33.51 = 0.970 of weight, so men almost
never differ in weight while their chest, abdomen, hip, and thigh stay fixed. The estimate
therefore rests on almost no real contrast: its standard error is inflated by
\sqrt{33.51} \approx 5.8 times, the t statistic is only -1.62 with p = 0.107, and the
coefficient is not distinguishable from zero. Multicollinearity inflates the variance of a
coefficient without biasing it, so the negative sign here is an artifact of the near-duplicate
size predictors, not evidence that gaining weight reduces fat. The coefficient cannot support
any advice about changing weight; the honest statement is that this model cannot separate the
effect of weight from the other girths. A weak answer stops at “it is not significant” or
“weight does not matter” without invoking the VIF and the missing contrast, or worse, treats
the negative sign as a real effect to be explained.

six-predictor version of the model, brozek ~ age + weight + height + abdom + hip + thigh,
reporting the best subset of each size.rs <- regsubsets(brozek ~ age + weight + height + abdom + hip + thigh,
                 data = fat, nvmax = 6)
ss <- summary(rs)
rbind(Cp = round(ss$cp, 2), adjR2 = round(ss$adjr2, 4), BIC = round(ss$bic, 1))
c(min_Cp = which.min(ss$cp), max_adjR2 = which.max(ss$adjr2), min_BIC = which.min(ss$bic))        [,1]    [,2]    [,3]    [,4]    [,5]    [,6]

adjR2 0.6608  0.7165  0.7203  0.7208  0.7212  0.7202
BIC  -262.40 -303.10 -302.00 -298.00 -293.70 -288.30

        3         5         2

The best size-2 model is weight + abdom, the best size-3 model is weight + abdom + thigh,
and the best size-5 model is weight + height + abdom + hip + thigh. Interpret this output.
State which model size each of the three criteria selects, explain in full sentences why
Mallows C_p and BIC land on different sizes, and say which model you would report to a
colleague who wants to interpret the coefficients, with your reason.

Model answer

Mallows C_p is smallest at size 3 (weight + abdom + thigh, C_p = 3.88), adjusted R^2
is largest at size 5 (0.72), and BIC is smallest at size 2 (weight + abdom, -303.1). The
criteria disagree because each charges a different price per added parameter. Adjusted R^2
penalizes size only lightly, so it tolerates the largest model that still improves the fit at
all; BIC charges p \ln n, about 5.5 per parameter with n = 252, the heaviest toll, so it
settles on the leanest model; C_p sits between them. All three agree on the substance:
abdomen and weight together capture nearly all the signal (C_p falls from 55 to 6.3 as
soon as both are in), and height buys essentially nothing for its parameter. For a colleague
who wants to interpret coefficients I would report the BIC or C_p model (two or three
predictors), because a lean model gives coefficients you can actually read and trust, and I
would state plainly that the criteria point to a small range of defensible sizes (two to
three), not a single winner. A weak answer just reads the three minima off the printout without
explaining that the differing penalties per parameter are what drive the disagreement, or
declares one criterion the “correct” one.

EP 12.3 (what would change if). For the four-predictor model
brozek ~ weight + abdom + forearm + wrist, the error sum of squares is
\mathrm{SSE}_p = 3994.31 with n = 252 and p = 5 parameters. The full model’s estimate of
the noise variance is \hat{\sigma}^2 = 15.90.cand4 <- lm(brozek ~ weight + abdom + forearm + wrist, data = fat)
c(SSE = sum(resid(cand4)^2), sigma2_full = summary(full)$sigma^2)       SSE sigma2_full
  3994.311      15.904

First compute C_p for this model. Then answer: what would change if you estimated
\sigma^2 not from the full model but from the abdomen-only model, whose residual mean square
is 20.38? Compute the resulting C_p and explain, in full sentences, why C_p becomes
meaningless with that choice.

Model answer

With the full-model variance, C_p = \mathrm{SSE}_p/\hat{\sigma}^2 - (n - 2p) =
3994.31/15.90 - (252 - 10) = 251.2 - 242 = 9.15, a little above p = 5, which hints at slight
bias in this small model. If instead \sigma^2 is estimated from the abdomen-only model, that
estimate is 20.38, and C_p = 3994.31/20.38 - 242 = 196.0 - 242 = -46.0, a negative and
nonsensical value. The reason is that the abdomen-only model is underspecified: it has left
real predictive signal in its residuals, so its residual mean square estimates \sigma^2 plus
a positive bias, not \sigma^2 itself, and it comes out too large. The derivation of C_p
assumes \hat{\sigma}^2 is unbiased for the true \sigma^2, which is exactly why you take it
from the full model (or a large near-unbiased model), whose residual mean square is unbiased. A
biased, inflated \sigma^2 shrinks every \mathrm{SSE}_p/\hat{\sigma}^2 term, pushes all
C_p values far below p, and destroys both the “C_p \approx p” benchmark and any honest
comparison between models. A weak answer computes -46.0 but does not explain that the
abdomen-only mean square is biased upward because it omits real predictors, nor connects the
failure to the unbiasedness assumption behind C_p.

EP 12.4 (evaluate a claim, interpret output). Three models were compared by their training
RMSE and by 5-fold cross-validated RMSE on the full body-fat data: the thirteen-predictor full
model, the eight-predictor candidate (age, weight, neck, abdomen, hip, thigh, forearm, wrist),
and a two-predictor model (abdomen, weight).set.seed(4210)
folds <- sample(rep(1:5, length.out = nrow(fat)))
# training RMSE and 5-fold CV RMSE for each modelfull   train RMSE 3.876   5-fold CV RMSE 4.097
cand8  train RMSE 3.893   5-fold CV RMSE 4.003
two    train RMSE 4.103   5-fold CV RMSE 4.183

A student argues: “the full model has the lowest training RMSE (3.876), so it predicts best;
report the full model.” Evaluate this argument. Say which number estimates how the model will
do on a new man and why, explain why the ranking flips between the two columns, and state which
model you would report.

Model answer

The argument confuses fit with prediction. Training RMSE can only fall or stay equal as
predictors are added, because least squares fits the very sample it is scored on, so the full
model’s lowest training RMSE is guaranteed by its size and is not evidence of better
prediction. The honest measure is the 5-fold cross-validated RMSE, which scores each man with a
model that did not train on him. By that measure the eight-predictor candidate is best
(4.003) and the full model is the worst of the three (4.097): the five extra predictors in
the full model fit training-set noise that does not carry over to new men, so the ranking flips
between the columns. The two-predictor model has the highest CV RMSE (4.183), so it underfits:
too few predictors hurt as well. I would report the eight-predictor candidate, because it
minimizes the out-of-sample error, and I would note that CV RMSE, not training RMSE, is the
number that estimates accuracy on a new man. A weak answer says the full model “overfits”
without noting that training RMSE must decrease with size, fails to name CV RMSE as the
out-of-sample estimate, or overlooks that the two-predictor model underfits.

\lambda chosen by cross-validation, and the coefficients were read at two penalties,
lambda.min (the error minimizer) and the sparser lambda.1se.X <- as.matrix(fat[, preds]); Y <- fat$brozek
set.seed(4210)
cvl <- cv.glmnet(X, Y, alpha = 1)

            onese = coef(cvl, s = "lambda.1se")[, 1]), 3)                min   onese

weight       -0.065   0.000

At lambda.min eleven predictors have nonzero coefficients; at lambda.1se only six do.
Explain what happens to the coefficients as \lambda increases from lambda.min to
lambda.1se, why the lasso sets some coefficients exactly to zero while ridge regression would
not, and what would change if the predictors were not standardized before fitting.

Model answer

As \lambda grows from lambda.min to lambda.1se, the penalty on coefficient size gets
heavier, so every coefficient is pulled harder toward zero and five more predictors (weight,
hip, thigh, ankle, biceps) reach exactly zero, leaving six nonzero. The lasso can zero
coefficients because its L_1 penalty \sum |\beta_k| has corners on the coordinate axes: as
the least-squares loss contour grows outward it tends to first touch the diamond-shaped
constraint region at a corner, where one coefficient is exactly zero, so the lasso performs
variable selection as it shrinks. Ridge regression uses the L_2 penalty \sum \beta_k^2,
whose constraint region is a smooth disk with no corners, so it shrinks every coefficient
toward zero but essentially never to exactly zero; ridge would keep all thirteen predictors. If
the predictors were not standardized, the penalty would be unfair, because it sums coefficients
measured in each predictor’s own units: a predictor recorded in small units carries a naturally
large coefficient and would be penalized more heavily, so it would be shrunk and zeroed first
for a reason unrelated to its importance. Standardizing puts every predictor on a common scale
so the penalty compares them fairly, and without it the set of surviving predictors would
change arbitrarily. A weak answer says only that “the coefficients shrink” without the corner
geometry that explains the exact zeros, or cannot say why standardization matters for a fair
penalty across differing units.

Resumen del capítulo (en español)

Este capítulo trata el problema de tener demasiados predictores, casi todos redundantes, usando
datos de grasa corporal de 252 hombres (fat.csv): se predice el porcentaje de grasa, medido por
pesaje bajo el agua, a partir de trece medidas de cinta métrica. Al ajustar el modelo completo,
R^2 = 0.749 parece bueno, pero los coeficientes no tienen sentido (el de peso sale negativo).
La causa es la multicolinealidad (multicollinearity): los predictores están muy
correlacionados, así que el modelo no puede repartir el efecto compartido.

El factor de inflación de la varianza (variance inflation factor, VIF),
\mathrm{VIF}_k = 1/(1 - R_k^2), mide el daño. Lo derivamos de una regresión auxiliar y
mostramos que \operatorname{Var}\{b_k\} = \sigma^2/(S_{kk}(1 - R_k^2)): la colinealidad
infla la varianza del coeficiente por ese factor. Para el peso, \mathrm{VIF} = 33.5.

Para elegir entre modelos usamos criterios que premian la predicción, no el ajuste: R^2
ajustado, el C_p de Mallows (Mallows’ Cp) (que derivamos, con C_p \approx p para un
modelo sin sesgo), AIC, BIC y PRESS, cuyo atajo del residuo eliminado,
\mathrm{PRESS} = \sum (e_i/(1 - h_{ii}))^2, demostramos. El modelo de ocho predictores
elegido tiene C_p = 6.19 y menor PRESS que el modelo completo.

Advertimos sobre los peligros de la selección paso a paso (stepwise selection): sobreajusta,
y los valores p posteriores a la selección son falsos (una demostración con puro ruido produce
modelos “significativos” el 94 por ciento de las veces). La solución honesta es la validación
(validation): división en entrenamiento y prueba, y validación cruzada de k particiones
(k-fold cross-validation), que escribimos a mano. El modelo pequeño predice mejor fuera de la
muestra. Finalmente, presentamos la contracción (shrinkage): ridge y lasso encogen los
coeficientes hacia cero, equilibrando sesgo y varianza; el lasso además selecciona variables.
Es la respuesta moderna cuando el objetivo es predecir.

Read in English

La noche del 27 de enero de 1986, ingenieros de Morton Thiokol y de la NASA discutían en una
llamada de conferencia si debían lanzar el transbordador espacial Challenger a la mañana
siguiente. El pronóstico para Cabo Cañaveral era frío, cerca del punto de congelación, más frío
que cualquier lanzamiento anterior. La preocupación eran las juntas tóricas de goma (O-rings) que
sellaban las uniones de los cohetes aceleradores sólidos. Con el frío la goma se endurece, y una
junta tórica rígida podría no sellar a tiempo para contener el gas ardiente. Los ingenieros tenían
datos de 23 vuelos anteriores: para cada uno, la temperatura en el lanzamiento y el número de
juntas tóricas, de seis, que mostraron daño por calor después.

Figure 1 muestra esos datos. El daño ocurrió tanto en vuelos cálidos como fríos,
pero los lanzamientos más fríos cargaron los peores incidentes, y el lanzamiento en discusión sería
mucho más frío que cualquier cosa en el registro. Esa es la parte difícil. Todo vuelo que había
volado alguna vez lo hizo a 53 grados Fahrenheit o más cálido. La temperatura de lanzamiento
pronosticada era de unos 31 grados, fuera del borde izquierdo de toda la experiencia. Para decir
algo sobre el riesgo a 31 grados, hay que extender un patrón más allá del último punto de datos, que
es exactamente el movimiento que un estadístico está entrenado para desconfiar.



Figure 1:Los 23 vuelos anteriores al Challenger. El daño fue peor en los lanzamientos más fríos, y la decisión de lanzamiento a 31 grados (línea discontinua) quedó muy por debajo de cualquier temperatura a la que el programa había volado, así que juzgar su riesgo significa extrapolar.

La respuesta aquí no es un número como horas de trabajo o tasa de ahorro. Está más cerca de un sí o
no: ¿falló una junta tórica o no? La regresión de recta, la herramienta de los últimos once
capítulos, está construida para una respuesta continua y se rompe de maneras específicas cuando el
resultado es binario. Este capítulo construye la herramienta correcta. Aprenderás el modelo de
regresión logística, los momios y el logaritmo de los momios que lo hacen lineal, cómo ajustarlo por
máxima verosimilitud cuando ninguna fórmula da las estimaciones, cómo leer sus coeficientes como
razones de momios sin los errores habituales, cómo probarlos, y cómo juzgar qué tan bien el modelo
separa los dos resultados. Al terminar podrás poner un número al riesgo sobre el que discutían los
ingenieros del Challenger, y decir honestamente cuánto confiar en él.

Esta lección de un vistazo

Qué estamos haciendo: Construir la regresión logística para una respuesta de sí o no: el modelo, los momios y el logaritmo de los momios, el ajuste por máxima verosimilitud, la lectura de coeficientes como razones de momios, su prueba, y el juicio del ajuste con una tabla de clasificación y una curva ROC, sobre los datos de las juntas tóricas del Challenger.

Por qué lo hacemos: La respuesta ahora es binaria (¿falló una junta tórica?), y el modelo de recta de los últimos once capítulos se rompe de tres maneras específicas cuando el resultado es 0 o 1.

Objetivo principal: Enunciar el modelo logístico, ajustarlo por máxima verosimilitud, interpretar un coeficiente como una razón de momios sin los errores habituales, y evaluar el ajuste con la devianza, la clasificación y una curva ROC.

Qué cambió respecto a los capítulos anteriores: Los capítulos 2 a 12 supusieron una respuesta continua, de varianza constante y normal; este capítulo mantiene el predictor lineal pero lo envuelve en un enlace para un resultado binario, reutilizando la codificación de indicadores (

11.1 De categorías a números: codificación con indicadores), la prueba lineal general en forma de devianza (

8.4 La prueba lineal general) y la mentalidad de validación (

12.4 Validación: entrenar, probar y validar cruzadamente).

Objetivos de aprendizaje

Al terminar este capítulo serás capaz de:

Explicar por qué los mínimos cuadrados ordinarios son el modelo equivocado para una respuesta binaria, nombrando las tres fallas específicas.

Enunciar el modelo de regresión logística y conectar probabilidad, momios y logaritmo de los momios.

Deducir las ecuaciones de puntaje de la regresión logística y describir cómo los mínimos cuadrados reponderados iterativamente (IRLS) las resuelven.

Interpretar un coeficiente logístico como una razón de momios, y corregir sus lecturas erróneas comunes.

Probar coeficientes con la prueba de Wald y la prueba de razón de verosimilitud, y explicar cuándo discrepan.

Calcular la devianza y usarla para comparar modelos anidados.

Evaluar un modelo ajustado con una tabla de clasificación y una curva ROC, y diagnosticar problemas de datos antes de confiar en cualquiera de las dos.

records.content:
Empecemos con lo que sale mal. Supón que codificas la respuesta como Y = 1 para un resultado
positivo (una junta tórica dañada, una prueba de diabetes positiva) y Y = 0 en caso contrario, y
ajustas la recta ordinaria Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X + \varepsilon. Tres cosas se rompen.

Primero, la recta ajustada no tiene cota. Una recta sigue subiendo a medida que X crece, así que
con el tiempo predice probabilidades por encima de 1 y, en el otro sentido, por debajo de 0.


Figure 2 muestra la recta de mínimos cuadrados a través de los datos de diabetes
deslizándose directamente fuera del rango [0, 1] donde una probabilidad tiene que vivir.

Segundo, la dispersión no es constante. Una respuesta 0/1 tiene varianza \pi(1 - \pi), donde
\pi es la probabilidad de que Y = 1. Esa varianza es mayor cerca de \pi = 0.5 y se encoge
hacia cero cuando \pi se acerca a 0 o a 1, la parábola invertida de 

Figure 3. Una
moneda cerca de 50-50 es la más difícil de predecir, así que su resultado es el más variable; una
moneda que casi siempre cae cara es casi una certeza, así que apenas varía. La dispersión queda
fijada por la media. Así que el supuesto de varianza constante detrás de los mínimos cuadrados
(

2.1 El modelo de regresión lineal simple) es falso por construcción, y la ponderación que lo arreglaría cambia con la
media, una idea que vimos en los mínimos cuadrados ponderados (

10.4 Mínimos cuadrados ponderados).

Tercero, los errores no pueden ser normales. Si Y solo es alguna vez 0 o 1, entonces para un X
dado el error toma solo dos valores, así que el modelo de error normal que justificó nuestra
inferencia t y F simplemente no aplica.



Figure 2:La recta de mínimos cuadrados (roja discontinua) sale del rango en el que una probabilidad debe permanecer, cayendo por debajo de 0 y subiendo por encima de 1. La curva logística (azul) se dobla para caber dentro de la banda.



Figure 3:La varianza de un resultado de sí o no no es una constante libre: es igual a \pi(1-\pi), con su máximo en \pi = 0.5 y anulándose en ambos extremos. Como la dispersión está soldada a la media, el supuesto de varianza constante de los mínimos cuadrados no puede cumplirse para una respuesta binaria.

La solución es modelar la probabilidad \pi directamente y doblar la recta para que nunca pueda
salir de [0, 1]. La curva es la curva logística en forma de S de la figura.

hierarchy.lvl4: De la probabilidad a los momios al logaritmo de los momios
hierarchy.lvl3: 13.1 Por qué una recta falla, y qué usar en su lugar

hierarchy.lvl4: De la probabilidad a los momios al logaritmo de los momios
hierarchy.lvl3: 13.1 Por qué una recta falla, y qué usar en su lugar

records.content:
El puente son los momios (Definición 13.1).

Definición 13.1: Momios

Los momios (odds) de un evento con probabilidad \pi son \text{odds} = \pi/(1 - \pi), la
probabilidad de que el evento ocurra dividida por la probabilidad de que no ocurra. Los momios van
de 0 (el evento nunca ocurre) a \infty (es seguro).

Una probabilidad de 0.5 son momios de 1 (dinero parejo). Una probabilidad de 0.75 son momios de
3 (tres a uno). A medida que \pi sube hacia 1 los momios se disparan al infinito, y a medida que
\pi baja hacia 0 los momios caen hacia 0 pero nunca se vuelven negativos. 

Figure 4
dibuja este mapeo. Los momios estiran la escala acotada de probabilidad [0, 1] sobre la semirrecta
[0, \infty).



Figure 4:Los momios como función de la probabilidad. Pasos iguales en probabilidad no son pasos iguales en momios: de 0.5 a 0.75 los momios se triplican, pero de 0.75 a 0.9 se triplican de nuevo, así que la escala de momios se expande a medida que te acercas a la certeza.

Dando un paso más, el logaritmo de los momios o logit (Definición 13.2) es el logaritmo
natural de los momios:\operatorname{logit}(\pi) = \log\!\left(\frac{\pi}{1 - \pi}\right) .

Definición 13.2: Logaritmo de los momios (logit)

El logaritmo de los momios (log-odds) o logit de una probabilidad \pi es
\operatorname{logit}(\pi) = \log[\pi/(1 - \pi)], el logaritmo natural de los momios. Mapea el
rango acotado (0, 1) sobre toda la recta real (-\infty, \infty).

En palabras: el logit es el logaritmo de los momios. El logaritmo envía [0, \infty) sobre toda la
recta real (-\infty, \infty), el mismo rango que puede tomar un predictor lineal \beta_0 +
\beta_1 X. Ese es todo el truco. No podemos igualar una probabilidad a una recta, porque una está
acotada y la otra no, pero sí podemos igualar el logit de la probabilidad a una recta.

Idea clave

Una probabilidad vive en [0, 1] y una recta no, así que las dos nunca pueden ser iguales. El
logit quita las paredes: estira la escala acotada de probabilidad sobre toda la recta real, y allí
un modelo lineal puede alcanzarla. Todo modelo lineal generalizado funciona de esta misma manera,
transformando la media hasta que una recta pueda ajustarla.

13.1 Por qué una recta falla, y qué usar en su lugar

records.content:
El modelo de regresión logística (Definición 13.3) para una respuesta binaria dice que el
logaritmo de los momios es lineal en los predictores:\operatorname{logit}(\pi_i) = \log\!\left(\frac{\pi_i}{1 - \pi_i}\right)
= \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_{i1} + \dots + \beta_{p-1} X_{i,p-1} ,

donde \pi_i = P(Y_i = 1 \mid X_i) es la probabilidad de que el caso i tenga un resultado
positivo, las \beta son los parámetros de regresión (con p de ellos contando el intercepto), y
X_{i1}, \dots, X_{i,p-1} son los predictores del caso i. Escribiendo \eta_i = \beta_0 +
\beta_1 X_{i1} + \dots para el predictor lineal, resolvemos la ecuación del logit para \pi_i
y obtenemos el modelo en la escala de probabilidad:\pi_i = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\eta_i}} = \frac{e^{\eta_i}}{1 + e^{\eta_i}} .

Definición 13.3: Modelo de regresión logística

El modelo de regresión logística para una respuesta binaria Y_i con probabilidad de éxito
\pi_i = P(Y_i = 1 \mid X_i) hace del logaritmo de los momios una función lineal de los
predictores, \operatorname{logit}(\pi_i) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_{i1} + \dots + \beta_{p-1}
X_{i,p-1} = \eta_i, equivalentemente \pi_i = 1/(1 + e^{-\eta_i}). La cantidad \eta_i es el
predictor lineal y el logit es la función de enlace.

En palabras: la probabilidad es el predictor lineal pasado a través de la función logística
1/(1 + e^{-\eta}), la curva en S de 

Figure 5. La función está comprimida para quedar
estrictamente entre 0 y 1, así que sin importar cuáles sean los coeficientes, la probabilidad
predicha es siempre una probabilidad válida.



Figure 5:Izquierda: la función logística convierte cualquier predictor lineal en una probabilidad entre 0 y 1. Derecha: en la escala del logaritmo de los momios la misma relación es una línea recta, y por eso la regresión logística es un modelo lineal disfrazado.

Los dos vínculos en el término modelo lineal generalizado son visibles aquí. Hay una parte
aleatoria, Y_i siendo 0 o 1 con probabilidad \pi_i, y una parte sistemática, el predictor
lineal \eta_i, unidas por la función de enlace logit que mapea la media sobre la escala
lineal. La regresión ordinaria es la misma imagen con el enlace identidad y una respuesta normal;
el Capítulo 14 hace explícita la familia y añade el miembro de Poisson (

14.6 Una familia: el modelo lineal generalizado).
